name: Journal of Emerging Investigators (JEI)
ageNote: Grades 6-12, age 13+ — explicitly welcomes middle schoolers; THE legit MS science option.
prerequisites: A hypothesis-driven experiment written to the JEI template (life + experimental physical sciences).
costNote: $49 submission fee (the only fee); need-based waivers available.
cadence: Rolling, year-round (review often 6-8 months).
howToStart: Run a hypothesis-driven experiment, write to the template, submit via Editorial Manager (or request a waiver).
a2cInsight: The legit, near-free, genuinely peer-reviewed science journal — meaningful precisely because review is real. NOT pay-to-publish.

name: Frontiers for Young Minds (Young Reviewer)
ageNote: Ages 8-15 peer-REVIEW real scientific manuscripts (a Science Mentor pairs with them).
prerequisites: Curiosity; the published review is signed — real reviewer credit on a real journal.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Rolling.
howToStart: Sign up as a Young Reviewer at kids.frontiersin.org/participate/young-minds.
a2cInsight: Uniquely accessible AND genuinely prestigious for the 8-15 band — a signed reviewer credit on a real journal.

name: The Concord Review
ageNote: Grades 9-12; the word-count/scholarship bar excludes most middle schoolers.
prerequisites: An original, heavily-endnoted ~5,000-8,000+ word history research essay (Chicago/Turabian).
costNote: $70 author-membership/submission fee per essay.
cadence: Quarterly (Aug 1 / Nov 1 / Feb 1 / May 1); ~5% accepted.
howToStart: Read past issues to calibrate, write a Chicago-endnoted history essay, submit at tcr.org/submit.
a2cInsight: The gold standard for HS humanities — the rare journal admissions officers actually respect. Top essays win the Emerson Prize.

name: The Adroit Journal (Adroit Prizes)
ageNote: Adroit Prizes for secondary + undergrad (~ages 13-19); the teen on-ramp.
prerequisites: Polished original poetry, fiction, or creative nonfiction with a distinctive voice.
costNote: General submissions free/tip-based; Adroit Prizes $15 (waivers).
cadence: Adroit Prizes ~Apr-mid May annually; ~1-2% general acceptance.
howToStart: Enter the Adroit Prizes via Submittable in spring; use the fee-waiver if needed.
a2cInsight: A gold-standard teen literary credential alongside Scholastic; a win/finalist is a true needle-mover.

name: Blue Marble Review
ageNote: Ages 13-22 — accepts middle schoolers.
prerequisites: Original writing or art; curated, ~22% acceptance.
costNote: Free — and PAYS contributors $30/piece (a legitimacy signal).
cadence: Rolling.
howToStart: Submit via the on-site form; use a personal email so acceptance replies aren't filtered.
a2cInsight: A clean early/first byline; strong entry point because it accepts younger writers and pays.

name: Literature review (self-directed)
ageNote: The single most accessible genuine FIRST paper — no lab or data collection.
prerequisites: A narrow question + 15-30 peer-reviewed sources; synthesis (surface debates/gaps), not summary.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Self-paced.
howToStart: Pick a narrow question, read 15-30 papers, synthesize; submit to a legit student journal or post on Zenodo.
a2cInsight: T3 self-posted, T2 in a legit student journal — the most accessible legitimate route to a real first paper.

name: GitHub project write-up / technical report
ageNote: In CS/ML, arguably the most respected self-directed output — reviewers can run it.
prerequisites: A working project; write the README AS a paper (problem -> method -> data -> results -> reproduce).
costNote: Free.
cadence: Self-paced.
howToStart: Build it, document it as a paper, tag a release + Zenodo DOI; credibility = reproducibility.
a2cInsight: High-functioning T3 — for a builder-track CS student, a reproducible repo beats a pay-to-publish journal.

name: Kaggle Competitions (Grandmaster path)
ageNote: 13+ to participate (minors need verifiable parental consent); medals/rank NOT prize-gated.
prerequisites: Python + pandas/scikit-learn; for top results: gradient boosting, PyTorch, rigorous cross-validation.
costNote: Free (free GPU/TPU notebooks); cash prizes generally require 18+.
cadence: Featured comps have deadlines; Getting Started comps never close.
howToStart: Account (parental consent if under 18) -> Titanic -> fork a starter notebook -> monthly Playground Series.
a2cInsight: Genuinely prestigious AND attainable by a consenting 13+ teen. Trust your own cross-validation, not the public leaderboard.

name: AMC 8
ageNote: Grade 8 and below; the entry rung of the AMC ladder.
prerequisites: Middle-school math + problem-solving practice (AoPS/Alcumus).
costNote: Small per-student fee, usually paid by the host school.
cadence: Annually in January; register through a school or a registered site in the fall.
howToStart: Find a hosting school/center; practice past papers; the first rung of AMC 8 -> AMC 10 -> AIME.
a2cInsight: A great first win and the on-ramp to the most recognized math ladder. Participation is T3; advancement is the goal.

name: MATHCOUNTS Competition Series
ageNote: Grades 6-8 — the flagship middle-school math competition.
prerequisites: A school team/coach; sustained problem-solving practice.
costNote: Registration fee, typically school-paid.
cadence: School -> Chapter -> State -> National (Feb-May); register in the FALL.
howToStart: Join or start a school MATHCOUNTS team in the fall; practice with past rounds.
a2cInsight: The premier MS math signal; advancing to State/Nationals is a genuine T1-leaning distinction.

name: AMC 10
ageNote: Grade 10 and below — strong middle schoolers take it early.
prerequisites: Algebra + geometry fluency; AMC 8 background helps.
costNote: Per-student fee, often school-paid.
cadence: November; register in the fall. Qualifies for AIME.
howToStart: Register through a school/site in the fall; a high score qualifies you for the AIME.
a2cInsight: The gateway to AIME -> USA(J)MO. A qualifying score is a real differentiator.

name: AIME (American Invitational Mathematics Examination)
ageNote: Invitation-only via AMC 10/12; exceptional middle schoolers reach it.
prerequisites: Qualify via AMC 10/12.
costNote: Free for qualifiers.
cadence: Feb/March, by invitation.
howToStart: Qualify through the AMC 10/12; the rung below USA(J)MO.
a2cInsight: A national-flagship math signal; reaching AIME is a recognized scarcity marker.

name: 3M Young Scientist Challenge
ageNote: Grades 5-8 — a premier middle-school science competition.
prerequisites: A 1-2 minute video pitching a solution to an everyday problem.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Entries spring; finalists summer; finals fall.
howToStart: Submit a short video describing an innovation; 10 finalists get a summer mentorship.
a2cInsight: A genuine T1 MS science signal with a mentorship prize; one of the best early science showcases.

name: Thermo Fisher Junior Innovators Challenge (JIC)
ageNote: Grades 6-8 — formerly Broadcom MASTERS; the top MS science-fair capstone.
prerequisites: Nomination from a partner regional/state science fair (top ~10%).
costNote: Free; reached via a feeder science fair.
cadence: Nominations summer/fall after spring fairs.
howToStart: Do a science-fair project, place at an affiliated regional fair, get nominated.
a2cInsight: The premier middle-school research distinction and the rung below ISEF on the science ladder.

name: Scholastic Art & Writing Awards
ageNote: Grades 7-12 — the most prestigious creative-teen program; MS welcome.
prerequisites: Original art or writing in one of 28+ categories.
costNote: Per-submission fee with fee waivers available.
cadence: Regional submissions ~Sept-Dec; National Medals in spring.
howToStart: Submit work in the fall through the regional affiliate; a Gold Key climbs toward a National Medal.
a2cInsight: Filed as a competition; the National Medal is a T1 award. Regional Gold Key -> National Medal is the creative ladder.

name: USACO (USA Computing Olympiad)
ageNote: No age floor; strong MS coders start at Bronze.
prerequisites: Programming (C++/Java/Python) + algorithms; self-study friendly.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Four contest weekends Dec-Feb; promotion within a contest.
howToStart: Make an account, start Bronze with the USACO Guide; climb Bronze -> Silver -> Gold -> Platinum.
a2cInsight: Bronze is Intro difficulty but the USACO brand is T1 — a dated, ungameable algorithm-track ladder.

name: picoCTF (Carnegie Mellon)
ageNote: Beginner-friendly capture-the-flag; great MS cybersecurity on-ramp.
prerequisites: Curiosity; basic CS helps. Challenges teach as you go.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Annual competition (spring) + year-round practice gym.
howToStart: Make an account and work the picoGym; enter the annual picoCTF event.
a2cInsight: A friendly first rung on the security track (Bebras/picoCTF -> USACO).

name: Congressional App Challenge (CAC)
ageNote: Middle + high school; one winner PER congressional district -> tiny pool.
prerequisites: An original app in any language; solo or small team.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Register summer; submit by early November.
howToStart: Build an app, record a demo video, submit before the fall deadline.
a2cInsight: Intro/T3 but reads above its weight — district-level and Congress-backed. A great builder-track first win.

name: Verizon Innovative Learning App Challenge
ageNote: Middle + high school teams; concept-stage (no code required).
prerequisites: A team + a teacher sponsor; an app concept addressing a community need.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Fall submission window.
howToStart: Form a team with a teacher sponsor, design an app concept, submit in the fall.
a2cInsight: The gentlest builder-track on-ramp (Verizon -> Congressional App Challenge -> hackathons -> a deployed app).

name: Science Olympiad (Division B)
ageNote: Division B = grades 6-9; team-based across 20+ events.
prerequisites: A school team; preparation in chosen events.
costNote: Team registration; school-paid.
cadence: Regional -> State -> National (winter-spring); join the team in the fall.
howToStart: Join your school's Science Olympiad team and pick events that fit your interests.
a2cInsight: A strong, broad MS STEM team experience; State/National medals are real distinctions.

name: FIRST LEGO League (FLL)
ageNote: Ages 9-16 — the MS entry to the FIRST robotics ladder (FLL -> FTC -> FRC).
prerequisites: A team + coach; build/program a LEGO robot + a research project.
costNote: Team + kit costs; grants often available.
cadence: Season runs fall-winter; register in late summer/fall.
howToStart: Join or start an FLL team in the fall; compete in the regional tournament.
a2cInsight: The foundational rung of the FIRST robotics ladder; sustained involvement builds toward FTC/FRC leadership.

name: Technovation Girls
ageNote: Ages 8-18 girls/non-binary; build an app + a business plan for a community problem.
prerequisites: A team + mentor; an app idea solving a real-world problem.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Season ~fall-spring.
howToStart: Form a team with a mentor, pick a community problem, build and pitch the app.
a2cInsight: A genuine intersection of CS + entrepreneurship + impact; a regional/global placement is a strong T2 signal.

name: National History Day (NHD)
ageNote: Junior division = grades 6-8; the on-ramp to the history-writing ladder.
prerequisites: A researched paper, exhibit, documentary, performance, or website on an annual theme.
costNote: Small registration; often school-paid.
cadence: School/regional -> state -> national (winter-June).
howToStart: Pick a topic on the year's theme, research it deeply, enter at the school/regional level.
a2cInsight: NHD Junior -> World Historian Essay -> The Concord Review is the history-writing ladder.

name: Math Kangaroo USA
ageNote: Grades 1-12; a low-stakes, fun problem-solving contest.
prerequisites: None beyond grade-level math curiosity.
costNote: ~$21 registration; register in the fall/early winter.
cadence: Annually in March; register by mid-December.
howToStart: Register by December at mathkangaroo.org; great early habit-builder.
a2cInsight: A confidence-building early contest; momentum, not a resume centerpiece.

name: eCYBERMISSION (US Army / AEOP)
ageNote: Grades 6-9; team STEM project solving a community problem.
prerequisites: A team of 3-4 + a Team Advisor.
costNote: Free; cash awards as savings bonds.
cadence: Register fall; submit late winter.
howToStart: Form a team with an advisor, identify a local problem, run the project, submit.
a2cInsight: A free, accessible MS team-science on-ramp with real community framing.

name: Bow Seat Ocean Awareness Contest
ageNote: Ages 11-18 — art/writing/film on ocean & climate themes.
prerequisites: A creative piece (visual art, poetry, prose, film, music) on the annual theme.
costNote: Free; scholarships for winners.
cadence: Submissions winter-June.
howToStart: Make a creative piece on the year's ocean-awareness theme and submit online.
a2cInsight: A clean intersection of art/writing + environment for the creatively-inclined kid.

name: Regeneron ISEF (International Science & Engineering Fair)
ageNote: Grades 9-12; the world's top pre-college science fair (the north star).
prerequisites: Win/place at an affiliated regional or state fair to qualify.
costNote: Free; reached via a feeder fair.
cadence: Affiliated fairs winter-spring; ISEF in May.
howToStart: Do original research, place at an affiliated fair, advance to ISEF.
a2cInsight: An ISEF finalist is among the highest-signal Tier-1 STEM outcomes. The science-research north star.

name: Regeneron Science Talent Search (STS)
ageNote: Seniors only — the summit of the US science-research ladder.
prerequisites: An original, sustained research project + full application as a HS senior.
costNote: Free to apply.
cadence: Application in fall of senior year.
howToStart: Build a multi-year research project; apply senior fall. (Start the arc now.)
a2cInsight: The most prestigious US pre-college science award; the multi-year arc that early starters can uniquely build.

name: Davidson Young Scholars (DYS)
ageNote: Ages 5-18 (apply by ~16); free support for profoundly gifted students.
prerequisites: Evidence of profound giftedness (qualifying test scores / portfolio).
costNote: Free.
cadence: Rolling applications.
howToStart: Apply with qualifying evidence at davidsongifted.org/young-scholars.
a2cInsight: A free, selective recognition + support network for highly gifted young students.

name: Johns Hopkins CTY — Talent Search
ageNote: Grades 2-8 take above-grade tests for recognition + course eligibility.
prerequisites: An above-grade-level qualifying test (e.g., SCAT).
costNote: Test + program fees; financial aid available.
cadence: Rolling testing windows.
howToStart: Register for the qualifying test; high scores earn recognition + CTY course access.
a2cInsight: A recognized early talent-search credential and a gateway to acceleration.

name: National Latin Exam (NLE)
ageNote: Any Latin student; a stackable, low-stakes language honor.
prerequisites: Enrollment in (or self-study of) Latin at the exam level.
costNote: Small per-student fee, school-administered.
cadence: Annually in spring; register through a school in winter.
howToStart: Take Latin (or self-study), register through a school, sit the exam for a medal/certificate.
a2cInsight: A clean, repeatable recognition that compounds year over year for a languages-classics student.

name: Doodle for Google
ageNote: Grades K-12; a national art contest on an annual theme.
prerequisites: An original doodle on the year's theme + a short artist statement.
costNote: Free.
cadence: Annual entry window (winter-spring).
howToStart: Create a themed doodle and submit with a parent/guardian sign-off.
a2cInsight: A fun, accessible national art recognition; a confidence-builder for young visual artists.

name: National Junior Honor Society (NJHS)
ageNote: Grades 6-9; school-chapter membership on scholarship + service + leadership.
prerequisites: GPA threshold + demonstrated service/leadership/character.
costNote: Small chapter dues.
cadence: School-chapter selection cycles.
howToStart: Meet your school chapter's criteria and apply when invited.
a2cInsight: Membership alone is T3 filler; pursue a leadership role to make it count.

name: The Congressional Award
ageNote: Ages 13.5-24; self-set goals across service, fitness, and exploration.
prerequisites: Register and log progress toward self-set goals (Bronze -> Gold).
costNote: Free.
cadence: Rolling; self-paced over months/years.
howToStart: Register, set goals, and log hours toward the Bronze medal first.
a2cInsight: A stackable official recognition; pair it with a sustained, measurable project (the spike itself).

name: Conrad Challenge
ageNote: Ages 13-18 teams; innovation + entrepreneurship to solve global problems.
prerequisites: A team + coach; a product/venture concept with a business plan.
costNote: Free to enter early rounds; finals travel costs vary.
cadence: Season fall-spring.
howToStart: Form a team, pick a challenge category, build the concept + plan, submit each round.
a2cInsight: A strong STEM + entrepreneurship intersection for team-oriented builders.

name: Zenodo (DOI repository)
ageNote: Any age can upload; useful with a real dataset/codebase/poster.
prerequisites: A report, dataset, poster, or code release worth archiving.
costNote: Free (up to 50 GB/record).
cadence: Rolling.
howToStart: Upload + metadata + license; for code, link a GitHub Release to auto-mint a DOI.
a2cInsight: The standard 'give my code/data a DOI' move. A DOI makes work citable — it is NOT peer review or prestige.

name: Curieux Academic Journal
ageNote: Middle + high school; accepts middle schoolers.
prerequisites: A manuscript; markets a two-editor review but high acceptance (~60-70% est.).
costNote: Free to submit; on acceptance ~$185-250 (fast-track upsell).
cadence: Rolling/seasonal.
howToStart: Fine only as an early publishing EXPERIENCE for a younger student — never present it as a credential.
a2cInsight: Textbook pay-to-publish / accept-almost-everyone; the fast-track upsell is exactly what ProPublica flagged. Adds little admissions value.

name: International Journal of High School Research (IJHSR)
ageNote: HS (global); publisher is Terra Science. Do NOT confuse with the unrelated ijhsr.org.
prerequisites: A science manuscript; controversially requires the student to SUPPLY three reviewers.
costNote: No submission fee; on acceptance ~$200-300.
cadence: Rolling.
howToStart: Treat skeptically — never present as a credential.
a2cInsight: Named by ProPublica; effectively pay-to-publish, and author-supplied reviewers offload peer review onto the student's contacts.

name: Journal of Student Research (JSR) — HS Edition
ageNote: HS edition; widely marketed to teens.
prerequisites: A manuscript; pay-to-publish pattern with a fast-track upsell.
costNote: Fee on acceptance; expedited review upsell.
cadence: Rolling.
howToStart: Avoid as a credential; a legit student journal or a preprint is a better route.
a2cInsight: Named by ProPublica — fee-on-acceptance + fast-track upsell. The confusingly-similar name is itself a red flag.

name: Questioz — International Journal for HS Research
ageNote: HS with an advisor (required).
prerequisites: A research manuscript + advisor; student-run review (~4 wk).
costNote: Free (no fee).
cadence: Rolling; ~75-80% acceptance.
howToStart: Submit at questioz.org; useful as experience, not as a distinction.
a2cInsight: Legit-ish and free, but high acceptance reads more as participation than achievement.

name: President's Volunteer Service Award (PVSA)
ageNote: Any age; log service hours for an automatic federal medal.
prerequisites: Logged volunteer hours through a certifying organization.
costNote: Small certification fee via the certifying org.
cadence: Annual cycles — PAUSED as of 2026.
howToStart: Verify relaunch before recommending; historically log hours via a certifying organization.
a2cInsight: PVSA alone is Tier-4 filler — a supporting line, not the spike. Paused as of 2026.

name: Junior Science & Humanities Symposium (JSHS)
ageNote: HS research presentation program; DoD-sponsored.
prerequisites: An original research project to present.
costNote: Free.
cadence: On hiatus — confirm relaunch before recommending.
howToStart: Verify status before recommending; historically present research at the regional symposium.
a2cInsight: Historically a respected research-presentation venue; suspended as of 2026 — watch for relaunch.

name: Duke TIP (Talent Identification Program)
ageNote: Former gifted talent search.
prerequisites: n/a — program ended.
costNote: n/a.
cadence: Discontinued.
howToStart: Do not pursue — Duke TIP has permanently ended; consider CTY or Belin-Blank instead.
a2cInsight: Listed only so families don't chase a dead program. Redirect to active talent searches.

name: National Geographic GeoBee
ageNote: Former grades 4-8 geography bee.
prerequisites: n/a — discontinued.
costNote: n/a.
cadence: Discontinued (2020).
howToStart: Do not pursue — discontinued; consider US Geography Championships / IAC Geography Olympiad instead.
a2cInsight: Listed only to steer families off a dead program. Redirect to active geography contests.

name: Math League (mathleague.com)
prerequisites: Register through your school; open to grades 4-12 depending on the specific contest track.
costNote: School registration fees are modest (varies by contest level); summer online challenge ~$66/grade level.
cadence: Six contests per year (roughly monthly Nov-Apr) plus an annual summer international tournament.
howToStart: Have a teacher/coordinator register the school at mathleague.com; students take monthly in-school contests.
a2cInsight: A solid, long-running practice-and-recognition circuit; useful for building a consistent math resume line but not a standout credential on its own.

name: mathleague.org State/National/International Championships
prerequisites: Free school membership registration; open to elementary, middle, and high school divisions.
costNote: School membership is free; optional past-contest materials sold separately.
cadence: In-season qualifying contests, State Championships in April, US National in May, International in July.
howToStart: Register your school for a free membership at mathleague.org, then join qualifying contests.
a2cInsight: Free and accessible nationwide circuit (distinct org from mathleague.com); good stepping-stone recognition, moderate prestige beyond regional level.

name: Math Olympiads for Elementary and Middle Schools (MOEMS)
prerequisites: Open to Elementary (grades 4-6) and Middle (grades 6-8) divisions via school, homeschool, or organization registration.
costNote: Program/team registration fee (check moems.org store; typically under $100/team).
cadence: Five monthly contests, November through March, each within a ~4-week administration window.
howToStart: Register your school, homeschool group, or organization at moems.org/pages/register-today.
a2cInsight: A widely-used, low-stakes on-ramp into competition math; good for building consistency but limited standalone admissions weight.

name: Noetic Learning Math Contest (NLMC)
prerequisites: Grades 2-8; register as a school, community, or parent-led team.
costNote: $99 per team of up to 30 students.
cadence: Twice yearly, fall and spring; 45-50 minute timed test.
howToStart: A parent, teacher, or coordinator registers a team at noetic-learning.com/mathcontest.
a2cInsight: Friendly, accessible national contest for younger students; a 'Top National Scorer' certificate is nice enrichment but carries little admissions weight.

name: Continental Mathematics League (CML)
prerequisites: School registers a team; open across elementary through high school divisions (grades 2-12).
costNote: Per-student/team pricing set by CML catalog; contact for current rates.
cadence: In-school contest series running through the school year since 1980.
howToStart: Teacher/coordinator orders a CML contest package at cmleague.com.
a2cInsight: A legacy in-classroom contest league — solid enrichment tool, but a minor line on a resume rather than a differentiator.

name: Purple Comet! Math Meet
prerequisites: Team of up to 6 students, middle or high school division; supervised by an adult (21+).
costNote: Free to register and participate online.
cadence: Annual, ~9-day online window in April (2026: April 14-23).
howToStart: An adult supervisor registers at purplecomet.org and forms a team.
a2cInsight: A free, well-regarded international team contest with real problem-writing quality — top placements are a credible signal, especially combined with other results.

name: Mandelbrot Competition
prerequisites: N/A (discontinued); historical problem sets remain available for practice.
costNote: N/A — competition is discontinued; historical archive only.
cadence: Formerly ran 1990-2019 with individual and team rounds during the school year; permanently discontinued in 2019.
howToStart: Not enterable; use archived problem sets (via AoPS Wiki or Mu Alpha Theta references) for practice only, not for a live credential.
a2cInsight: Was historically a well-respected proof-adjacent contest; now defunct, so it can only be cited as practice material, not a current achievement.

name: American Regions Mathematics League (ARML)
prerequisites: Grade 12 or below, 18 or younger by Dec 31 of competition year; must join or form a regional/state team of 15.
costNote: Roughly $65-75 per student plus a team fee (~$400, often waived for first-time teams); full 15-person team runs well over $1,000 total.
cadence: Annual, late May/early June, held simultaneously at multiple US university sites.
howToStart: Contact your local/state ARML team organizer (often through a math league or school) to try out for a spot on a regional team.
a2cInsight: A demanding national team-based olympiad-adjacent contest; making a competitive regional team is a credible achievement, though team dependence limits individual signal.

name: Who Wants to Be a Mathematician (AMS)
prerequisites: Secondary school students (younger students may also participate); qualify via a regional online test.
costNote: Free to enter regional/national qualifying tests.
cadence: Regional qualifying tests during the fall/winter; national championship at the Joint Mathematics Meetings each January.
howToStart: Take the online qualifying test posted at ams.org/publicoutreach/championship each fall.
a2cInsight: A fun, game-show-style AMS event with real cash prizes; a national finalist slot is a nice credential but more niche than USAMO-track results.

name: USA Mathematical Talent Search (USAMTS)
prerequisites: Open to any US middle/high school student; no qualifying test needed to enter.
costNote: Completely free, proof-based, open contest.
cadence: Three rounds per year (roughly Sept-Dec, Dec-Feb, Feb-Apr); students get over a month per round.
howToStart: Register directly at usamts.org and submit written proof solutions each round.
a2cInsight: A rare free, open-entry proof-writing contest that develops genuine olympiad skills and can qualify students for AIME — strong signal for students without AMC access.

name: Harvard-MIT Mathematics Tournament (HMMT)
prerequisites: High school students, typically strong AMC/AIME-level solvers competing in teams of up to 8.
costNote: $80 per team plus $10 per competitor; financial aid available including waived fees and covered lodging.
cadence: Two annual tournaments: Autumn (Harvard, November) and Spring/February (MIT); plus an online tournament.
howToStart: Register a team through hmmt.org during the open registration window (often competitive/lottery-based).
a2cInsight: One of the most prestigious student-run university math tournaments globally; a strong individual or team placement is elite-tier signal, especially at February HMMT.

name: Stanford Math Tournament (SMT)
prerequisites: High school students; strong AMC/AIME-caliber background recommended, individual and team rounds.
costNote: Modest per-team/individual registration fee; some financial aid offered.
cadence: Annual, typically mid-April, both in-person and online formats.
howToStart: Register via stanfordmathtournament.org during the open registration window.
a2cInsight: A top-tier student-run university tournament alongside HMMT/BMT; strong placements read well to selective STEM admissions.

name: Berkeley Math Tournament (BMT) / Berkeley mini Math Tournament (BmMT)
prerequisites: BMT: high school students, olympiad-adjacent difficulty. BmMT: middle school and advanced elementary students (grade 8 and below).
costNote: Modest team/individual registration fee; BmMT (middle school) offered as a lower-tier companion event.
cadence: Annual, main BMT in fall/winter; BmMT for middle schoolers typically in spring.
howToStart: Register a team at berkeley.mt during the open window; BmMT is a good middle-school entry point.
a2cInsight: The largest student-run university math tournament worldwide; competitive results (especially at BMT) are a strong, respected STEM credential.

name: Rocket City Math League (RCML)
prerequisites: Middle and high school students; five divisions from pre-algebra through comprehensive/discovery level.
costNote: Free, sponsored by Mu Alpha Theta.
cadence: Interschool team round in fall, three individual rounds in spring, each year-long season.
howToStart: A school math team coach registers via grissommath.org/rcml or through Mu Alpha Theta.
a2cInsight: A free, well-established student-run league that's easy to access through a school math team; solid but modest resume line rather than a headline credential.

name: MathCON
prerequisites: Grades 4-12; take the online qualifying test, top scorers advance to in-person finals.
costNote: Individual registration ~$75/student for the online round; school registration ~$295 covers up to 1,000 students; finals travel costs (or virtual option) are separate.
cadence: Annual: online qualifying test Jan-March, national finals in May.
howToStart: Register directly at mathcon.org for the online test window.
a2cInsight: A growing national contest with real finals travel and prizes; a finals qualification is a credible mid-tier achievement, less established than MATHCOUNTS/AMC.

name: Online Monmouth Math Competition (OMMC)
prerequisites: Open to any student 18 or under; teams of up to 4 for the main round.
costNote: Free to enter; run by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit.
cadence: Annual; main round (25 questions, 8 days) followed by an invite-only proof-based final round (3 days).
howToStart: Register a team at ommcofficial.org when the annual competition window opens.
a2cInsight: A newer, student-run online contest with genuine proof-round rigor; growing reputation in the AoPS competition community but not yet a widely recognized admissions signal.

name: Advantage Testing Foundation/Jane Street Math Prize for Girls
prerequisites: Female high school students in the US/Canada; selection is based on AMC/AIME-caliber qualifying scores.
costNote: No fee to apply or participate.
cadence: Annual, fall, held at MIT.
howToStart: Apply via mathprize.atfoundation.org during the application window (typically based on prior AMC/AIME results).
a2cInsight: The most prestigious US math competition specifically for young women — an invitation alone (let alone a win) is an elite, highly-recognized credential.

name: Mu Alpha Theta National Convention Competitions
prerequisites: High school (grades 9-12) or two-year college students; must be a member of a Mu Alpha Theta chapter (3.0+ math GPA, algebra/geometry completed).
costNote: Convention registration plus travel/lodging costs (multi-day event, typically several hundred dollars per student).
cadence: Annual national convention, typically late June/early July; local/regional Mu Alpha Theta chapter contests happen throughout the year.
howToStart: Join or start a Mu Alpha Theta chapter at your school, then register for the National Convention via mualphatheta.org.
a2cInsight: A major national gathering with dozens of subject-specific individual and team contests; strong placements are respected, but chapter membership itself is common and low-differentiation.

name: Hardest Math Problem Contest (Actuarial Foundation)
prerequisites: Grades 5-8, US and territories, submitted by an adult (18+) on the student's behalf.
costNote: Free to enter; cash prizes and scholarships awarded.
cadence: Annual, two-challenge structure across the school year (fall/winter submission windows).
howToStart: Register and submit solutions at hardestmathproblem.org during the open window.
a2cInsight: A free, real-world-flavored applied math contest run by the Actuarial Foundation; nice for younger students and hints at actuarial/finance interest, but modest national prestige.

name: Perennial Math Tournaments
prerequisites: Grades 3-8 (online/on-site); WebEx track extends through grade 12.
costNote: Team membership ~$100/season (up to 30 students); individual membership ~$25.
cadence: Two online seasons per year, plus live on-site and WebEx virtual tournament options.
howToStart: A teacher/coordinator registers a team membership at perennialmath.com.
a2cInsight: A flexible, moderately-priced contest circuit good for building a consistent participation record; limited standalone prestige beyond local recognition.

name: North South Foundation Math Bee
prerequisites: Grades 1-8, organized in three levels (MB1: 1-3, MB2: 4-5, MB3: 6-8).
costNote: Modest regional registration fee; scholarships awarded to top national finishers.
cadence: Regional contests through the year, culminating in an annual national finals.
howToStart: Register for a regional contest via northsouth.org/regional-contests.
a2cInsight: A well-established community-run bee (originally serving Indian-American students, now broadly open) that doubles as SAT/standardized-test prep; solid local credential, modest national prestige.

name: Karl Menger Memorial Awards in Mathematics (AMS, at Regeneron ISEF)
prerequisites: Must first qualify for Regeneron ISEF with a math (or math-oriented CS/physics/engineering) project via a local/state/national-affiliated fair.
costNote: No entry fee; awarded automatically to qualifying ISEF projects, includes cash prizes.
cadence: Annual, awarded each May at Regeneron ISEF.
howToStart: Enter a research project at an ISEF-affiliated local/regional science fair; math and math-adjacent projects are automatically eligible for Menger judging at ISEF.
a2cInsight: A special AMS-sponsored award layered on top of ISEF for math research specifically — a strong signal of both math research ability and ISEF-level rigor.

name: Mu Alpha Theta Membership (National Mathematics Honor Society)
prerequisites: High school students with 2+ years of college-prep math completed (through algebra/geometry) and a 3.0+ math GPA, at a chartered Mu Alpha Theta school.
costNote: One-time chapter membership fee, typically around $10 (reduced to $5 for Title I schools).
cadence: Rolling; students are inducted once eligibility criteria are met, usually announced annually by the chapter.
howToStart: Ask your school's math department if it has a chartered Mu Alpha Theta chapter, or petition to start one via mualphatheta.org/how-to-join.
a2cInsight: A widely-held honor society membership — nice to list but very common; admissions officers treat it as a baseline indicator of math coursework, not a differentiator.

name: PUMaC (Princeton University Mathematics Competition)
prerequisites: Competitive proof and computational math background; teams of up to 8 students with an adult coach, or individual power-round-only entry.
costNote: Team fee (~$100-200/team of 8) plus travel to Princeton, NJ; online-only participation option reduces cost.
cadence: Annual, held in late November on Princeton's campus.
howToStart: Register a school/regional team via pumac.princeton.edu when fall registration opens; power round packet released about a week before the event.
a2cInsight: A well-known, selective proving ground for serious math students - strong results add credibility to a STEM application but carry slightly less weight than HMMT/Putnam-adjacent showings.

name: CMIMC (Carnegie Mellon Informatics and Mathematics Competition)
ageNote: The legacy cmimc.org domain is now an inactive historical archive; the current, active site is cmimc.math.cmu.edu.
prerequisites: Solid competition math or CS/algorithms background; teams of 4-6 with an adult coach, individuals also welcome.
costNote: Modest per-team registration fee; travel costs for the in-person Math contest at CMU, programming contest is online.
cadence: Annual - Math contest held in-person at CMU in spring (e.g., late March); Programming contest held online a few weeks later.
howToStart: Register a team through cmimc.math.cmu.edu when registration opens (typically winter); review past problem archives to prepare.
a2cInsight: A solid, growing student-run tournament that signals genuine quantitative interest, especially valuable for students bridging math and CS.

name: Caltech Math Meet / CHMMC (Caltech Harvey Mudd Math Competition)
ageNote: Rebranded from CHMMC (a Caltech/Harvey Mudd collaboration) to Caltech Math Meet (CMM), organized solely by Caltech students since 2023; both names refer to the same continuously active program.
prerequisites: Strong olympiad/AIME-level problem solving; team-based competition organized by Caltech (formerly Caltech and Harvey Mudd) undergrads.
costNote: Low/free registration; travel costs to Caltech's Pasadena campus for in-person teams.
cadence: Annual, held on Caltech's campus (recently rebranded from CHMMC to Caltech Math Meet / CMM).
howToStart: Register via caltechmathmeet.org when the fall/winter announcement goes out.
a2cInsight: Smaller than HMMT/PUMaC but respected among serious competitors for its difficulty - the recent CHMMC-to-CMM rebrand can confuse past-result verification, so keep documentation.

name: MMATHS (Math Majors of America Tournament for High Schools, Yale)
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; team-based format designed to be geographically/mathematically accessible.
costNote: Modest per-team registration fee plus travel to Yale's New Haven campus.
cadence: Annual, typically held in fall or early spring at Yale.
howToStart: Register a team via mmaths.org during the registration window.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded regional-to-national tournament that also spawns satellite MMATHS-branded events at other schools, giving it broader reach than its single-campus size suggests.

name: Girls in Math at Yale (GiMaY)
prerequisites: Open to female-identifying, non-binary, and other underrepresented-gender-minority high-school students interested in math; no olympiad-level prerequisite.
costNote: Low/modest registration fee plus travel to Yale.
cadence: Annual, run alongside MMATHS programming.
howToStart: Register via mmaths.org/girls-in-math when the event opens.
a2cInsight: A worthwhile access-and-community credential for young women and gender minorities in math, though it carries less standalone competitive prestige than the main MMATHS tournament.

name: Physics Cup (TalTech)
prerequisites: Open worldwide to high school and university students; no team required, individual entry via Google Form.
costNote: Free to enter; run by Tallinn University of Technology (TalTech) with prizes (thermoses, T-shirts, certificates) and admissions/scholarship perks for top scorers.
cadence: Annual, starts mid-October; five very hard problems released with weekly hints, individual online.
howToStart: Register via physicscup.ee ahead of the October start date; problems are IPhO-and-beyond difficulty, authored/curated by Jaan Kalda (renowned olympiad trainer).
a2cInsight: A cult-favorite among serious olympiad physicists for its brutally hard, creative problems; a strong showing signals genuine elite problem-solving ability to physics-savvy admissions readers, though it's less broadly known than IPhO. Realistically out of reach before advanced olympiad training, so it reads best as an 11th/12th-grade credential.

name: Canadian Association of Physicists (CAP) High School Prize Exam
prerequisites: Upper-year Canadian high school physics student; exam covers IB Physics Higher-level-equivalent content.
costNote: Free or low-cost to sit through participating schools; national/provincial prizes awarded.
cadence: Annual, typically administered in April in person at Canadian high schools.
howToStart: Ask your Canadian physics teacher to register for the CAP High School Prize Exam.
a2cInsight: Top scorers feed directly into Canada's IPhO training camp and national team — a legitimate, well-regarded Canadian pathway credential.

name: Indian National Physics Olympiad pathway — NSEP / INPhO (IAPT)
prerequisites: Indian student (typically class 11-12) registered through a school/exam center recognized by IAPT.
costNote: Modest registration fee for NSEP through school/center; subsequent stages funded by IAPT/HBCSE.
cadence: NSEP held last Sunday of November annually; INPhO and orientation camp follow in the new year, feeding the IPhO team.
howToStart: Register for the National Standard Examination in Physics (NSEP) via IAPT (iapt.org.in); top ~400 advance to INPhO.
a2cInsight: The definitive entry gate to India's IPhO/APhO pipeline; NSEP alone (200,000+ takers) is a decent credential, but INPhO qualification and beyond is what genuinely impresses.

name: Australian Science Olympiads — Physics (ASOP)
prerequisites: Australian senior high school student; enter via school registration with Australian Science Innovations.
costNote: Modest exam fee through school; top performers' summer school/camp costs largely subsidized.
cadence: Annual; qualifying exam typically in July/August, ASO Summer School (residential camp) in January at ANU.
howToStart: Register through your school for the Physics Olympiad exam via asi.edu.au.
a2cInsight: Australia's official IPhO/APhO feeder program; being selected for ASO Summer School (top 24 nationally) is a genuinely elite, well-recognized Australian STEM credential.

name: Japan Physics Olympiad / Physics Challenge (JPhO)
prerequisites: Japanese secondary school student (or resident student); register through JPhO's official contest process.
costNote: Low registration fee for the national "Physics Challenge" contest.
cadence: Annual national "Physics Challenge" contest, feeding selection for IPhO and APhO Japanese delegations.
howToStart: Register for Physics Challenge via the official JPhO website (jpho.jp).
a2cInsight: Japan's official national gateway to IPhO/APhO team selection; strong domestic prestige though not well known internationally outside Japan.

name: Singapore Junior Physics Olympiad (SJPO)
prerequisites: Secondary school student (commonly Secondary 2-3 / roughly grade 8-9); entry only via school nomination, not individual application.
costNote: No individual entry fee; schools are invited to nominate students by the organizing committee (Institute of Physics, Singapore).
cadence: Annual, typically held in May.
howToStart: Ask your Singapore (or eligible international) school to nominate students through the Institute of Physics Singapore.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded early-track credential in Singapore's physics olympiad ecosystem — a stepping stone toward the Singapore Physics Olympiad and IPhO team, useful proof of early aptitude for MS/early-HS students.

name: British Astronomy and Astrophysics Olympiad (BAAO)
prerequisites: UK (or international) secondary student, primarily Year 12-13, though ambitious GCSE-level (Year 10-11) students may attempt it; syllabus-independent.
costNote: Low per-student entry fee via school registration, run within the BPhO Trust.
cadence: Annual, typically administered in the autumn/winter term via schools.
howToStart: Register through your school via the BPhO Trust's BAAO page (bpho.org.uk/baao).
a2cInsight: The direct UK gateway into IOAA team selection; a natural complement to BPhO for students leaning toward astrophysics, well-regarded but narrower in scope than the main BPhO.

name: International Olympiad on Astronomy and Astrophysics (IOAA)
prerequisites: Selected onto a national team (up to 5 students) via a national astronomy/astrophysics or physics olympiad pipeline; secondary school student.
costNote: Entry via national team selection; national federations/host typically cover travel and lodging for the international event.
cadence: Annual international event (roughly September-October), preceded by national selection processes (e.g., BAAO in the UK).
howToStart: Pursue your national astronomy/physics olympiad pathway (e.g., BAAO in UK, USAAAO in US) toward IOAA team selection.
a2cInsight: The astrophysics sibling of IPhO — an elite, internationally recognized credential for students combining physics with astronomy passion; medals are rare and highly respected.

name: IOAA-Jr (International Olympiad on Astronomy and Astrophysics for Juniors)
prerequisites: Junior secondary student (typically under 16) selected onto a small national team via a national junior astronomy program.
costNote: Entry via national junior team selection; host and national coordinators typically cover event costs.
cadence: Annual international junior event (varies by host country each year; the 5th edition runs Nov 2026 in Thailand).
howToStart: Look for a national junior astronomy olympiad or astronomy club affiliated with IOAA-Jr in your country to begin the selection pathway.
a2cInsight: A genuinely middle-school-accessible international astronomy credential — good early proof of STEM passion, though still developing in global reach/prestige compared to full IOAA.

name: FYKOS (Physics Correspondence Seminar)
prerequisites: Any secondary (or younger, motivated) student worldwide; individual entry, problems solved and mailed/submitted online at own pace.
costNote: Free to participate; run by Charles University physics students in Prague.
cadence: Year-long correspondence seminar with multiple problem series released across the school year; culminates in a residential camp for top solvers.
howToStart: Register at fykos.org and begin submitting solutions to the current problem series.
a2cInsight: A long-running, well-regarded Czech physics correspondence seminar (39+ years) — strong evidence of sustained self-directed problem-solving, though its recognition is mostly regional/within the physics-olympiad community.

name: The University Physics Competition (UPhC)
prerequisites: Primarily undergraduate teams of up to 3, but ambitious advanced high schoolers with a faculty/university sponsor can occasionally participate; open-ended research-style physics modeling problem.
costNote: Team registration fee (modest, typically under $100/team) required.
cadence: Annual, 48-hour contest held over a November weekend.
howToStart: Find a faculty sponsor and register a team of up to 3 at uphysicsc.com ahead of the November contest weekend.
a2cInsight: Primarily a college-level credential, but exceptionally advanced high schoolers (via a university mentor) can use it to show research-caliber physics modeling — a nice stretch credential for very strong students, not a mainstream HS pathway.

name: New Zealand Physics Cup
prerequisites: New Zealand (or international) high school student; Seniors bracket (Year 12-13) and Juniors bracket (Year 9-11).
costNote: Free, student-led online competition.
cadence: Annual, run by a student-led organizing committee including IPhO medalists.
howToStart: Register via nzphysicscup.com when the current year's competition opens.
a2cInsight: A grassroots but Olympiad-caliber effort (built by IPhO medalists) filling the gap left by New Zealand's dormant national physics olympiad — legitimate niche credential, still growing its reputation.

name: New Zealand Young Physicists' Tournament (NZYPT)
prerequisites: New Zealand senior high school student, team-based, working through open-ended physics research problems.
costNote: Team/school participation costs are modest domestically; costs rise significantly for the team representing NZ at the international IYPT.
cadence: Annual national tournament feeding selection for New Zealand's IYPT team; NZ will host the 40th IYPT itself in Auckland in July 2027.
howToStart: Register your school/team via iypt.org.nz for the current year's NZYPT problem set and tournament.
a2cInsight: New Zealand's national feeder into the world-renowned IYPT; participation demonstrates genuine research-style physics teamwork, well-regarded within NZ STEM circles.

name: U.S. National Chemistry Olympiad (USNCO)
prerequisites: Enrolled US high school student; nominated by a local ACS section USNCO Coordinator to sit the local/national exam (no direct self-registration).
costNote: Local/national exam fees are typically nominal or covered by local ACS sections; Study Camp is fully funded.
cadence: Annual: local exams in spring, national exam in April, Study Camp in June, IChO team selection follows.
howToStart: Ask your chemistry teacher or local ACS section to register you for the local USNCO exam; strong scorers are invited to the National Exam.
a2cInsight: The single most respected pre-college chemistry credential in the US — making the 20-person Study Camp or the 4-student IChO team is genuinely elite and reads as such to any admissions officer.

name: International Chemistry Olympiad (IChO)
prerequisites: Must first win a spot on your country's 4-student national team through that country's national olympiad process (e.g., USNCO, UKChO, CCO).
costNote: Free to individual students; national teams/federations pay a participation fee (~$3,000/team) to attend.
cadence: Annual, mid-to-late July; IChO 2026 is in Tashkent, Uzbekistan (July 10-19).
howToStart: Excel in your national chemistry olympiad program; there is no direct individual application to IChO.
a2cInsight: A gold medal at IChO is one of the most globally recognized STEM credentials a high schooler can earn — instantly legible to any university worldwide.

name: You Be The Chemist Challenge (now CEF You Be The Solution Challenge)
prerequisites: Grades 5-8 student; often requires teacher/school registration and, for the newer format, a team of 3-4 students.
costNote: Regional participation is generally free or low-cost through participating schools; national finals travel is often covered.
cadence: Annual: school -> local -> state -> national challenge rounds, culminating in a National Challenge each spring.
howToStart: Register through the Chemical Educational Foundation (CEF in STEM) via your school or local NACD-affiliated chapter.
a2cInsight: A solid, legitimate middle-school chemistry credential with real regional-to-national structure, but its prestige ceiling is modest compared to olympiad-track programs.

name: Avogadro Exam (University of Waterloo)
prerequisites: Grade 11 (or grade 10/12 in some cases) student in their first high school chemistry course, at an accredited high school.
costNote: Small per-student exam fee paid by the registering school.
cadence: Annual, written in spring (registration opens mid-February) via teacher-administered online exam.
howToStart: Have your chemistry teacher register the school and class through the University of Waterloo Chemistry Contests office.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded but fairly low-stakes Canadian benchmark exam — useful for building a chemistry-competition track record, not a headline credential on its own.

name: UK Chemistry Olympiad (UKChO, RSC)
prerequisites: Enrolled at a secondary school/college in the British Isles, typically in final pre-university year (16+ recommended).
costNote: Free to students; schools register on students' behalf.
cadence: Annual; Round 1 written test (winter), Round 2 for top scorers, then IChO team selection.
howToStart: Ask your school/college to register you with the Royal Society of Chemistry — students cannot register directly.
a2cInsight: Gold/top-in-school results are a strong, well-recognized UK chemistry credential and the direct feeder into the UK's IChO team.

name: Cambridge Chemistry Challenge (C3L6)
prerequisites: Lower Sixth (roughly grade 11/AS-level) student with strong chemistry interest, beyond standard A-level syllabus.
costNote: Free; top scorers get a fully-funded residential at Cambridge.
cadence: Annual, written paper in schools around January/February.
howToStart: Register through your school with support from University of Cambridge Chemistry Outreach, OCR, and RSC.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded UK enrichment paper that signals genuine intellectual reach beyond the curriculum, especially valuable for Oxbridge-track applicants; top 0.5% earn a funded Cambridge residential.

name: International Junior Science Olympiad (IJSO)
prerequisites: Under 16 years old (as of December 31 of the competition year); must be selected onto a national team via a domestic junior science selection process.
costNote: Free to individual students; national delegations bear travel/participation costs.
cadence: Annual, typically December; IJSO 2026 will be held in Sofia, Bulgaria.
howToStart: Pursue your country's junior science olympiad selection pipeline (in the US and many countries this runs through national science olympiad bodies).
a2cInsight: A genuine international science credential accessible to middle schoolers that combines chemistry with physics and biology — strong signal of early, broad STEM talent, though the chemistry-specific angle is diluted by the tri-subject format.

name: Indian National Chemistry Olympiad (INChO) / NSEC
prerequisites: Indian students in grades 11-12 (age <20); must first clear the National Standard Examination in Chemistry (NSEC), open to top ~1% of NSEC sitters.
costNote: Modest exam registration fee for NSEC (Stage I, ~Rs 300); later stages are funded by HBCSE.
cadence: Annual: NSEC in November, INChO in January/February, Orientation Camp and Pre-departure Training follow.
howToStart: Register for NSEC through the Indian Association of Physics Teachers (IAPT) via your school.
a2cInsight: Extremely competitive (top 1% of 30,000+ NSEC sitters advance) — INChO qualification alone is a strong, nationally-scarce signal even without making the IChO team.

name: Canadian Chemistry Contest & Canadian Chemistry Olympiad (CCC/CCO)
prerequisites: Canadian high school/CEGEP student under 20; CCC targets top 10% of chemistry students, CCO targets top 5% and feeds Canada's IChO team.
costNote: Small per-student fee handled by the school; CCO training program itself is free.
cadence: Annual, written in April (CCC); CCO training runs October-March culminating in a take-home exam.
howToStart: Register through your school with the Chemical Institute of Canada (CIC); top CCC performers are invited into the CCO training stream.
a2cInsight: CCC is a solid, broadly accessible Canadian benchmark; advancing into the CCO training stream and IChO selection is a much rarer, elite-tier signal.

name: Chemistry Olympiad Ireland (ChOIRL)
prerequisites: Final-year secondary student (age 17-19) from Republic of Ireland or Northern Ireland.
costNote: Free to students; run through school/college nomination.
cadence: Annual: Round 1 in February at regional assessment centres, Round 2 in Dublin in April.
howToStart: Register through your school with CASTeL (Dublin City University) or the Irish Science Teachers' Association.
a2cInsight: The direct route to Ireland's IChO team; well-regarded nationally but modest international name recognition outside Ireland/UK.

name: New Zealand Chemistry Olympiad (NZChO)
prerequisites: Any Year 12 or below student (knowledge at Level 2 NCEA Chemistry level).
costNote: Registration around NZ$40 plus fees.
cadence: Annual; registration June-September, exam in late September, training group over the summer.
howToStart: Register directly online via the NZ Chemistry Olympiad Trust / Science Olympianz (SONZ) or through your school.
a2cInsight: A legitimate national pipeline to New Zealand's IChO team; useful evidence of chemistry initiative for NZ/Australia-bound applicants, less recognized internationally.

name: Singapore Junior Chemistry Olympiad (SJChO)
prerequisites: Secondary 3, 4, or 5 student in Singapore (or international student <=grade 10 / age 17).
costNote: Modest registration fee paid through school.
cadence: Annual, held for upper-secondary students; check SNIC site for current-year dates.
howToStart: Register through your school with the Singapore National Institute of Chemistry (SNIC), co-run with NUS and MOE.
a2cInsight: A solid Singapore-specific junior chemistry credential and stepping stone to the senior Singapore Chemistry Olympiad, but limited recognition outside Singapore/Southeast Asia.

name: Singapore Chemistry Olympiad (SChO)
prerequisites: JC1 (Junior College Year 1, roughly grade 11) or equivalent student in Singapore.
costNote: Modest registration fee paid through school.
cadence: Annual since 1989; JC1-level students.
howToStart: Register through your Junior College with SNIC/NUS Chemistry Department.
a2cInsight: The senior-level feeder to Singapore's IChO team; strong regional credential for Singapore-based applicants.

name: Philippine National Chemistry Olympiad (PNCO)
prerequisites: Filipino secondary school student (commonly Grade 11-12 STEM strand); advances through regional selection to national finals.
costNote: Regional/school-level exam fees are typically minimal.
cadence: Annual: regional selection rounds followed by the national finals; check PFCS site for current dates.
howToStart: Register through your school's science department with the Philippine Federation of Chemistry Societies (PFCS).
a2cInsight: The country's premier chemistry credential and direct pipeline to the Philippines' IChO team; well recognized domestically, especially among science high schools.

name: Chinese Chemistry Olympiad (CChO)
prerequisites: High school student in mainland China, Hong Kong, or Macau, under 20 at time of the upcoming IChO.
costNote: Low registration fee for preliminary round; funded further for finalists.
cadence: Annual: preliminary round in late August/early September, final round in late November/early December.
howToStart: Register through your school with the Chinese Chemical Society for the preliminary round.
a2cInsight: One of the hardest chemistry exams in the world by reputation — even reaching the ~170-student final round is an extraordinary, globally-legible achievement given 170,000+ annual entrants.

name: Olimpíada Brasileira de Química (OBQ) & OBQ Júnior
prerequisites: OBQ Júnior: grades 6-9; OBQ (senior): high school students. No prior qualification needed to enter first round.
costNote: Completely free to enter at all levels.
cadence: Annual, with state-level selection rounds feeding into national rounds on a coordinated national calendar.
howToStart: Register free through your school via obquimica.org (Programa Nacional Olimpíadas de Química).
a2cInsight: A free, large-scale (50,000+ participants) Brazilian olympiad with a genuine junior division, making it one of the more middle-school-accessible chemistry olympiads worldwide; strongest signal for Brazil-focused applications.

name: International Mendeleev Chemistry Olympiad (IMChO)
prerequisites: Secondary school student selected via national qualification (varies by country) or by invitation.
costNote: Free to individual competitors; hosted by Lomonosov Moscow State University with the Andrey Melnichenko Foundation.
cadence: Annual, typically held April/May; the 60th IMChO is scheduled April 15-23, 2026 in Moscow, Russia.
howToStart: Check with your country's chemistry olympiad body about a Mendeleev Olympiad nomination pathway, or contact the organizers directly at imcho@conf.msu.ru.
a2cInsight: A historic (est. 1967), UNESCO-recognized international olympiad considered by many nearly as prestigious as IChO — a strong credential, though less familiar to US admissions officers than IChO itself, and its Russia-hosting has made international participation/visibility more complicated in recent years.

name: International Youth Chemistry Olympiad (IYCO, Indonesia)
prerequisites: Open to Indonesian and international senior high school and university students; no prior qualifying round required.
costNote: Low registration fee for the online competition (exact amount set per cycle by organizers).
cadence: Annual online competition; check iyco-iysa.or.id for current-cycle dates.
howToStart: Register directly online through the IYCO/IYSA official website.
a2cInsight: An accessible, online-format regional olympiad organized by Indonesia's Science Association — legitimate but lower prestige and name recognition than IChO-track national olympiads, and it's a senior-high/university competition, not middle-school accessible.

name: International Chemistry Quiz (ICQ, RACI)
prerequisites: Secondary school students aged 12-18 (six divisions from Year 7 to Year 12); compete within your own country/state cohort.
costNote: Modest per-student entry fee, paid through school; early bird rates apply.
cadence: Annual, since 1982; typically run in the second half of the year (registration opens earlier in the year).
howToStart: Register through your school with the Royal Australian Chemical Institute (RACI).
a2cInsight: One of the few chemistry competitions genuinely built for middle schoolers (grade 7+) at scale (100,000+ students, 21 countries) — good early credential, but it's a quiz format, not olympiad-caliber problem solving.

name: International Chemistry Tournament (IChTo)
prerequisites: High school students competing in teams of 4-6; open-ended research-style problems assigned in advance.
costNote: Participation fee plus travel to host country (varies by year).
cadence: Annual, typically held in summer; IChTo 2026 is scheduled August 15-19 in Hoengseong, South Korea.
howToStart: Form or join a team through your school and register via the official IChTo website (ichto.org).
a2cInsight: A distinctive team-debate format (unlike standard written olympiads) that rewards scientific communication and teamwork — a good complement to, not replacement for, an olympiad medal.

name: International Chemistry Competition (IChC)
prerequisites: Junior division open to grade 10 and below (under 16); separate Youth (grades 11-13) and Senior (college+) divisions for older students.
costNote: Qualification round is free; Semi-Final registration is a modest fee (roughly €12); financial aid available.
cadence: Annual: online Qualification Round (~March), Semi-Finals (~May), Final Exam (~June).
howToStart: Register directly online at intchc.org for the Qualification Round.
a2cInsight: A newer, low-barrier online olympiad explicitly designed to include younger/beginner students alongside experienced competitors — good entry point, but not yet an established prestige credential.

name: ChemiCo (StemCo)
prerequisites: Secondary school student in grades 7-12 with general secondary-level chemistry syllabus knowledge; no age restrictions; online registration.
costNote: Low online registration fee typical of StemCo-family competitions.
cadence: Annual, 90-minute online multiple-choice competition, held up to four times a year; check stemco.org/chemico for current dates.
howToStart: Register directly online at stemco.org/chemico.
a2cInsight: A low-stakes, easy-entry online quiz good for building early interest and genuinely middle-school accessible, but minor prestige compared to national/international olympiads.

name: Japan Chemistry Grand Prix (Chemical Society of Japan)
prerequisites: Japanese high school students or younger; direct individual registration.
costNote: Free to enter; funded by the Chemical Society of Japan and the Dream Chemistry-21 Committee.
cadence: Annual since 1999; qualifying exam followed by a final round with lab/experimental components, typically in August.
howToStart: Register through the official Chemical Society of Japan Chemistry Grand Prix website (gp.csj.jp).
a2cInsight: Japan's flagship chemistry competition (nicknamed the 'Chemistry Koshien') and the primary domestic feeder to its IChO team — top results are a strong, nationally elite credential, though the site is Japanese-language only.

name: Grand Contest on Chemistry for High School Students (Gracon, Japan)
prerequisites: High school (or younger) students, individually or in teams, presenting original chemistry research/projects.
costNote: Free; sponsored by Shibaura Institute of Technology, MEXT, JST, and industry partners.
cadence: Annual since 2004 (restarted 2023 under Shibaura Institute of Technology); presentations/finals typically in autumn.
howToStart: Submit a research report/project via the official Gracon website (s-gracon.jp) by the annual deadline.
a2cInsight: Unlike quiz-style olympiads, this rewards original student research in chemistry — a good fit for students building an independent-research profile rather than pure exam performance.

name: Chemagination (ACS)
prerequisites: Team of 2-3 currently enrolled high school students (accredited or homeschool).
costNote: Regional-meeting participation costs vary; often free or nominal through school/local-section teams.
cadence: Annual, tied to ACS regional meetings (e.g., MARM); team submissions due ahead of each region's event.
howToStart: Have your team write a ChemMatters-style article (with cover) on a future chemistry innovation and submit through your ACS local section, then advance to your ACS region's (e.g., MARM) event.
a2cInsight: A creative, low-barrier way to pair chemistry content knowledge with science writing/design — a nice portfolio piece but a regional-scale competition, not a national prestige credential.

name: ACS Project SEED
prerequisites: US high school student who has completed at least one chemistry course, typically from an economically disadvantaged background, with interest in chemistry/STEM research.
costNote: Free and paid — students receive a stipend (roughly $4,000) for the 8-10 week program.
cadence: Annual; applications typically open in February for an 8-10 week summer research placement.
howToStart: Apply online via acs.org/education/students/highschool/seed, often with support from a local ACS section coordinator.
a2cInsight: A genuinely valuable paid research apprenticeship in a real lab — strong for demonstrating sustained research experience and commitment, especially meaningful given its access/equity mission.

name: ACS Scholars Program
prerequisites: African American/Black, Hispanic/Latino, or American Indian/Native American graduating high school senior (or early college student) planning to major in chemistry, biochemistry, chemical engineering, or chemical technology; US citizen/permanent resident with demonstrated financial need.
costNote: Free to apply; renewable scholarship award up to $5,000/year.
cadence: Annual application cycle (applications typically open in December, deadline in March) for graduating high school seniors heading into a chemical-sciences degree.
howToStart: Apply online through the American Chemical Society (acs.org/education/students/highschool/seed/scholarship.html) with transcripts and recommendation letters.
a2cInsight: A meaningful, renewable need/merit scholarship specifically supporting underrepresented students in chemistry — signals both financial need context and STEM commitment to colleges.

name: ACS ChemClub Student Scholarship
prerequisites: Active member of a chartered ACS ChemClub, current high school senior planning full-time enrollment at a US college in a STEM-related major.
costNote: Free to apply; scholarship award granted to select ChemClub seniors.
cadence: Annual, for graduating high school seniors who are active ChemClub members.
howToStart: Join or start an ACS-chartered ChemClub at your school, stay active through senior year, then apply via acs.org with a teacher advisor recommendation and transcript.
a2cInsight: A nice, easily-earned local credential for consistent extracurricular chemistry involvement, but modest national prestige on its own.

name: USA Biolympiad (USABO)
prerequisites: Enrolled in grades 9-12 at a US high school (or homeschooled); US citizenship/permanent residency required to advance past the Open Exam to Semifinals.
costNote: $125 school registration fee covers the Open Exam for all students at that school; no direct student fee for Open Exam.
cadence: Annual; Open Exam in fall (Nov), Semifinal exam in winter, National Finals (residential) in spring/summer at a university campus (e.g., Harvard).
howToStart: Ask your school (or an authorized USABO testing center) to register for the Open Exam through usabo-trc.org; study AP Bio-level content plus Campbell Biology.
a2cInsight: Making USABO Semifinalist or National Finalist is one of the most respected STEM olympiad credentials on a college application, roughly on par with USAMO in prestige within its subject.

name: International Biology Olympiad (IBO)
prerequisites: Must be selected as one of 4 team members representing your home country, via that country's national biology olympiad (e.g., USABO in the US, INBO in India, CBO in Canada).
costNote: No fee to the individual student; national teams are funded by their sponsoring organizations (e.g., CEE for the US team).
cadence: Annual, typically in July; IBO 2026 (37th edition) is in Vilnius, Lithuania (July 12-19).
howToStart: Compete in and win your national biology olympiad program; there is no direct individual application to IBO itself.
a2cInsight: An IBO medal (especially gold) is an internationally elite, globally recognized credential that admissions offices and scholarship committees treat as top-tier evidence of scientific talent.

name: British Biology Olympiad (BBO)
prerequisites: Open to students in Year 12/13 in England & Wales (or equivalent Y13/14 NI, S5/S6 Scotland); worldwide equivalent year groups may enter via a school.
costNote: Free for all UK schools (UKBC has secured funding); schools outside the UK pay a flat £30 to enter an unlimited number of students.
cadence: Annual, sat in school in the winter (typically January).
howToStart: Register through your school with UK Biology Competitions (UKBC) at ukbiologycompetitions.org; the top scorers form the UK IBO training squad.
a2cInsight: A Gold or top-25 result in the BBO is a strong, internationally legible signal of biology aptitude, especially valuable for UK/US applications from British and international schools.

name: UK Biology Challenge
prerequisites: Open to Y9/Y10 students in England & Wales (or equivalent internationally, roughly ages 13-15).
costNote: Small per-student entry fee via the school, typically a few pounds.
cadence: Annual, sat in school, typically in the spring term.
howToStart: Register through your school with UKBC at ukbiologycompetitions.org; two 25-minute multiple-choice papers.
a2cInsight: A good entry point into competitive biology for younger students; a Gold award is a nice early credential but carries modest weight on its own for older-student applications.

name: UK Intermediate Biology Olympiad
prerequisites: Open to students in their first year of post-16 study (Y12 in England & Wales, roughly age 16-17) or equivalent.
costNote: Small per-student entry fee via the school.
cadence: Annual, sat in school in the winter/spring term.
howToStart: Register through your school with UKBC at ukbiologycompetitions.org as a stepping stone toward the British Biology Olympiad.
a2cInsight: Useful bridge credential in the UK biology olympiad pathway, but less recognized internationally than the full British Biology Olympiad.

name: Genes in Space
prerequisites: US students in grades 7-12, individually or in teams of up to 3, propose a DNA-analysis/biotech experiment for space.
costNote: Free to enter; no application fee.
cadence: Annual; applications open January-April, winner announced mid-year, experiment flown to ISS later in the year.
howToStart: Read the rules and past winning proposals at genesinspace.org, form a team, and submit a research proposal by the spring deadline.
a2cInsight: Winning (having your experiment actually flown to the ISS) is a rare, headline-worthy achievement that strongly signals original scientific thinking to selective colleges.

name: BioGENEius Challenge
prerequisites: Enrolled in grades 9-12; must complete original biotechnology research and compete in a regional/state affiliate (CA, CO, DE, GA, IL, IA, KS, MD, NM, NC, PA, VA) or the At-Large track if no local option exists.
costNote: Regional/state competitions typically have modest or no entry fees; travel to International Finals in NYC is a family cost unless covered.
cadence: Annual; regional/state competitions in spring, International BioGENEius Challenge finals in fall (Oct) at CURE Innovation Campus in NYC.
howToStart: Check biotechinstitute.org/biogeneius for your regional affiliate or apply At-Large; prepare a poster/presentation on original biotech research.
a2cInsight: A respected niche biotech research credential, particularly valuable for students pursuing biomedical/biotech-focused applications, though less universally known than ISEF.

name: Canadian Biology Olympiad (CBO)
prerequisites: Canadian secondary school students (or first-year CEGEP in Quebec); open exam has broad eligibility, top scorers advance toward IBO team selection.
costNote: Free online exam.
cadence: Annual, single online exam round typically held in April.
howToStart: Register via canadianbiologyolympiad.ca ahead of the April exam window; study university-intro-level biology.
a2cInsight: The official pipeline to Canada's IBO team, so a top national ranking is a strong, verifiable biology credential for Canadian applicants.

name: University of Toronto National Biology Competition
prerequisites: Open to secondary school students (Canada and internationally) in grades 11-12 via participating schools.
costNote: Small per-student fee (about $7 CAD), paid through school registration; minimum of 5 students per school.
cadence: Annual, written exam typically in spring, hosted by University of Toronto since 1995.
howToStart: Have your school register through biocomp.utoronto.ca; exam covers standard high-school biology topics.
a2cInsight: A solid, long-running Canadian academic credential, but more of a strong regional accolade than a nationally elite one like CBO/USABO.

name: Indian National Biology Olympiad (INBO)
prerequisites: Indian students in classes 11-12, entering via the National Standard Examination in Biology (NSEB) administered by IAPT/HBCSE.
costNote: Modest exam registration fee for the initial NSEB stage.
cadence: Multi-stage annual cycle: NSEB (Nov) to INBO (Jan/Feb) to Orientation-cum-Selection Camp (Jun) to Pre-Departure Camp, feeding the IBO team.
howToStart: Register for the NSEB through your school or the HBCSE Olympiad portal at olympiads.hbcse.tifr.res.in.
a2cInsight: Reaching INBO or the OCSC camp is an extremely selective, nationally elite Indian STEM credential comparable in rigor to USABO Semifinalist/Finalist status.

name: New Zealand International Biology Olympiad (NZIBO)
prerequisites: All current New Zealand secondary school students may register for the entrance exam; advancement to the selection camp requires strong biology performance.
costNote: Modest program/registration fee for the online tutorial and exam program.
cadence: Annual programme with online tutorials followed by selection exams, culminating in IBO team selection.
howToStart: Register through nzibo.org for the online tutorial programme, which also strengthens standard NCEA/school biology performance.
a2cInsight: NZ's official IBO feeder program; consistent medal success at IBO makes a national NZIBO placement a credible, internationally comparable achievement.

name: Philippine Biology Olympiad (PBO)
prerequisites: Filipino students in grades 7-12; entry typically through participating schools.
costNote: Modest registration fee per student/school.
cadence: Annual, multi-stage (elimination, semifinal, final rounds), organized by Ateneo de Manila University's Department of Biology; PBO 2026 (SINAG) runs January-March 2026.
howToStart: Register through pbolympiad.org or a participating school; the national event feeds the Philippines' IBO team.
a2cInsight: The country's premier biology credential and IBO pathway, valuable for Philippine and Southeast-Asia-based applicants, though less internationally known than USABO/IBO itself.

name: Singapore Biology Olympiad (SBO)
prerequisites: Singapore junior college / through-train school students (roughly Years 7-12, typically the 11th year / age 17) with strong biology background.
costNote: Modest entry fee via school registration.
cadence: Annual, run by the Singapore Institute of Biology in coordination with MOE, held in November.
howToStart: Register through your school via the Singapore Institute of Biology (sibiol.org.sg) ahead of the annual exam.
a2cInsight: Singapore's official IBO feeder, so it carries genuine national-elite weight for Singapore-based applicants; long-running since 2000/2001.

name: Singapore Junior Biology Olympiad (SJBO)
prerequisites: Singapore upper secondary school students (younger than SBO eligibility, roughly Sec 1-4).
costNote: Modest entry fee via school registration.
cadence: Annual, established 2016 as a junior complement to the SBO; theory round typically held around May.
howToStart: Register through your school; see sjbo.sibiol.org.sg for exam format and past papers.
a2cInsight: A good early stepping-stone credential for younger Singapore students aiming eventually at SBO/IBO, but modest weight on its own.

name: Singapore Biology League (SBL)
prerequisites: Teams of 3-5 students currently enrolled in a Singapore pre-university institution (Junior College/Millennia Institute/Polytechnic/ITE or international equivalent).
costNote: Team registration fee, modest and typically split among 3-5 team members.
cadence: Annual online team competition, open-book format, typically held mid-year.
howToStart: Register a team at sgbioleague.org before the contest window; format rewards critical thinking over pure memorization.
a2cInsight: A fun, accessible team-based supplement to the more elite SBO/SJBO individual olympiads; good for building interest rather than admissions prestige.

name: South African Life Sciences Olympiad
prerequisites: South African secondary students, typically grades 10-11.
costNote: Modest entry fee coordinated through the National Science Olympiad Organizing Committee.
cadence: Annual, established 1997, three rounds (March-June, August, and a February selection round), feeds South Africa's IBO team.
howToStart: Register through your school via scienceolympiad.co.za for the National Science Olympiad in Natural, Life & Physical Sciences.
a2cInsight: South Africa's official IBO qualifying pathway; a national top placement is a credible elite biology credential regionally.

name: African Biology Olympiad (ABO)
prerequisites: Junior category (JSS1-JSS3, roughly grades 7-9) and Senior category (SS1-SS3, roughly grades 10-12) students across Africa, primarily Nigeria-led so far.
costNote: Modest per-student registration fee; schools register first, then students.
cadence: Annual, launched its maiden edition in 2025.
howToStart: Register your school (or as an individual student if unaffiliated) at africanbiologyolympiad.org.
a2cInsight: A brand-new, still-establishing continental competition; a strong result is a promising credential but not yet a widely recognized brand outside the region.

name: iGEM High School Division
prerequisites: Team of high school students (at least 13 years old, with an instructor/PI who is a school employee) designs and executes an original synthetic-biology project over a season.
