costNote: Team registration fees run several thousand dollars per team (roughly €4,950-€7,700 across Stage I and II as of 2026), plus lab/reagent and travel costs, usually covered by school/sponsor funding.
cadence: Season runs May-October, culminating in the Giant Jamboree in late October/November.
howToStart: Form a school team, find a faculty/industry advisor, and register at competition.igem.org for the High School track.
a2cInsight: A serious, lab-intensive synthetic biology credential that signals genuine hands-on research capability, valued by biology/bioengineering-focused admissions readers, though team-based and resource-heavy.

name: National Ocean Sciences Bowl (NOSB)
prerequisites: High school team of 4-5 students plus a coach; open to any enrolled high schooler.
costNote: Team/regional entry fees are typically modest and often subsidized by the hosting institution; regional bowls may charge up to $300 per team, though many charge less or nothing, and Nationals is free.
cadence: Annual; regional competitions in winter/spring, National Finals in late spring/summer.
howToStart: Find your regional NOSB competition via nosb.org and form a school team to prepare on ocean/marine science topics.
a2cInsight: A solid, well-established quiz-bowl credential for marine/ocean science interest; good team activity but a lower prestige ceiling than individual olympiads like USABO.

name: International Brain Bee
prerequisites: Open to students aged 13-19, regardless of academic standing; study materials are free (Brain Facts, Neuroscience textbook).
costNote: Local/regional chapters typically charge little to no entry fee; costs mainly emerge if advancing to national/international travel.
cadence: Annual; local and regional bees throughout the school year, national and international finals typically mid-to-late year.
howToStart: Find your local/regional chapter via thebrainbee.org, study the provided free materials, and compete in a quiz-format exam and practical stations.
a2cInsight: A well-recognized global neuroscience credential; making Nationals or Internationals is a strong signal for students pursuing neuroscience/pre-med interests.

name: NCF-Envirothon
prerequisites: Teams of 5 high school students (grades 9-12, ages 14-19) competing in aquatics, forestry, soils, wildlife, and a current environmental issue.
costNote: State/regional qualifiers typically have low or no fees; travel to the international finals is the main cost.
cadence: Annual; state/provincial competitions in spring, NCF-Envirothon international final in July.
howToStart: Find your state/provincial Envirothon program (listed via envirothon.org) and form a team through your school or club.
a2cInsight: North America's largest and most established high-school environmental science team competition; a strong, legitimate credential for environmental/biology-focused applicants.

name: MATE ROV Competition
prerequisites: K-12 team (ranges: Scout for grades K-4, Navigator, Ranger, Explorer classes by age/skill, up through grade 12) builds and pilots an underwater ROV for ocean-science-based missions; teams need at least 3 students plus an adult mentor.
costNote: Registration plus ROV build materials typically run $100-$500+ depending on class level; travel to Worlds is extra.
cadence: Annual; regional competitions in spring, World Championship in June.
howToStart: Register a team via materovcompetition.org and choose the appropriate class for your grade/experience level.
a2cInsight: A respected marine-engineering/ocean-science team credential; strong for students blending biology/environmental science with engineering, though resource- and mentor-dependent.

name: National DNA Day Essay Contest (ASHG)
prerequisites: Students grades 9-12 worldwide; essay must be original, under 750 words, in English, with at least one cited reference; individual submissions only.
costNote: Free to enter; requires creating a free ASHG account.
cadence: Annual; essay prompt released in early spring, submissions due around DNA Day (April 25), with recent deadlines extended into early March.
howToStart: Create an account at apply.ashg.org, review the current year's prompt at ashg.org/dna-day, and submit before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded, genetics-specific essay credential run by a major professional society; winning is a credible signal of both scientific literacy and writing ability, though the pool is large (1,200+ essays from dozens of countries) and essays are short.

name: Plant the Moon Challenge
prerequisites: High school (and university/professional) teams (up to 5 students, minimum age 13) design a plant-growth experiment in lunar/Mars regolith simulant; a Plant the Moon Challenge Jr. exists for elementary/middle schoolers.
costNote: Registration and simulant/regolith materials typically cost a modest amount, often subsidized by state Space Grant Consortia; a Jr. track exists for younger students.
cadence: Annual, registration opens each spring, 8-week growing/experiment period.
howToStart: Register at plantthemoon.com, obtain a Project Guide and simulant, and design/report an 8-week growth experiment.
a2cInsight: A NASA-Artemis-linked hands-on plant biology/space biology project; a nice authentic-research credential for space-bio-interested students, though still a fairly young and lower-prestige program.

name: National Biology Bowl
prerequisites: Middle school teams of 4-5 students; quiz-bowl format covering cell biology, genetics, physiology, microorganisms, animals, plants, evolution, and ecology.
costNote: Modest team registration fee for the online (Zoom-based) competition.
cadence: Annual, held on Zoom on the last Saturday of May; running since 2019.
howToStart: Register a team via nationalbiologybowl.org ahead of the May competition.
a2cInsight: A fun, accessible entry point to competitive biology for middle schoolers; low prestige weight but a good confidence-builder and biology-club activity.

name: MIT THINK Scholars Program
prerequisites: US high school students (any grade) submit a written proposal for an original STEM research/engineering project, which can be biology-focused.
costNote: Free to apply; selected finalists receive up to $1,000 in project funding plus a fully-funded MIT campus trip (when travel is permitted).
cadence: Annual; applications open in the fall, finalists announced winter, project executed over the spring semester.
howToStart: Draft a research proposal for a project completable in one semester on a ~$1,000 budget and submit via think.mit.edu.
a2cInsight: A selective MIT-run research-funding and mentorship award; strong signal of initiative and technical proposal-writing, though it is proposal-based rather than a finished-research competition like ISEF.

name: Sanofi Biogenius Canada
prerequisites: Canadian high school/CEGEP students propose university-level biotech research and are matched with a mentor from the local biotech community.
costNote: Free to participate; program is sponsor-funded, though it was paused in 2025 for reassessment and current-year status should be verified.
cadence: Normally annual with regional competitions in spring; paused in 2025 for program assessment, refreshed program details pending as of mid-2026.
howToStart: Check biogenius.ca for current program status before beginning; historically required a project proposal in the fall to secure a mentor.
a2cInsight: Historically a well-respected biotech mentorship-research credential in Canada, but its 2025 pause for reassessment means students should verify it is running before counting on it.

name: National Youth Science Camp (NYSCamp)
prerequisites: Two graduating high school seniors per US state (plus DC) are nominated, typically through a state selection process tied to state science fairs or academic achievement; delegates attend the summer after graduation.
costNote: Fully funded (housing, meals, travel) residential honors program; free to selected delegates.
cadence: Annual, roughly three-week residential program each summer in West Virginia (2026 dates July 11 - Aug 1); nominees selected via state-level processes in spring.
howToStart: Ask your state's NYSCamp coordinator (listed via nysacademy.org) about the state nomination/selection process, usually linked to strong science fair or academic performance.
a2cInsight: A prestigious, fully-funded honors recognition (not a competition per se) that signals your state identified you as a top science student; valuable but selection pathways vary significantly by state.

name: USA National Brain Bee Championship
prerequisites: Ages 13-19, enrolled in accredited US high school, must have won a Local Brain Bee; one lifetime entry to Nationals.
costNote: $400 registration fee per competitor (covers meals/shirt); $50 per guest.
cadence: Annual, held each spring (2026 championship April 25 at UC Irvine); qualify via a Local Brain Bee in fall/winter.
howToStart: Locate and register for a Local Brain Bee chapter (many run through university neuroscience departments) in your state; win it to advance to Nationals.
a2cInsight: Being a US National Brain Bee finalist or state champion is a strong, verifiable STEM credential that signals deep self-directed study beyond the classroom.

name: UK Brain Bee
prerequisites: Secondary students aged 13-18 in the UK (GCSE/A-Level or equivalent); no prior neuroscience experience required.
costNote: Free to register individually or through school.
cadence: Annual: online preliminary round March, national final (Imperial College London) April, IBB World Championship representation later in the year.
howToStart: Register online individually or through your school at ukbrainbee.com for the preliminary round.
a2cInsight: A credible national-level science credential for UK students that also feeds into the globally recognized IBB pipeline via the national champion, who explicitly advances to the IBB World Championship.

name: British Neuroscience Olympiad (British Brain Bee)
prerequisites: Ages 14-19; regional round open globally, national round narrows to top 50 regional scorers.
costNote: Paid registration via online portal; exact fee not published but positioned as low-cost regional/national exam entry.
cadence: Annual: regional round mid-March (proctored online, 50 questions/30 min), national round later in the year in London.
howToStart: Register for the regional online round at britishneuroscienceolympiad.co.uk; study neuroanatomy/histology/clinical case material.
a2cInsight: A separate, more exam-focused UK neuroscience credential explicitly stated by its own organizers to have no affiliation with the International Brain Bee (its winner does NOT advance to IBB Worlds), useful as an additional but slightly lower-prestige data point versus the UK Brain Bee.

name: France Brain Bee / Neuroscience Olympiad (Olympiades des Neurosciences)
prerequisites: Students under 19 enrolled in French educational institutions (including foreign students studying in France); requires a reference teacher to supervise participation, plus a competitive application/cover letter.
costNote: No participation fee; partial travel-cost assistance available for students outside Paris region.
cadence: Annual: applications typically open in fall (Nov-Dec), results by end of January, competition held at Paris Brain Institute (ICM).
howToStart: Submit an application with a teacher sponsor via the Paris Brain Institute Olympiads page during the fall registration window.
a2cInsight: France's official IBB-affiliated national round, running since 2019; winning sends the champion to the IBB World Championship (with a prep internship at Paris Brain Institute), giving it solid international credibility for French students.

name: Canadian National Brain Bee
prerequisites: High school students who win a local/provincial Canadian Brain Bee, aged roughly 13-19.
costNote: Local/regional bees typically free or low-cost; national event costs vary by host institution.
cadence: Annual, national event hosted by a rotating Canadian university (supported by CIHR); local qualifiers held earlier in the year.
howToStart: Find a local Canadian Brain Bee chapter through brainbee.ca and register to compete.
a2cInsight: Canada's official IBB national round, backed by the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR); winning is a credible national science achievement for Canadian applicants.

name: International Neuroscience Olympiad (INO)
prerequisites: Enrolled high school/secondary or homeschool student, or college freshman, 18 or younger as of the eligibility cutoff date.
costNote: $10 registration fee for the Open Round; fee waivers available for financial hardship.
cadence: Annual, three rounds: Open Round (fall/winter, MCQ + data analysis), Semifinal (20 questions), Final (research case-study proposals presented to judges).
howToStart: Register online at ino-global.net (or via ASEEDER for students in Asia) and prepare using released mock exams before the Open Round.
a2cInsight: A research-oriented alternative to Brain Bee's memorization format, emphasizing data analysis and proposal-writing; strong signal for students aiming at a research-heavy neuroscience track, though less internationally established than IBB.

name: AAN Neuroscience Research Prize (incl. Bhuwan Garg High School Prize)
prerequisites: Enrolled in US secondary school grades 9-12; original individual (not group) neuroscience research required.
costNote: No application fee; winners receive travel, hotel, and per diem support to present.
cadence: Annual; applications open August, deadline late October, winners notified end of January, presentation at AAN Annual Meeting (spring).
howToStart: Complete an independent neuroscience research project (lab-based or clinical) with a mentor, then submit via the American Academy of Neurology's application portal.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, judged research-recognition award from a major medical professional society; winning and presenting a poster at a national AAN meeting is a genuine research credential, not just a certificate mill.

name: Brain Awareness Video Contest (BAVC) — Society for Neuroscience
prerequisites: Open to SfN members; non-members eligible if sponsored/submitted by an SfN member. No age floor specified, so middle/high schoolers can enter with a sponsoring scientist or teacher.
costNote: No entry fee; entrants need SfN membership or a member sponsor.
cadence: Annual, entries open ~March, close late June; winners announced later in the year.
howToStart: Create an original short (under 5 min) educational video on a neuroscience concept, secure an SfN-member sponsor if needed, and submit via brainfacts.org rules page.
a2cInsight: A fun, low-barrier way to show initiative and science communication skill, but far less selective/prestigious than IBB; best as a supplementary rather than headline credential.

name: Neuroethics Essay Contest (International Neuroethics Society / IYNA)
prerequisites: High school category: single author enrolled in high school/equivalent, essay under 1,000 words in English on a neuroethics topic.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; submission deadline typically late July.
howToStart: Choose a focused neuroethics issue (e.g., neurotechnology and law, brain privacy), write within the word limit, and submit through the International Neuroethics Society website.
a2cInsight: A niche but genuinely selective interdisciplinary (neuroscience + ethics/policy) essay prize run by a real academic society; a good fit for students bridging STEM and humanities interests.

name: IYNA Ideathon (with Alzheimer's Association)
prerequisites: Secondary school and undergraduate students; teams develop a research/intervention proposal on a neurodegeneration theme (e.g., Alzheimer's).
costNote: Free to participate; virtual format.
cadence: Annual, roughly Jan/Feb-March; virtual training weeks culminating in a live finale event.
howToStart: Register through IYNA (youthneuro.org/ideathon) and form or join a team before the registration deadline; attend training sessions.
a2cInsight: A youth-run but partnership-backed (Alzheimer's Association) proposal competition; legitimate for showing initiative in translational neuroscience, though newer and less prestigious than an established research prize.

name: IYNA Journal
prerequisites: Open to ages 5-26 interested in neuroscience; write an original article (review, opinion, or research-style) on a neuroscience topic.
costNote: Free to submit and publish.
cadence: Rolling submissions; 3-6 month turnaround from submission to publication.
howToStart: Draft an article and submit through the IYNA Journal portal at youthneuro.org; go through two rounds of formatting and two rounds of student editing.
a2cInsight: Not peer-reviewed and run entirely by students, so it signals initiative and communication skill rather than rigorous academic vetting — useful as a stepping stone, not a substitute for a peer-reviewed venue.

name: Project Brain Light Essay Contest
prerequisites: Delaware students grades 6-12 (public, private, home, or cyber school, or youth organization); separate divisions for middle school (6-8) and high school (9-12).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual, tied to Brain Awareness Week (typically winter/spring submission window).
howToStart: Write an original essay on the year's theme (e.g., biomedical research and personal life) and submit per instructions on projectbrainlight.org.
a2cInsight: A small, regionally-limited (Delaware) essay contest — good early practice and a legitimate credential for local students, but not nationally competitive or widely recognized.

name: Project Brain Light Poster Contest
prerequisites: Delaware students (public, private, home, or cyber school, or youth organization); original artwork illustrating biomedical research/neuroscience themes.
costNote: No entry fee; $100 cash prize for winners.
cadence: Annual, tied to Brain Awareness Week.
howToStart: Create an original poster on the year's theme and submit by mail or digitally with a signed release form per projectbrainlight.org instructions.
a2cInsight: A very local, entry-level art-meets-science competition; fine for a Delaware middle/high schooler's portfolio but carries little weight outside the region.

name: IBB NextGen Program (FENS Forum)
prerequisites: Top 5 performers from the International Brain Bee World Championship.
costNote: Travel/registration typically covered for selected top competitors.
cadence: Recurring, tied to the FENS Forum (biennial); most recent cohort attends FENS Forum in Barcelona, July 2026.
howToStart: Qualify by finishing among the top scorers at the IBB World Championship; selection is automatic based on IBB results, no separate application.
a2cInsight: An elite, invitation-only add-on to an already elite credential (IBB) — signals top-tier international recognition, but is unreachable without first excelling at Brain Bee. No dedicated NextGen website exists; official info is published via FENS and the International Brain Bee (thebrainbee.org).

name: Cajal Club International Brain Bee Awards (Neuroanatomy/Histology & Neural Progenitor Award)
prerequisites: Must be a competitor at the IBB World Championship; awards go to top neuroanatomy/histology station scorer and top live-judging-round finisher.
costNote: No cost; cash prizes ($100-$500) awarded automatically to qualifying IBB finalists.
cadence: Annual, awarded at the IBB World Championship.
howToStart: Qualify for and compete at the IBB World Championship — the Cajal Club awards are conferred automatically based on in-competition performance.
a2cInsight: A prestigious specialty add-on recognizing neuroanatomical mastery within the IBB Worlds, from a respected 90+ year-old neuroanatomy society; strong niche signal for a would-be neuroscientist.

name: TOPSS Competition for High School Psychology Students (APA)
prerequisites: Current high school student worldwide, individual entries only; annual prompt ties psychological science to a current topic (e.g., misinformation, AI in learning).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; deadline typically in spring (e.g., late March) each year.
howToStart: Watch for the year's prompt on apa.org/ed/precollege/topss, produce an original under-3-minute video addressing it with a teacher's review/approval, and submit before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A respectable, free video-essay competition from the American Psychological Association bridging psychology and (often) neuroscience/brain topics; solid but not top-tier prestige.

name: National Brain Research Centre (NBRC) India — Student Outreach Programmes
prerequisites: Varies; NBRC is India's premier dedicated brain-research institute and periodically runs outreach/training programmes (e.g., Open Days, National Science Day events) that pre-college students can inquire about.
costNote: Government-funded institute; specific student programme costs vary and are typically free or nominal.
cadence: Varies by programme; check current listings on nbrc.ac.in as offerings change year to year.
howToStart: Review current programme listings at nbrc.ac.in and contact the institute directly regarding any open pre-college outreach opportunities.
a2cInsight: Not a standing student competition per se — more a research institute whose occasional outreach programmes can add legitimacy to an India-based student's neuroscience interest, but availability should be verified each year.

name: Houston Brain Bee (Rice Neuroscience Society Local Chapter)
prerequisites: High school students; open to participants beyond the Houston area (residency not required).
costNote: Local chapter competitions are typically free to enter.
cadence: Annual, held early spring (2026 edition: March 1, 2026) at Rice University.
howToStart: Register through the Rice Neuroscience Society Brain Bee page ahead of the local competition date; study the official IBB study guide.
a2cInsight: A representative example of the many Local Brain Bee chapters worldwide — winning advances you to the state/national round, but the local round itself is an accessible entry point rather than a headline credential.

name: Regional Brain Bee — Rutgers/Brain Health Institute (Newark, NJ)
prerequisites: High school students in the greater Newark/NJ area.
costNote: Free to enter local competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held in March at New Jersey Medical School, Newark.
howToStart: Register through the Rutgers Brain Health Institute regional Brain Bee page before the competition date.
a2cInsight: Another concrete example of a real, active Local/Regional Brain Bee chapter — a solid, accessible first rung on the Brain Bee ladder for New Jersey students.

name: University of Pittsburgh Brain Bee (Center for Neuroscience)
prerequisites: Local Pittsburgh-area students ages 13-19; local chapters of the Brain Bee generally accept both middle and high schoolers within that age range.
costNote: Free local chapter competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held winter/early spring at the University of Pittsburgh.
howToStart: Register via the University of Pittsburgh Center for Neuroscience (CNUP) Brain Bee outreach page before the local competition date.
a2cInsight: A well-established, university-run Local Brain Bee chapter that feeds into the Pennsylvania/national pipeline; a legitimate, accessible regional entry point.

name: Boston University Brain Bee
prerequisites: Open to students of all ages, but only those aged 13-19 are eligible to advance to the national competition.
costNote: Free local chapter competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held winter/spring at Boston University.
howToStart: Register through BU's Undergraduate Program in Neuroscience Brain Bee page before the local competition date.
a2cInsight: Another real, long-running Local Brain Bee chapter run by a top neuroscience undergraduate program — accessible entry point with strong institutional backing.

name: Albany Medical College Brain Bee (D-NET)
prerequisites: Local Capital Region (NY) students ages 13-19, regardless of academic standing.
costNote: Free local chapter competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held late winter/early spring (late Feb/early March).
howToStart: Register through Albany Medical College's Department of Neuroscience & Experimental Therapeutics Brain Bee page.
a2cInsight: A real regional Local Brain Bee hosted by a medical college — legitimate accessible entry point, particularly useful for upstate New York students.

name: KU Medical Center Brain Bee
prerequisites: Local Kansas-area high school students; KU's own materials describe the event specifically as for high schoolers, unlike some other chapters that explicitly welcome middle schoolers.
costNote: Free local chapter competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held winter/early spring at the University of Kansas Medical Center.
howToStart: Register via the KU Medical Center announcement/outreach page ahead of the annual competition.
a2cInsight: A genuine, university-hosted Local Brain Bee for the Kansas region — solid accessible entry to the national Brain Bee pipeline, though framed as high-school-only rather than middle-school-inclusive.

name: University of Florida Brain Bee (SfN North Central FL Chapter)
prerequisites: Local North Central Florida middle and high school students, ages 13-19.
costNote: Free local chapter competition.
cadence: Annual, typically held winter/early spring.
howToStart: Register through the UF Department of Neuroscience outreach page for the SfN North Central FL Chapter Brain Bee.
a2cInsight: A genuine, SfN-chapter-affiliated Local Brain Bee — legitimate accessible entry point for Florida students heading toward the state/national pipeline; explicitly open to middle schoolers.

name: Indiana University Indianapolis Brain Bee
prerequisites: Local Indiana-area students in grades 9-12 (event page explicitly states 'Open to students in grades 9-12').
costNote: Free local chapter competition; winner receives a scholarship to IU Indianapolis School of Science plus trip to Nationals.
cadence: Annual, typically held winter/early spring.
howToStart: Register through the IU Indianapolis Department of Neuroscience Brain Bee event page.
a2cInsight: A real Local Brain Bee with an added college-scholarship incentive from the host institution — a nice concrete local benefit beyond the usual advancement prize; note this chapter is explicitly high-school-only (grades 9-12), unlike some sister chapters.

name: HOSA-Future Health Professionals Competitive Events Program
prerequisites: Must join a chartered HOSA chapter (school-sponsored, CTSO) and be enrolled in a health science/biomedical pathway or related coursework in most states; select and register for one competitive event per conference cycle.
costNote: Local/state HOSA chapter dues plus conference registration fees (often $20-$100 depending on level); travel costs to state/international conference add up for finalists.
cadence: School-year cycle: chapter formed in fall, regional/state conferences in winter/spring, International Leadership Conference (ILC) in June; roughly 60 competitive events across Health Science, Health Professions, Emergency Preparedness, Leadership, Teamwork, and Recognition categories; separate Middle School Division exists for grades 6-8.
howToStart: Find or start a HOSA chapter at your school via hosa.org, work with your state HOSA association for regional qualifying events, and pick an event category (e.g., Public Health, Biomedical Debate, Medical Spelling, Prepared Speaking) using the current Competitive Event Guidelines at hosa.org/guidelines.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, career-technical-education-backed pipeline into health careers that reads well for pre-med/nursing-track applicants, especially state/international finalist placements, but it is not viewed with the same rigor as ISEF/Olympiad-tier science competitions.

name: HOSA Public Health Event
prerequisites: HOSA chapter membership; team of 2-6 members; select a significant public health issue to research and present. Also offered in the Middle School Division as a Recognition/Emergency category event.
costNote: Included in HOSA chapter membership/conference fees.
cadence: Annual, aligned to HOSA conference cycle (regional/state fall-spring, ILC in June); two-round event (video trailer + live presentation).
howToStart: Register through your HOSA chapter advisor and review the Public Health ILC Guidelines PDF on hosa.org for the current topic and rubric.
a2cInsight: A genuine hands-on public-health-communication credential that demonstrates initiative on health advocacy, useful supporting evidence for a public-health or nursing application but not a headline national award on its own.

name: USMDO / IMDO - US and International Medicine & Disease Olympiad
prerequisites: US high school student (any grade 9-12), US citizen or permanent resident; strong grounding in cell biology, genetics, human physiology, and disease.
costNote: Registration fee for the USMDO exam (typically under $50); no additional cost to advance to IMDO if you qualify.
cadence: Annual online exam; the fifth annual USMDO is scheduled for August 2, 2026, with a 12-hour testing window; top scorers advance to the International Medicine and Disease Olympiad (IMDO).
howToStart: Register on usmdo.org, study using posted topic outlines, and take the 160-question, 2-hour multiple-choice exam during the open testing window.
a2cInsight: A young but growing niche olympiad that signals strong pre-med biology knowledge; respectable but still building the track record and name recognition of established science olympiads.

name: BEAMS Challenge (Biomedical Engineering Adapted for Middle Schoolers)
prerequisites: Team creates a biomedical-engineering lesson plan adaptable for grades 6-8; was open to educators, students, or mixed teams.
costNote: Free to enter via Challenge.gov; open primarily to educators/teams creating lesson plans rather than students directly.
cadence: Ran as a one-time NIH prize challenge in 2023 (submissions closed October 2023, winners announced December 2023) via Challenge.gov; no evidence of a subsequent open cycle as of mid-2026. The page now serves only as an archive distributing the winning lesson plans.
howToStart: No active submission window is currently open. Review past winning lesson plans at nibib.nih.gov/science-education/beams-challenge and monitor Challenge.gov in case NIBIB reopens the challenge.
a2cInsight: A niche, low-prestige NIH outreach prize that appears to have been a single 2023 cycle rather than a recurring competition; better suited to a teacher-led classroom project than an individual student credential, and currently not accepting new entries.

name: Teen Biotech Challenge (UC Davis)
prerequisites: US high school student (must be 16 years old by June 1 of the contest year) attending a high school within roughly 45 miles of UC Davis for in-person participation, though the online poster format is broadly promoted; design an original scientific poster in one of three categories (Agriculture & Food Science, Human & Animal Health, Platform Tools & Technologies).
costNote: No entry fee; regional in-person symposium optional for nearby students.
cadence: Annual poster-design competition; entries typically due around April 1, with an awards/poster symposium at UC Davis.
howToStart: Review instructions and past winning posters at biotech.ucdavis.edu/teen-biotech-challenge and submit a PDF poster online by the deadline.
a2cInsight: A solid, accessible regional-turned-national biotech poster contest that's a good early rung for students without lab access, though less prestigious than ISEF-affiliated fairs; note current eligibility skews toward students near UC Davis and closer to age 16, so it is less middle-school-friendly than previously listed.

name: Preventing Chronic Disease (PCD) Student Research Paper Contest
prerequisites: Original, unpublished research relevant to chronic disease prevention, screening, surveillance, or population-based intervention; open to high school, undergraduate, graduate, medical students, and recent postgraduates; requires the student to be first author and to have led the research.
costNote: No submission fee; peer-reviewed CDC journal.
cadence: Annual call for papers, typically opens in fall with a spring deadline; winners published in a special PCD collection.
howToStart: Read the current Call for Papers at cdc.gov/pcd/call_for_papers/Student_Paper_Contest.htm, prepare a manuscript following PCD's author guidelines, and submit with a cover letter and advisor recommendation.
a2cInsight: A genuinely prestigious placement for a high schooler: publication in a real, PubMed-indexed CDC-run peer-reviewed journal is a rare and admissions-impressive research credential in public health.

name: High School Health Research Challenge (HSHRF)
prerequisites: Full-time high school student (grades 9-12), any nationality; submit a written proposal for an original, feasible solution to a societal health issue.
costNote: Entry fees are typically modest (organization is a smaller nonprofit-run forum); verify current fee on the site.
cadence: Annual challenge cycle with semifinalist/finalist rounds; winner receives the Pioneer in Health Research Award and a $500 scholarship.
howToStart: Review past finalist projects and current prompt at hshrf.org/hshrf-competitions and submit a written solution proposal by the stated deadline.
a2cInsight: A newer, student-run global health forum with real judges (PhD/MD-affiliated) — legitimate and growing, but not yet a widely recognized 'brand name' the way HOSA or Regeneron are.

name: Cancer Awareness & Action Challenge (CAAC)
prerequisites: Full-time high school student (grades 9-12) attending public, private, or home school; submit an original, evidence-based proposal addressing a cancer-related community issue (prevention, access to care, awareness, or policy).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual challenge presented jointly by curingwithCARE and HSHRF; applications typically open in August with a submission deadline in early September.
howToStart: Review the current year's prompt and rules at curingwithcare.org/caac or hshrf.org/caac and submit a written proposal.
a2cInsight: A small, cause-specific challenge run by grassroots nonprofits — fine as a supplementary credential showing initiative on cancer advocacy, not a major prestige signal.

name: Speaking Up About Mental Health! National Essay Challenge (NIMH/NIMHD/NICHD)
prerequisites: US high school students ages 16-18; write a short essay (max 2 pages) on reducing mental health stigma, especially for diverse communities.
costNote: No entry fee; run via Challenge.gov by federal NIH institutes.
cadence: Recurring annual essay contest (held in 2019, 2022, 2023, and 2024 cycles), typically opens in fall/winter with a spring deadline; winners announced later in the year with cash prizes (recent pools have ranged roughly $7,000-$15,000 across multiple winners).
howToStart: Read official rules at nimhd.nih.gov/programs/edu-training/hmi/essay-contest.html and submit via the Challenge.gov portal linked from the NIMH/NIMHD site before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A federally-run (NIH) essay contest with real cash prizes and national visibility on a topic admissions officers respond well to — legitimate and easy to verify, though it's a writing/advocacy credential rather than a research one.

name: RSTMH Student Essay Competition
prerequisites: Current full-time student anywhere in the world studying global health, medicine, or a related degree with a global health component; essay must be 1,500-2,000 words plus a summary.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual, opens in March with a submission deadline in early June (£200 prize plus a year of student membership).
howToStart: Read the current essay prompt and rules at rstmh.org/medals-awards/rstmh-student-essay-competition and submit before the deadline.
a2cInsight: Prestigious within tropical medicine/global health circles (backed by a 100+ year old Royal Society), but technically aimed at university-level global-health students, so ambitious advanced high schoolers should confirm current-year eligibility before entering.

name: Worldwide CHOP Global Health Youth Arts & Essay Competition
prerequisites: Open to youth ages 12-19 worldwide; submit creative writing (essay ≤1,000 words or poem), visual art, or performing art on a global health theme, in English, French, or Spanish.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; submissions typically open in spring with a deadline around late June, tied to CHOP's Global Health Conference.
howToStart: Review the current theme and submission form at chop.edu/worldwide-chop-global-health-youth-essay-competition and email globalhealthcenter@chop.edu with questions.
a2cInsight: A genuinely global, low-barrier creative competition backed by a top pediatric hospital's global health center — good for demonstrating global health interest through the arts, but modest in prestige compared to research-based awards.

name: Health Equity Initiative School Poster Contest
prerequisites: US middle or high school student (international students also encouraged); design a poster addressing a health equity topic.
costNote: No entry fee; nonprofit-run.
cadence: Periodic/annual, announced via the Health Equity Initiative's website and past-announcements page; check current-year timing.
howToStart: Check healthequityinitiative.org/miscellaneous.html and the organization's past-announcements page for the current contest window and submission instructions.
a2cInsight: A small nonprofit's outreach contest — legitimate and easy to enter, useful for a middle schooler's first health-advocacy credential, but not nationally prestigious. Note: the org's website has had intermittent SSL/connectivity issues, so also try searching for current-year announcements if the page doesn't load.

name: Washington Tracking Network (WTN) Youth Science Contest
prerequisites: Washington State high school student (no school sponsorship required); analyze WTN public health/environmental data for a Health Science, Program & Policy Design, or Science Communications project.
costNote: No entry fee; run by Washington State Dept. of Health.
cadence: Annual; registration opens in winter with submissions due mid-April.
howToStart: Review contest tracks and past winners at doh.wa.gov/data-and-statistical-reports/washington-tracking-network-wtn/youth-science-contest and register during the open window.
a2cInsight: A well-run state-government-backed data-science/public-health contest — legitimate and rigorous for its scope, but geographically restricted to Washington State residents, limiting broader admissions signal outside the state.

name: Cancer Unwrapped Teen Writing Contest (Cancer Pathways)
prerequisites: US teen in grades 9-12; write a 500-1,500 word personal essay about experience with cancer (self or loved one).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; regional events in winter, national-level recognition in April; roughly 20 winners receive $1,000 each.
howToStart: Review submission guidelines at cancerpathways.org/event/cancer-unwrapped-contest-begins (or contact paige@cancerpathways.org) and submit an original personal essay.
a2cInsight: A meaningful personal-narrative writing contest with real cash prizes run by an established cancer-support nonprofit; strong for students with a genuine personal connection to cancer, but a niche/emotional-topic credential rather than a research one.

name: Maurice R. Hilleman Essay Contest (Vaccine Makers Project / CHOP Vaccine Education Center)
prerequisites: Students in grades 6-12 in the US or Canada (any school type, including homeschool); write a 500-1,000 word essay on the assigned vaccine-science prompt.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; opens in fall with a submission deadline typically in early February.
howToStart: Read the official rules and current prompt at hillemanfilm.com/contest and submit online or by mail before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A well-established, credible vaccine-education essay contest tied to a real virologist's legacy and a respected children's hospital program — good entry-level credential on immunology/public-health topics for younger students.

name: Lasker Foundation Essay/Video Contest
prerequisites: Open to early-career scientists and clinicians: medical/doctoral/graduate students, interns, residents, fellows, and postdocs in biomedical or health professions — not high schoolers.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; topic announced early February, winners announced mid-July. Format has shifted to a Video Contest starting 2026 (replacing the essay format), with the 2-minute video due April 2 and winners announced mid-July.
howToStart: Not currently accessible to high school students; included here for context/awareness — monitor laskerfoundation.org/education/essay-contest for any future eligibility changes.
a2cInsight: Extremely prestigious in biomedical research circles (Lasker Awards are often called 'America's Nobels'), but this specific essay/video contest is restricted to graduate-level scientists and clinicians, not accessible to secondary students.

name: International Humanitarian Law (IHL) Youth Action Campaign – JROTC Competition (American Red Cross)
prerequisites: Must be a JROTC cadet (any branch) forming a team with classmates; complete Red Cross IHL training then design and run a community outreach campaign.
costNote: Free American Red Cross training and program materials.
cadence: Annual; campaigns run January-March/April 1 deadline; national JROTC champion school announced with a trip to a Washington D.C. summit in July.
howToStart: Enroll your JROTC unit via redcross.org/humanityinwar/ihl-yac.html, complete the required IHL training modules, and register your team's campaign before the April 1 deadline.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, Red-Cross-backed civics/humanitarian-law leadership competition tightly tied to JROTC service requirements — strong for military-track or civic-engagement-focused applicants, but requires JROTC membership so it's not broadly accessible.

name: American Heart Challenge (American Heart Association)
prerequisites: High school juniors/seniors organize a school-based fundraising/awareness event (jump, dance, basketball, etc.) for heart disease and stroke; scholarship applicants must show leadership in the event.
costNote: Free to run; participating schools can additionally apply for up to $2,500 in grant funding.
cadence: Runs anytime during the school year (2-4 week turnkey program); scholarship application typically due in spring.
howToStart: Register your school at heart.org/en/professional/educator/school-programs/american-heart-challenge-middle-and-high-schools and apply for one of five annual $1,000 scholarships as a junior/senior leader.
a2cInsight: A recognizable national-nonprofit-backed service/fundraising credential (AHA is a major health brand) that reads as solid leadership-and-service evidence, though it is fundraising/awareness-focused rather than a research or academic competition.

name: National Rural Health Day Essay Contest (NMU Center for Rural Health)
prerequisites: High school seniors residing and attending school in Michigan's Upper Peninsula who intend to pursue a healthcare career; write a 500-1,000 word essay on rural healthcare challenges/solutions.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual, tied to National Rural Health Day (Nov 17); submission deadline typically late October/early November.
howToStart: Review the current prompt and rules at nmu.edu/ruralhealth/essay-contest and submit before the fall deadline.
a2cInsight: A small, well-run regional essay contest that's essentially a scholarship for Upper-Michigan seniors headed into healthcare — legitimate but hyper-local, of limited value outside that specific region.

name: Youth Medical Journal
prerequisites: Pre-college (high school, some early undergraduate) students interested in medicine/health sciences; write an original review or research article following the journal's editorial policies.
costNote: Free to submit and publish; student-run peer review.
cadence: Rolling submissions with periodic re-opening windows announced on the site; peer review handled by a volunteer student editorial team.
howToStart: Read the editorial and publishing policies at youthmedicaljournal.com before submitting a manuscript through their online portal.
a2cInsight: A real, actively-publishing student-run outlet that gives medically-inclined students a low-barrier byline, but since review is by fellow students rather than credentialed scientists, admissions officers weight it below faculty-reviewed or PubMed-indexed journals.

name: Penn State EPIC Epi Challenge (Think Like an Epidemiologist Challenge)
prerequisites: Enrollment limited to students at specific partner high schools in the Harrisburg/central PA area (e.g., Middletown, Cedar Cliff, Lower Dauphin, John Harris, Sci Tech).
costNote: Free; funded by an NIH grant to Penn State/partner high schools.
cadence: Year-long, project-based program run in partnership with a handful of central Pennsylvania high schools; not a standing open-enrollment national contest.
howToStart: Ask your science teacher whether your school participates in the Penn State College of Medicine's EPIC/Epi Challenge program; see the program overview at pennstatehealthnews.org (search 'Epi Challenge') or contact the Department of Public Health Sciences at PennStatePublicHealth@pennstatehealth.psu.edu.
a2cInsight: A well-designed, NIH-funded epidemiology pipeline program run out of Penn State College of Medicine (Hershey), but access is restricted to a small set of partner high schools in central Pennsylvania — not something a student elsewhere can simply sign up for. Note: the originally-cited schuylkill.psu.edu URL is for an unrelated Penn State Schuylkill program that coincidentally shares the 'EPIC' name — corrected here to point to verified coverage of the actual epidemiology program.

name: Medic Mentor National Medical Essay and Art Competitions
prerequisites: UK secondary school students (any year) interested in medicine, veterinary medicine, or dentistry; submit an essay or artwork on the quarterly theme.
costNote: No entry fee; winners published in Mentor Magazine.
cadence: Runs four times per year (quarterly rolling themes/deadlines).
howToStart: Check the current theme and deadline at medicmentor.org/national-medical-essay-and-art-competitions and submit via their online form.
a2cInsight: A UK-specific pre-med enrichment competition tied to a scholarship-granting mentoring organization — useful for UK applicants building a medicine-school portfolio, but not really on US admissions officers' radar.

name: Dental Mentor National Dental Essay and Art Competitions
prerequisites: UK secondary school students interested in dentistry; submit an essay or artwork on the quarterly theme.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Runs four times per year (quarterly rolling themes/deadlines), sister competition to Medic Mentor.
howToStart: Check the current theme and deadline at dental-mentor.org/national-medical-essay-and-art-competitions and submit via their online form.
a2cInsight: Same structure and audience as its sister Medic Mentor contest but dentistry-focused — a nice-to-have credential for aspiring UK dental students, low broader prestige.

name: New Jersey Vaccine Awareness Contest
prerequisites: New Jersey students in grades 5-12; design a poster or 30-second video promoting vaccine awareness (e.g., HPV, flu, meningococcal, COVID-19, Tdap).
costNote: No entry fee; run by the Partnership for Maternal and Child Health of Northern New Jersey in collaboration with the NJ Dept. of Health.
cadence: Annual; submissions open October 1, deadline January 31, finalists selected March, awards ceremony in May around National Infant Immunization Week.
howToStart: Visit the official contest site at protectmewith3.com for current-year rules, topics, and submission portal.
a2cInsight: A small, state-run public-health-communication contest (branded 'Protect Me With 3+') — decent low-stakes entry point for a NJ middle/high schooler's first health-advocacy credential, no broader national recognition.

name: USA Earth Science Olympiad (USESO)
prerequisites: Under-19 US student with strong geology/meteorology/astronomy/oceanography background; individual entry via national qualifying exam.
costNote: Open Exam registration is low-cost; selected students attend a residential summer Training Camp (around $2,600, need-based financial aid available).
cadence: National Open Exam registration opens around December, exam window in spring (March); Training Camp for the top 40 students held in June to select the IESO team.
howToStart: Register for the USESO National Open Exam at useso.org and begin studying IESO-style earth science content (geology, astronomy, meteorology, environmental science).
a2cInsight: Making Team USA for IESO via USESO is a genuinely elite, internationally-benchmarked Earth science credential comparable in rigor to other national science olympiad teams.

name: International Earth Science Olympiad (IESO)
prerequisites: Under-19 student selected onto a national team (e.g., via USESO in the US) through a qualifying process.
costNote: Travel/accommodation for the international event is typically covered by national delegations or sponsors once selected.
cadence: Annual international event, usually August-October, hosted by a different country each year.
howToStart: Qualify through your country's national Earth science olympiad selection program (in the US, this is USESO); no direct individual application to IESO itself.
a2cInsight: One of the internationally recognized Science Olympiads — an IESO medal is elite-tier and immediately legible to selective admissions committees worldwide.

name: NASA App Development Challenge (ADC)
prerequisites: High school or community college team; basic app/software development skills to respond to a NASA-issued technical problem for deep space exploration.
costNote: No entry fee; winning teams earn a trip to NASA Johnson Space Center.
cadence: Was annual (fall proposal, spring build); paused for the 2026 cycle with a possible return in 2027.
howToStart: Monitor nasa.gov/learning-resources/app-development-challenge for the challenge to reopen, then form a team to respond to the annual technical problem statement.
a2cInsight: A NASA-branded coding challenge that signals applied software skills tied to real space-exploration problems, though its current pause reduces near-term reliability as a target.

name: NASA HUNCH (High Schools United with NASA to Create Hardware)
prerequisites: Enrolled high school student at a participating HUNCH partner school; teacher/school sign-up required.
costNote: School must join as a HUNCH partner; costs vary by project track (hardware, software, culinary, design/prototyping).
cadence: Year-long school-based program aligned to the academic calendar; culminates in spring project reviews at NASA centers.
howToStart: Have your teacher/school register as a NASA HUNCH partner at nasahunch.com and join a project track (hardware, sewing, culinary, design & prototyping, or software).
a2cInsight: HUNCH work that ships real flight-qualified hardware to the ISS is a strong, tangible engineering credential — especially compelling for engineering-school applications.

name: CanSat Competition (AAS/AIAA)
prerequisites: Primarily university/college teams (some advanced high school teams have entered via affiliated programs); strong aerospace engineering, electronics, and programming skills to build a can-sized simulated satellite.
costNote: Registration fee plus hardware/materials costs and travel to the final launch event; typically several hundred to a few thousand dollars per team.
cadence: Two-semester competition; kickoff in fall, Preliminary Design Review and Critical Design Review through the year, final launch competition in June.
howToStart: Form a team (advisor required), review the CanSat competition guide at cansatcompetition.com, and submit a Preliminary Design Review proposal by the fall deadline.
a2cInsight: A genuine end-to-end space-hardware build-and-launch competition; the university-level competitiveness makes it elite-tier for the rare high schooler who can field a competitive team.

name: American Rocketry Challenge (formerly Team America Rocketry Challenge, TARC)
prerequisites: Team of 3-10 students grades 6-12; design, build, and fly a model rocket to precise altitude/duration specs carrying a raw-egg or payload.
costNote: Rocket materials, entry fee, and travel to the National Fly-Off (near Washington, DC) if a team qualifies.
cadence: Annual; team qualification flights in early spring, National Fly-Off in May/June.
howToStart: Register your team at rocketrychallenge.org, obtain the year's rules packet, and begin design/build/test flights to qualify for the National Fly-Off.
a2cInsight: The largest model-rocket contest in the US — a strong, accessible engineering credential that also feeds directly into NASA Student Launch for top finishers.

name: NASA Human Exploration Rover Challenge (HERC)
prerequisites: Middle school (ages 11-14, RC division), high school, college, or international team; propose and build a human- or remote-powered rover to traverse simulated lunar/Martian terrain.
costNote: Materials/build costs plus travel to the final event at Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, AL.
cadence: Nine-month challenge; proposals due in fall, culminating event (Huntsville, AL) in spring.
howToStart: Submit a proposal in response to NASA's annual HERC call for proposals via nasa.gov/learning-resources/nasa-human-exploration-rover-challenge; recently expanded eligibility to middle schools via a remote-control division.
a2cInsight: A hands-on, decades-running NASA engineering competition with real alumni now working at NASA/aerospace firms — strong signal for engineering-track applicants.

name: NASA TechRise Student Challenge
prerequisites: US teams in grades 6-12 (student teams of any size with a teacher/mentor); propose a science/technology experiment to fly on a high-altitude balloon or suborbital vehicle.
costNote: Free to enter; winning teams receive a $1,500 stipend and a flight test slot for their experiment.
cadence: Annual; proposals typically due in fall, winners announced in winter (January), flight tests the following spring.
howToStart: Submit a proposal via futureengineers.org/nasatechrise during the open submission window (next challenge expected to open around September 2026).
a2cInsight: A genuinely rare opportunity for middle/high schoolers to fly real experiments on NASA-sponsored suborbital platforms — high novelty value for a STEM application narrative.

name: Zero Robotics High School Tournament
prerequisites: High school team (grades 9-12), 5-10 students with an adult mentor; program real Astrobee/SPHERES satellites aboard the ISS using a block- or Java-based coding environment to solve an annual game challenge.
costNote: Free team registration for US high schools and ESA-member-country teams; no fees at any stage.
cadence: Annual; registration in fall (through November), online qualifying rounds through winter, championship aboard the ISS in winter/spring.
howToStart: Register a team at zerorobotics.mit.edu when the annual competition opens and begin working through the online programming tutorials.
a2cInsight: A rare, MIT/NASA-run competition where code actually runs on hardware aboard the ISS — a distinctive, highly legible programming + space credential.

name: Spaceport America Cup (Intercollegiate Rocket Engineering Competition)
prerequisites: Primarily university/college teams (some advanced pre-college teams have competed via academic sponsorship); design, build, and launch solid/hybrid/liquid rockets to 10,000 or 30,000 ft targets.
costNote: Team registration fee plus rocket build costs (often thousands of dollars) and travel to the launch event (recent venue: Midland, TX).
cadence: Annual; team registration and design reviews through the year, culminating in launch week in June.
howToStart: Register a team through the Experimental Sounding Rocket Association (ESRA) at soundingrocket.org and begin the Preliminary Design Review process.
a2cInsight: The world's largest intercollegiate rocket-engineering competition; exceptionally advanced and mostly out of reach pre-college, but a strong aspirational target for elite rocketry teams.

name: GLOBE Program (Global Learning and Observations to Benefit the Environment)
prerequisites: K-12 student at a GLOBE-registered school or through a GLOBE-trained educator; collect and submit real environmental data (atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere, soil).
costNote: Free to participate; schools/teachers register to access the protocols and data system.
cadence: Rolling participation year-round; specific Student Research Campaigns run on their own annual cycles with an annual Virtual Science Symposium (VSS, formerly IVSS).
howToStart: Have your teacher complete GLOBE training via globe.gov, then begin collecting and submitting data using approved protocols; consider entering the annual Virtual Science Symposium.
a2cInsight: A legitimate NASA-linked citizen-science data program; participation alone is modest, but a strong independent research project built on GLOBE data and presented at the symposium can be a genuine research credential.

name: NASA Lunabotics Junior Contest
prerequisites: K-12 student (individual entry, US public/private/home schools); create a visual design (drawing, 3D model, diagram, or photo of a prototype) and short written summary (150 words) of a lunar-regolith-digging robot concept, divided into K-5 and 6-12 categories.
costNote: Free to enter; no travel required.
cadence: Annual, typically fall submission window with winners announced in winter.
howToStart: Submit a robot design and written summary via futureengineers.org/lunaboticsjunior during the open window.
a2cInsight: A low-barrier, NASA-branded entry point into aerospace engineering thinking for younger students — good for building an early STEM portfolio, not a standalone admissions differentiator.

name: NASA Student Launch
prerequisites: Middle/high school teams must place top-25 in American Rocketry Challenge or top-3 in Rockets for Schools Class II to qualify (plus complete the Advanced Rocketry Workshop); college teams apply directly. Design, build, and launch a high-powered rocket to 4,000-6,000 ft with a scientific/engineering payload.
costNote: Rocket/payload build costs plus travel to Huntsville, AL for launch week; middle/high school entry requires qualifying via TARC/Rockets for Schools first.
cadence: Nine-month challenge; proposals due fall, launch week near Huntsville, AL each spring (25+ years running).
howToStart: Qualify through American Rocketry Challenge (grades 6-12) or apply directly (college), then submit a proposal via nasa.gov/learning-resources/nasa-student-launch.
a2cInsight: A genuine step up from TARC in engineering rigor and NASA mentorship — strong signal of sustained, advanced rocketry/engineering commitment.

name: NASA Power to Explore STEM Writing Challenge
prerequisites: K-12 student (US); write a short essay (~275 words) imagining a space mission powered by radioisotope power systems (RPS).
costNote: Free to enter; winners receive prizes including a NASA visitor center experience.
cadence: Annual; submission window typically fall through early winter, winners announced in spring.
howToStart: Review the challenge prompt and submit an essay via futureengineers.org/powertoexplore during the open window.
a2cInsight: A friendly, low-barrier NASA essay contest good for younger students building an early space-science interest narrative; not a heavyweight credential for older applicants.

name: Earth Science Week Contests (AGI: Photography, Visual Arts, Essay)
prerequisites: Photography contest open to any age worldwide; Visual Arts contest for grades K-5 (US); Essay contest for grades 6-9 (US) on the year's Earth Science Week theme.
costNote: Free to enter; cash prizes (e.g., $300 first place) plus AGI publications.
cadence: Annual, tied to Earth Science Week in October; submission deadlines typically in early-to-mid October.
howToStart: Review the current year's theme and rules at earthsciweek.org/contests and submit an original photo, artwork, or essay by the deadline.
a2cInsight: A pleasant, low-stakes way to engage younger students with geoscience themes; minor credential weight but a fine on-ramp to a bigger earth-science portfolio.

name: NSS Gerard K. O'Neill Space Settlement Contest
prerequisites: Students grades 6-12 (ages ~11-18) worldwide; individually or in teams of 2-12, design a free-space human settlement and submit a written/illustrated proposal (up to 50 pages).
costNote: Free to enter; some regional prize tracks offer cash or scholarship awards.
cadence: Annual; entries typically due in spring (around March-April).
howToStart: Review the official rules at nss.org/nss-space-settlement-contest and begin drafting a space settlement design proposal addressing engineering, sociology, and economics.
a2cInsight: A long-running (since 1980s/1994), legitimate design-and-writing competition that rewards interdisciplinary space-settlement thinking; respected within space-advocacy circles though less known in mainstream admissions.

name: SEDS SpaceVision Conference & Research Symposium
prerequisites: Primarily college students via SEDS chapters, though ambitious advanced high schoolers with space-research projects have presented at the Research Symposium track.
costNote: Registration fee (~$90+ for students) plus travel/lodging to the host city.
cadence: Annual, typically held in fall (October/November).
howToStart: Check spacevision.seds.org for the annual call for research symposium abstracts and conference registration.
a2cInsight: A genuine student-run space conference with a research presentation track — valuable networking and presentation experience, though it's college-oriented and not a formal 'competition' with rankings.

name: Dream With Us: High School Engineering Design Challenge (FAA/NASA)
prerequisites: Team of high school students grades 9-12 with a teacher advisor; solve a real-world aviation/aeronautics engineering problem defined by FAA/NASA.
costNote: Free to enter; requires a teacher/mentor to register the team.
cadence: Annual, following the school year calendar with a spring capstone event.
howToStart: Have a teacher register the team via faa.gov/education/programs/highschool/design_challenge when the annual challenge opens.
a2cInsight: A solid FAA/NASA-backed aeronautics design challenge, now in its 4th+ annual cycle (2025-2026 cohort completed); less internationally known than TARC or HERC but a legitimate applied-engineering credential.

name: CTE Mission: CubeSat Challenge
prerequisites: Was open to high school CTE (Career and Technical Education) classes/teams, who designed and built a CubeSat prototype addressing a real mission scenario.
costNote: Was free to enter; classroom kits/materials involved some school-funded cost when it ran.
cadence: One-time national challenge (launched August 2020 by the U.S. Department of Education; concluded with a Flight Week in spring 2021). Not held again in subsequent years.
howToStart: The official challenge has ended and is not accepting new registrations; the archived site (ctemissioncubesat.com) documents the completed challenge and can inspire a self-directed CubeSat project, but this is no longer an active competition to enter.
a2cInsight: This was a single-round US Department of Education CubeSat design challenge, not a recurring annual program — do not present it as an ongoing competition. Still citable as a past hands-on space-hardware credential if a student actually participated in 2020-2021, but current students cannot enter it.

name: U.S. Youth Aviation Art Contest (NASAO/NAA)
prerequisites: Student ages 6-17 (three age categories); create original artwork on the year's aviation theme.
costNote: Free to enter; top US entries advance to the international FAI Young Artists aviation art competition.
cadence: Annual; state-level submissions typically due in early winter, national winners announced in spring.
howToStart: Check nasao.org for the current year's theme and submit artwork through your state aviation office by the deadline.
a2cInsight: A pleasant, accessible aviation-themed art contest run with the National Aeronautic Association; nice for an arts+STEM narrative but minor prestige weight.

name: American Meteorological Society Weather Challenge / Pre-College Programs
prerequisites: High school student, typically via a local AMS pre-college chapter (minimum 5 students plus a teacher/adult advisor to petition for a chapter); forecast weather variables (temperature, precipitation, wind) and compare accuracy against peers.
costNote: Free participation through AMS pre-college and local chapters; individual chapter weather-forecasting challenges have modest or no fees.
cadence: Ongoing through local AMS student/pre-college chapters; individual chapter weather challenges run on their own schedules (often monthly during the school year).
howToStart: Find or start an AMS pre-college chapter at your school via ametsoc.org/ams/index.cfm/information-for/students/, then participate in chapter-run weather forecasting challenges.
a2cInsight: Informal, chapter-run forecasting practice that builds real meteorology skill; useful for a passion narrative but carries limited standalone prestige since it's locally organized rather than a single national competition.

name: International Astronomy Olympiad (IAO)
prerequisites: Secondary school students (roughly ages 14-18); qualification usually via a national astronomy society or club.
costNote: Costs vary by host country/region; typically modest registration and travel costs for regional/national qualifying rounds.
cadence: Annual, usually held in October-November; founded 1996 by the Euro-Asian Astronomical Society. The 29th IAO was held in 2025.
howToStart: Contact your country's national astronomy society (many countries have IAO national coordinators) or check the IAO site for participating-country contacts.
a2cInsight: A respected but somewhat lower-profile sibling to IOAA (they split from a common origin); still a legitimate international science olympiad medal, especially valuable for students outside the US/UK who lack USAAAO/BAAO pipelines.

name: Canadian Astronomy Olympiad (CAAO)
prerequisites: Canadian secondary (and some junior) students; no prerequisite coursework, self-study from posted tutorials recommended.
costNote: Free to participate; run by the Canadian Association of Amateur Astronomers (Astro Society).
cadence: Annual; problems posted in spring with about one month to submit solutions; junior and senior divisions since 2022.
howToStart: Visit caao.ca, download the CAO tutorial/prep materials, and register to receive the annual problem set.
a2cInsight: Canada's official IOAA/IOAA-Jr team selection exam -- a clean, free way for Canadian students to build an astrophysics olympiad credential from home.

name: Asian-Pacific Astronomy Olympiad (APAO)
prerequisites: Middle and high school students (ages ~14-18) from participating Asia-Pacific countries, selected via national astronomy societies.
costNote: Costs vary by host country and depend on national delegation funding; individual students typically enter via a national team.
cadence: Annual, hosted by a different Asia-Pacific member country each year (usually held November-December); includes theoretical, observational, and practical rounds.
howToStart: Check whether your country's national astronomy society or planetarium (e.g., Beijing Planetarium for China) sends a team; regional students typically need a national coordinator.
a2cInsight: A solid regional-tier international olympiad, useful for Asia-Pacific students without easy access to IOAA training pipelines, though less globally recognized than IOAA itself.

name: Nepal National Astronomy Olympiad (NAO)
prerequisites: Nepali students in class 8-12.
costNote: Low provincial-round registration fee set by the Nepal Astronomical Society (NASO); later stages are free.
cadence: Annual, three stages (provincial, national, final round); feeds Nepal's teams to IOAA, IOAA-Jr, IAO, and APAO. The 13th NAO is set for 2026.
howToStart: Register through NASO (Nepal Astronomical Society) for the provincial entrance exam.
a2cInsight: A legitimate national feeder olympiad; primarily useful signal for students applying within Nepal or citing it as evidence of sustained STEM engagement, less recognized internationally on its own.

name: Bangladesh Olympiad on Astronomy and Astrophysics (BDOAA)
prerequisites: Bangladeshi secondary school students.
costNote: Low registration fee for the national rounds; travel funding for international stages typically organized by BDOAA.
cadence: Annual national rounds feeding into IOAA, IOAA-Jr, and IAO team selection; the 9th BDOAA is set for 2026.
howToStart: Register through the BDOAA official website for the national qualifying exam.
a2cInsight: Bangladesh's official astronomy olympiad pipeline; a solid regional/national credential, most impactful when a student advances to represent Bangladesh internationally.

name: China National Astronomy Olympiad (CNAO)
prerequisites: Chinese secondary school students; entry typically through school or provincial science offices.
costNote: Provincial and national rounds have modest registration fees set by the Beijing Planetarium/Chinese Astronomical Society.
cadence: Annual; provincial rounds feed into the national CNAO, which selects China's teams for IOAA, IAO, and APAO.
howToStart: Contact the Beijing Planetarium (organizer, recognized by China's Ministry of Education as a national competition since 2019) or a school science coordinator for registration details.
a2cInsight: One of the largest astronomy olympiad feeder systems in the world; an official Ministry-of-Education-recognized competition, valuable for students applying within China's own admissions system as well as abroad.

name: Copernicus Olympiad -- Physics & Astronomy
prerequisites: Open to students grades 7-12 worldwide; no specific prerequisite coursework.
costNote: Registration fee applies per student for the Preliminary Round; check current pricing on site.
cadence: Annual; Preliminary Round followed by a Global Round; open internationally, grades 7-12 for the physics/astronomy track.
howToStart: Register directly online at copernicusolympiad.us for the Physics and Astronomy Olympiad track.
a2cInsight: A younger, US-based international olympiad (founded 2020) that is growing but not yet as widely recognized by elite admissions offices as IOAA/BAAO; useful as an accessible on-ramp and for building a competition track record.

name: National Astronomy STEM & Astrophysics Competition (NASAC)
prerequisites: Open internationally to students grades 1-12; exams tailored to grade level.
costNote: Registration fee per student or school; specific pricing listed on the official registration page (nasac.in/fee).
cadence: Annual competition open to grades 1-12, schools of any country/curriculum; promoted via Sky & Telescope's events listing.
howToStart: Register individually or through a school at nasac.in.
a2cInsight: A newer, broad-access astronomy competition without the elite selectivity of IOAA/USAAAO; reasonable for building an early astronomy portfolio but carries less admissions weight than olympiad-track competitions.

name: Duke Math Meet (DMM)
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; teams of six students.
costNote: Team registration fee varies by year (free in some years thanks to sponsors); travel to Duke's Durham, NC campus; field limited to roughly 45 teams of 6.
cadence: Annual, held in the fall on Duke's campus.
howToStart: Register a team via dukemathmeet.org when fall registration opens; early registration recommended due to field cap.
a2cInsight: A respected regional tournament run by Duke's undergrad math union (DUMU) - solid credential-builder, though capped size limits its national visibility relative to HMMT/PUMaC.

name: Rice Math Tournament (RMT)
ageNote: No confirmed activity since the cancelled 2018 edition.
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; individual written tests plus group events (historical format).
costNote: Historically a low registration fee plus travel to Rice's Houston campus; not currently running so no active cost applies.
cadence: Formerly annual (spring, around February); the 2018 edition was cancelled due to lack of organizer capacity, and no evidence of the tournament resuming since.
howToStart: Not currently recruiting; the last confirmed announcement (2018) was on the tournament's own page, now stale. Verify directly with Rice's math department before pursuing.
a2cInsight: One of the longest-running university tournaments (since 1981), but reporting confirms the 2018 edition was cancelled for lack of organizer manpower and no results or announcements exist for any year since - treat as defunct rather than a live opportunity.

name: Michigan Math Meet (MMM)
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; five-round format including power, individual, team, and tiebreaker rounds.
costNote: Low registration fee plus travel to Ann Arbor, MI.
cadence: Annual, typically held in April at the University of Michigan.
howToStart: Register via michiganmathmeet.org when the spring event page opens.
a2cInsight: A solid regional tournament with Midwest reach - useful credential-builder for students without easy access to coastal tournaments like HMMT or PUMaC.

name: Michigan Lemma
ageNote: First held January 2020; recent-year continuity not independently confirmed in this research pass.
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; teams of four.
costNote: Low registration fee for the one-day, in-person event at University of Michigan.
cadence: Annual (as of last confirmed sitting in January 2020); current operating status should be verified for the latest year.
howToStart: Check websites.umich.edu/~michiganlemma for the current year's announcement before assuming the event is running.
a2cInsight: A smaller, newer Michigan-based tournament with limited track record - useful as a regional stepping stone but not yet a widely recognized national credential.

name: Cornell Big Red Math Competition (CUBRMC)
ageNote: Founded in 2023, so has a short track record relative to older tournaments.
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; founded 2023 by Cornell's Math Club and math department; teams of up to 5.
costNote: Low registration fee plus travel to Cornell's Ithaca, NY campus.
cadence: Annual, typically held in the fall (late October).
howToStart: Register via bigredmathcomp.org when the fall registration window opens.
a2cInsight: A newer entrant (founded 2023) to the collegiate tournament circuit - legitimate and growing (hundreds of participants by 2025), but not yet as established or selective as HMMT/PUMaC-tier events.

name: Georgia Tech High School Math Day / Competition (HSMD)
prerequisites: Open to a wide range of skill levels; covers algebra, geometry, combinatorics, number theory, and basic calculus.
costNote: Low/free registration; travel to Georgia Tech's Atlanta campus.
cadence: Annual, typically held in spring (March) on Georgia Tech's campus.
howToStart: Register via hsmd.math.gatech.edu when the event announcement is posted each winter.
a2cInsight: A welcoming, broad-appeal math festival/competition rather than an elite proving ground - good for building enthusiasm and a first competitive result, less impactful for top-tier admissions differentiation.

name: Southern California Mathematics Competition (SCMC, USC)
ageNote: Founded in 2022.
prerequisites: Open to a wide range of high-school math abilities; includes team, individual, and a lightning round.
costNote: Low registration fee; travel to USC's University Park campus, or compete online.
cadence: Twice yearly - fall (late October) and spring (late March) on USC's campus, plus an online option.
howToStart: Register via engage.usc.edu/scmathcompetition when the fall or spring window opens.
a2cInsight: A newer (2022-founded) regional Southern California tournament - legitimate community-building credential, though still building national name recognition.

name: Brown University Math Olympiad (BrUMO)
ageNote: First held in 2025; very limited track record so far.
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background; olympiad-style proof problems alongside standard rounds; teams of 4-6, grade 12 or below.
costNote: No registration fee; teams cover their own travel/housing to Brown's Providence, RI campus.
cadence: Annual, inaugural edition held April 2025; second edition Feb 2026, typically held in late winter/spring.
howToStart: Watch brumo.org for the current year's registration announcement.
a2cInsight: A very new but well-organized tournament (inaugural 2025, growing to ~350 students/60-75 teams by 2026) backed by Brown's Applied Math department - promising trajectory, but too new to carry heavy prestige yet.

name: Columbia Math Tournament / Columbia MMATHS
prerequisites: Competitive high-school math background, primarily drawing from the NY/NJ region.
costNote: Low registration fee plus travel to Columbia's NYC campus.
cadence: Two tournaments/year - Columbia Math Tournament in fall, Columbia MMATHS in spring.
howToStart: Check outreach.engineering.columbia.edu/content/columbia-university-competitions-math for the current year's registration links.
a2cInsight: A respected regional tournament run by Columbia undergrads - good for NY/NJ-area students building a competitive math resume without the travel of HMMT/PUMaC.

name: ARML (American Regions Mathematics League)
prerequisites: Selection onto a 15-member regional or school team, typically via a school math team or a state/regional ARML organization; strong AIME-level competition math skill expected.
costNote: On-site teams: ~$600/team plus ~$85/person for housing and meals at one of the host-university sites (e.g., Penn State, University of Iowa, UNLV); off-site teams: ~$150/team. Costs vary by regional team organization.
cadence: Annual, held simultaneously at multiple US university sites in late spring (May/June).
howToStart: Join a school or regional ARML team (contact a local team captain/coordinator - team formation usually happens through math clubs) via the official ARML site.
a2cInsight: A longstanding, highly respected national team competition - selection onto a regional ARML team is itself a notable signal of top-tier math ability, even before results are counted.

name: New York State Mathematics League (NYSML)
prerequisites: Selection onto a 15-member team representing a NY school or region; same format as ARML (team, power, individual, relay rounds).
costNote: Low team fee; travel to the annual host high school/site within New York State.
cadence: Annual, typically held in April at a rotating New York location.
howToStart: Join via a school math team or regional coordinator; check nysml.com for the current year's date and host site.
a2cInsight: New York's answer to ARML - a strong regional team credential, especially meaningful within NY/NJ admissions contexts but less nationally recognized than ARML itself.

name: MIT Integration Bee
ageNote: Open only to enrolled MIT students, not external high schoolers.
