costNote: Free to enter through participating schools; run by the South African Mathematics Foundation (SAMF).
cadence: Annual, multi-round: first round in March, second round in May, third (final) round in July.
howToStart: Register through your school's math teacher for the first round of SAMO; top performers advance through subsequent rounds and can be selected for South Africa's IMO/PAMO training squad.
a2cInsight: South Africa's premier national olympiad and direct feeder to its IMO and PAMO teams; a legitimate, well-run national credential, though its recognition value is strongest within South Africa and among African universities.

name: Kenya Mathematics Olympiad (KMO)
prerequisites: Enrolled Kenyan Junior School (grade 7+) or Senior/Secondary School student; advances through successive merit-based rounds.
costNote: Free registration through CEMASTEA's official portal.
cadence: Annual; registration typically closes in February, with subsequent rounds through the year.
howToStart: Register through kmo.cemastea.ac.ke via your school; top performers can progress toward representing Kenya at PAMO, the East African Mathematical Olympiad (EAMO), or the IMO.
a2cInsight: A solid, growing national pipeline for Kenyan students aiming at continental/international olympiads; legitimate but with limited name recognition outside East Africa.

name: Philippine Mathematical Olympiad (PMO)
prerequisites: Enrolled Filipino secondary school student; open entry at the qualifying round via school registration.
costNote: Modest registration fee for the qualifying round, usually covered through school registration.
cadence: Annual, multi-stage: qualifying round, area stage, and national finals typically held between October and March.
howToStart: Register through your school (or as an independent candidate where allowed) for the PMO qualifying round via pmo.ph; top scorers advance to area finals and the national finals, feeding the Philippines' IMO team selection.
a2cInsight: The oldest and most prestigious secondary math competition in the Philippines and the country's official IMO pipeline; a real, competitive national credential though not broadly known outside the Philippines.

name: Olimpiada Mexicana de Matematicas (OMM)
prerequisites: Enrolled Mexican student under the OMM age/grade limit (roughly through the equivalent of grade 12); entry begins at the state level.
costNote: Free, run through state-level committees under the Sociedad Matematica Mexicana.
cadence: Annual; state-level selection rounds run through the year, culminating in the national contest typically in November.
howToStart: Contact your state's OMM delegation (listed at ommenlinea.org) to enter local qualifying rounds; top state performers compete at the national olympiad and can be selected for Mexico's IMO, OIM, and OMCC teams.
a2cInsight: Mexico's flagship olympiad and the direct pipeline to its IMO/Ibero-American/Central American teams; well established domestically and a credible signal for Mexican and international university admissions.

name: Olimpiada Brasileira de Matematica das Escolas Publicas (OBMEP)
prerequisites: Enrolled student (grades 6-12 equivalent) at a Brazilian public school (private-school participation is limited; a separate OBM olympiad is the main route for private-school students).
costNote: Free; funded by the Brazilian federal government (MEC/MCTI), administered through schools via IMPA.
cadence: Annual; first round typically in June, second round in October, results/medals in early the following year.
howToStart: Ask your public school to register for OBMEP (schools enroll centrally, not individual students); strong performers earn medals and scholarships and can progress toward OBM/IMO team consideration.
a2cInsight: One of the largest school olympiads in the world by participation (millions of Brazilian students annually), making a medal genuinely selective despite the huge base; primarily a Brazil-domestic credential with a scholarship/social-mobility angle.

name: Olimpiada Brasileira de Matematica (OBM)
prerequisites: Enrolled Brazilian student (grades 6-12 equivalent) at any school, public or private.
costNote: Free; run by the Sociedade Brasileira de Matematica (SBM), open to all Brazilian schools including private.
cadence: Annual, multi-round competition typically running through the school year with national finals selecting IMO/OIM team candidates.
howToStart: Register through your school for the OBM (distinct from the public-school-focused OBMEP); top national performers are invited to training squads for Brazil's IMO, Ibero-American, and OMCC teams.
a2cInsight: Brazil's original and most competitive national olympiad, the direct feeder to its strong IMO teams; a credible, rigorous credential within Brazil and Latin America, less familiar to US admissions readers.

name: Bundeswettbewerb Mathematik (German Federal Mathematics Competition)
prerequisites: Enrolled German student (all grade levels can attempt, though realistically grade 8+ for meaningful results); open entry, no nomination required.
costNote: Free; run by Bildung & Begabung gGmbH with German federal government support.
cadence: Annual, three rounds: two homework-based written rounds and a final oral mathematical colloquium.
howToStart: Register directly online at mathe-wettbewerbe.de and submit solutions to the first round's take-home problem set; strong performers advance to the final round and are considered for Germany's IMO training squad.
a2cInsight: Germany's most prestigious school mathematics competition and a core part of its IMO pipeline; the unique take-home/oral format rewards sustained proof-writing skill, but its recognition is mostly limited to German-speaking academia.

name: UKMT Junior Mathematical Challenge (JMC)
prerequisites: Open to Year 8 and below in England/Wales (S2 and below Scotland; Year 9 and below NI); must be entered through a school.
costNote: Entry fee per pupil charged to school, typically a few pounds; individuals usually enter via their school.
cadence: Annual, held late April/early May; 2027 sitting Wed 5 May 2027.
howToStart: Ask your school's maths department to register for UKMT Junior Challenges via ukmt.org.uk; individual home-educated students can also be entered by a registered centre.
a2cInsight: A solid, low-stakes way to show early quantitative aptitude on a UK-recognized scale, but Bronze/Silver/Gold alone won't move the needle for competitive US/UK admissions without follow-on qualification (Olympiad/Kangaroo).

name: UKMT Junior Mathematical Olympiad (JMO)
prerequisites: By invitation: top ~1,200 JMC scorers nationally; requires full written proof-style solutions to 6 problems in 2 hours.
costNote: Entry fee charged per pupil via school; small cost beyond JMC.
cadence: Annual, June, following qualification from the JMC.
howToStart: Score highly on the Junior Mathematical Challenge; qualifying students are automatically invited by their school via UKMT.
a2cInsight: A genuine early-proof-writing credential that distinguishes a student as a serious young mathematician; a Distinction here is a meaningful, checkable signal for selective UK sixth-form math tracks and enrichment programs.

name: UKMT Junior Kangaroo
prerequisites: Qualification via JMC score (mid-tier scorers) or discretionary school entry; UK schools only.
costNote: £4 per entry for discretionary entries; automatic qualifiers are free.
cadence: Annual, shortly after the JMC (spring).
howToStart: Achieve a qualifying score on the Junior Mathematical Challenge, or ask your school to purchase discretionary entries.
a2cInsight: A pleasant mid-tier follow-on round linked to the worldwide Kangaroo network; nice for building momentum but not a standout credential on its own.

name: UKMT Primary Kangaroo
prerequisites: Year 5/6 or below in England/Wales (P6/P7 or below Scotland/NI); entered via school.
costNote: £10 per 10 pupils entry fee via school; piloted in 2025 with wide launch continuing in 2026.
cadence: Annual; piloted in 2025 (nearly 3,000 students, 68 schools), now an established annual UKMT competition for ages 9-11 held in spring.
howToStart: Ask your primary or feeder school to register for the UKMT Primary Kangaroo via ukmt.org.uk.
a2cInsight: Too young for admissions relevance; purely a fun early exposure and pipeline-builder into JMC/Kangaroo circuit later on.

name: Primary Maths Challenge (PMC)
prerequisites: UK (and international) pupils aged 9-11; open to schools and home-schooled pupils.
costNote: Paid per-pupil: online access, PDF papers, or physical packs sold in sets of 10 through The Mathematical Association.
cadence: Annual; main challenge held in November, with a Bonus Round for qualifiers in January.
howToStart: Have your school (or home-education group) order Primary Maths Challenge packs through primarymathschallenge.org.uk, run by The Mathematical Association (not UKMT); top scorers are invited to the Bonus Round.
a2cInsight: Elementary-level enrichment with no admissions weight; value is purely in early engagement and confidence-building. Note this is run by The Mathematical Association, a separate organization from UKMT, and is distinct from (not a UKMT product like) the newer UKMT Primary Kangaroo.

name: International Philosophy Olympiad (IPO)
prerequisites: Must qualify through a national philosophy essay competition/olympiad in your home country; write an original essay on a philosophical theme under exam conditions. Must be enrolled in upper-secondary/high school (or the year just after, in some systems); max age 20.
costNote: National qualification is usually free; IPO travel costs vary by country and are often subsidized by national organizing bodies.
cadence: Annual international final (typically May); national qualifying rounds vary by country and occur earlier in the year. The 2026 edition (XXXIV IPO) was held in Warsaw, Poland.
howToStart: Find your country's national philosophy olympiad (many nations have one feeding into IPO) and enter that national round first.
a2cInsight: The genuine international pinnacle of pre-university philosophy competition — a medal here is elite-tier and rare, but requires navigating an often opaque, country-dependent qualification pathway, and many countries have no feeder olympiad at all.

name: Aristotle Contest (University of Toronto Philosophy Essay Contest)
prerequisites: Canadian high school students; 1200-1500-word essay defending a position on one of three provided philosophical questions.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; deadline typically mid-July.
howToStart: Check philosophy.utoronto.ca for the annual questions and submission instructions; essays are double-marked by a teacher and a U of T faculty evaluator.
a2cInsight: A solid, low-stakes way for Canadian students specifically to get real university faculty feedback on philosophical writing, though its reach and prestige are mostly regional.

name: Berggruen Prize Essay Competition
prerequisites: Open to any writer worldwide (no formal age floor, but essays must show sophisticated original thinking); non-academic style essay on the announced theme, submitted as PDF.
costNote: Free to enter; $50,000 prize for the top English essay, $50,000 for the top Chinese essay.
cadence: Annual; theme and deadline announced each year (2026 cycle deadline is August 17, 2026).
howToStart: Check berggruen.org/essay-competition-open for the current theme and submission portal.
a2cInsight: Technically open to all ages with a massive prize, but in practice it draws professional writers and academics, so it's a long-shot moonshot rather than a realistic student credential unless the essay is truly exceptional.

name: Harvard International Economics Essay Competition (HIEEC)
prerequisites: High school students grades 9-12 worldwide; essay (up to 1500 words) responding to one of several announced economics prompts, with Chicago or APA citations.
costNote: Free to enter; only the first submission per student is accepted.
cadence: Annual, roughly Fall-Winter (2025-2026 cycle sponsored by the Harvard Undergraduate Economics Association).
howToStart: Check thehuea.org for the current year's prompts, word limit, and submission window.
a2cInsight: Harvard-brand name recognition helps it stand out on an application, but it's run by an undergraduate club, not the university, so treat it as a solid-but-not-elite subject credential.

name: Berkeley Economic Review High School Essay Contest
prerequisites: High school students grades 9-12; 1,000-word essay responding to the announced prompt, MLA format with Works Cited.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Runs each semester (Fall and Spring cycles); recent Spring 2026 application open.
howToStart: Check econreview.studentorg.berkeley.edu/high-school-essay for the current prompt and submission form.
a2cInsight: Like HIEEC, this is a respected undergraduate-run publication contest — good practice and a real byline in Equilibrium magazine, but not an institutionally-run Berkeley honor.

name: Coolidge Foundation Economic Essay Contest
prerequisites: US high school seniors (graduating no earlier than the contest year) who are US citizens/permanent residents; max 1,100-word essay on the announced economic policy topic.
costNote: Free to enter; top Block Prize is $10,000, second place $5,000, plus a fully paid DC finalist trip for finalists.
cadence: Annual; opens summer, deadline in fall (2026 cycle deadline September 10, 2026), finalist event follows in DC.
howToStart: Check coolidgefoundation.org for the current year's prompt and application portal; homeschoolers are welcome.
a2cInsight: Genuinely well-funded and rigorous — finalists get a real DC trip and publication in the Coolidge Review, making this one of the stronger free-market economics essay credentials for US seniors specifically.

name: IEA Student Essay Competition (Dorian Fisher Memorial Prize)
prerequisites: IB or A-Level students (roughly grades 11-13); pick three questions from an announced set and answer each in a short essay.
costNote: Free to enter; 1st prize £500, plus £250 each to three runners-up and £500 to the school with the most entrants.
cadence: Annual; open to IB and A-Level students, deadlines vary by year (typically spring, with an autumn event for top entrants).
howToStart: Check iea.org.uk/essay-competition for the current year's questions and submission details (entries emailed to essayprize@iea.org.uk).
a2cInsight: Run by the UK's oldest free-market think tank; winning is respected among UK economics/PPE admissions tutors but the program is ideologically branded.

name: IEA Monetary Policy Essay Prize (Sixth Form)
prerequisites: UK sixth-form students (or international equivalent); essay (up to 2,500 words) on a monetary policy question set annually, with Harvard-style referencing.
costNote: Free to enter; sixth-form track pays £1,500 for first prize and £750 each to two runners-up.
cadence: Annual; separate sixth-form (roughly grade 11-13) and undergraduate tracks.
howToStart: Check iea.org.uk/Monetary-Policy-Essay-Prize for the current year's question and submission portal.
a2cInsight: A narrower, more technical economics prize than the general IEA essay competition — good for students specifically interested in monetary economics/central banking.

name: Harvard Political Review Journalism Essay Competition
prerequisites: Middle and high school students worldwide; individual or teams of 2-5 from the same school; extended essay on an announced topic.
costNote: Entry fee of $45 per team/individual (priority/regular/late tiers); fee waivers available for low-income students via school counselor.
cadence: Multiple cycles per year (e.g., winter and spring rounds); 2026 spring cycle priority deadline January 15, regular March 1, late March 15.
howToStart: Register via the current cycle's application platform (linked from theharvardpoliticalreview.com/harvard-political-review-journalism-competition) and pick one of the announced prompts.
a2cInsight: Harvard's undergraduate political journal brand carries real recognition value, but this is a paid-entry, high-volume contest, so global/country-level placement (not mere participation) is what matters.

name: Columbia Political Review High School Essay Contest
prerequisites: High school students worldwide; 700-1,200-word essay on the announced political/policy prompt with in-text hyperlink citations.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, deadline typically around July 1 (2026 deadline: July 1, 2026).
howToStart: Check cpreview.org/essay-contest for the current year's prompt and submit as a PDF via the linked Google Form.
a2cInsight: Small cash prizes ($300/$100) and website publication make this a legitimate but modest credential — good practice for policy-argument writing.

name: Yale Review of International Studies High School Essay Contest
prerequisites: High school students (grades 9-12) worldwide; under 1,500-word essay on international relations/global citizenship theme.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, deadline typically end of June (2026 deadline: June 30, 2026).
howToStart: Check yris.yira.org/high-school-essay-contest for the current prompt and submission process.
a2cInsight: A respected Yale undergraduate IR journal; publication is a nice credential for students focused on international relations/foreign policy tracks.

name: Princeton Legal Journal High School Essay Contest
prerequisites: High school students worldwide (enrolled as of the contest's cutoff date); essay exploring an announced legal/constitutional theme.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; theme and deadline change each year (2026 cycle: applications due February 1, 2026, winners announced March 1, 2026).
howToStart: Check legaljournal.princeton.edu/high-school-essay-contest for the current theme and submission portal.
a2cInsight: Winning essays are published and go through real editorial review with the Princeton Legal Journal team — solid for pre-law-interested students, though the journal itself is undergraduate-run, not Princeton-institutional.

name: Columbia Undergraduate Law Review High School Essay Contest
prerequisites: Current high school students worldwide, not recent graduates; max 1,500-word essay on the announced legal topic with Chicago citations.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, deadline typically late June (2026 cycle deadline: June 26, 2026).
howToStart: Check culawreview.org/high-school-essay-contest for the current topic and submission form.
a2cInsight: A solid, focused legal-writing credential; winners are invited to a CULR speaker event, adding a modest networking/engagement bonus beyond just publication.

name: The Schola
prerequisites: High school students worldwide; up to 8,000-word original humanities/social-science essay, sole authorship, written during high school (post-graduation submission allowed near graduation deadline).
costNote: $180 non-refundable submission fee per essay.
cadence: Rolling submissions year-round; quarterly issues (March, June, September, December), review can take up to several months.
howToStart: Prepare an original essay (often from independent research or a strong term paper), format per the Chicago Manual of Style, and submit via theschola.org/products/submit.
a2cInsight: Genuinely selective, PhD-adjacent blind review process makes it one of the more credible humanities publication venues for high schoolers, but the journal does not publish a numeric acceptance rate and the steep $180 submission fee is a real barrier and fairness concern for some admissions readers.

name: National High School Ethics Bowl (NHSEB)
prerequisites: US high school students grades 9-12 at accredited schools (some homeschool teams admitted at organizer discretion, plus international regionals in Canada/England); teams of 3-7 discuss real ethical case studies, up to 5 seated per match.
costNote: Regional registration fees are typically modest (tens of dollars per team); travel costs for nationals add up, though qualifying teams' national travel is often subsidized.
cadence: Annual; regional competitions in fall/winter, national championship each spring (April) at UNC Chapel Hill.
howToStart: Find your regional NHSEB competition via nhseb.org and form or join a school team.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded, collaborative (not adversarial) format that signals strong ethical reasoning and public discourse skill; reaching nationals at UNC Chapel Hill is a credible, checkable achievement.

name: National Economics Challenge (Council for Economic Education)
prerequisites: US students grades 6-12 in three divisions: Milton Friedman (middle school, grades 6-8), Adam Smith (AP/IB/Honors or returning high schoolers), and David Ricardo (first-time high schoolers); team-based knowledge test on micro/macro/international economics.
costNote: State/regional registration fees vary; national finals travel is fully paid for qualifying teams.
cadence: Annual; state competitions in spring, national semifinals/finals following.
howToStart: Check councilforeconed.org/programs/for-students/national-economic-challenge for state affiliate registration.
a2cInsight: America's most established team-based economics knowledge competition, and accessible to middle schoolers via the Milton Friedman division — a strong, checkable credential for economics-focused applicants, especially at the national final level.

name: International Economics Olympiad (IEO)
prerequisites: Under 20 years old as of the competition's cutoff date, high school graduation after the specified date; national team of up to 5 students plus team leader(s), or individual entry via OpenTrack.
costNote: Participation is free; teams typically need national-level qualification and self-fund travel to the international round.
cadence: Annual international final; national qualification pathways vary by the 60+ participating countries. An OpenTrack format also allows individual participation without a national team.
howToStart: Check ieo-official.org for your country's national qualifying process, or enter directly via the OpenTrack option if no national round exists in your country.
a2cInsight: Backed by Nobel laureate economist Eric Maskin and drawing 60+ countries, medaling here is a genuine elite-tier economics credential comparable to other international olympiads; the OpenTrack option also makes it accessible without a national federation.

name: Economics World Cup (EWC)
prerequisites: Students aged 12-18 worldwide, teams of 2-6; fully virtual, open-book format including a real-world development case built with Oxford involvement; top 15% individual scorers qualify their team for finals.
costNote: Registration fee is approximately $20 per participant; exact amount varies by year and team size.
cadence: Annual; Round 1 (1 hour, open-book) then Round 2/finals (roughly 1-week team project), typically spring.
howToStart: Register a team at economicsworldcup.org before the round 1 deadline.
a2cInsight: A newer, highly accessible (open-book, virtual, MS-friendly) economics competition; good entry point but not yet as institutionally established as the National Economics Challenge or IEO.

name: World Economics Cup (WEC) Team Academic Essay
prerequisites: High school teams of 6 students under a school adviser; individual exam modules (Fundamentals, Deep Comprehension) plus a collaborative Thinking & Innovation presentation module.
costNote: Registration is free; teams self-fund any travel to in-person continental/world final rounds.
cadence: Annual; school team registration in spring (e.g., by March 31), then Continental Qualifiers, Continental Cups, and a World Final.
howToStart: Check worldeconcup.org for team registration and the current competition cycle.
a2cInsight: A distinct competition from the similarly-named Economics World Cup — the organization explicitly disclaims any affiliation on its site. The team-based, multi-module format (spanning 55+ countries) rewards sustained collaborative work over quiz-style speed, but registration is free rather than a paid fee as sometimes assumed.

name: YoungArts National Arts Competition (Writing)
prerequisites: Ages 15-18 or grades 10-12; submit fiction (up to 15 pages), poetry (3-5 poems, up to 10 pages total), or play/script (up to 15 pages).
costNote: Application fee (waivers available); no cost for winners.
cadence: Annual; application window typically opens in July, closes in early October (2026 cycle opens July 21, 2026; 2026 cycle closed Oct 8, 2025); winners announced late November.
howToStart: Create an account at youngarts.org, prepare a polished writing portfolio in one category, and apply before the October deadline.
a2cInsight: One of the most admissions-respected teen arts honors in the US; a Finalist/Winner distinction (especially with National YoungArts Week) is a genuine difference-maker on applications to elite colleges and arts conservatories.

name: Bennington Young Writers Awards
prerequisites: Grades 9-12 worldwide; requires a teacher (or non-parent mentor) sponsor; submit poetry (up to 3 poems), fiction, or nonfiction essay (fiction/nonfiction capped at 1,500 words).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; submission window opens September 1, entries due November 1.
howToStart: Have a teacher or mentor review your work, then submit online via Bennington College's Young Writers Awards page before November 1.
a2cInsight: A top-tier, no-fee national contest; winning is a legitimate signal of literary talent and comes with a full-tuition Bennington scholarship pathway, making it especially valuable if Bennington is a target school.

name: Patricia Grodd Poetry Prize for Young Writers (Kenyon Review)
prerequisites: Open only to high school sophomores and juniors; submit unpublished poetry.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; submissions open November 1-30; winners announced the following March.
howToStart: Prepare original, unpublished poems and submit through The Kenyon Review's Submittable page during the November window.
a2cInsight: Publication in The Kenyon Review as a teenager is a genuinely prestigious literary credential that signals serious poetic craft to admissions readers and future MFA programs alike.

name: Princeton Ten-Minute Play Contest
prerequisites: Limited to students in 11th grade (US or international equivalent, including homeschool); one play up to 10 pages per entrant; AI-generated plays not allowed.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; opens in fall, closes early (often before stated deadline) once 250 entries are received.
howToStart: Write an original ten-minute play (max 10 pages) and submit online via Princeton's Lewis Center for the Arts high school contests page as soon as the window opens, since it closes early.
a2cInsight: A niche but legitimate Ivy-affiliated playwriting credential; strong for students building a theater/writing portfolio, though prize money ($500/$250/$100) and prestige are modest compared to YoungArts or Scholastic.

name: Leonard L. Milberg '53 High School Poetry Prize (Princeton)
prerequisites: Currently enrolled 11th graders (US or international equivalent), any school setting including homeschool; submit up to 3 poems, no length limit per poem.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; application period opens in fall.
howToStart: Prepare up to 3 poems (previously submitted/published work allowed) and apply online via Princeton's Lewis Center for the Arts during the fall window.
a2cInsight: Judged by Princeton's own creative writing faculty poets, giving it real literary credibility, though it's junior-year-only and less widely known than Scholastic or YoungArts.

name: Leyla Beban Young Authors Foundation: $1000 for 1000 Words
ageNote: Confirmed discontinued: the Foundation's board announced in November 2025 that after twelve years (2013-2025) the contest was closing permanently. A final contest ran with a February 1, 2026 deadline; no future contests are planned as of July 2026.
prerequisites: Was open to grades 6-12; submitted an original short fiction piece of exactly 1000 words.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Was annual, deadline historically around February 1; now discontinued.
howToStart: No longer accepting entries. The foundation's site (bluefire.org) confirms the program has ended; do not plan around this contest for future cycles.
a2cInsight: Was a nice, MS-accessible entry point into competitive short fiction with real scholarship money, but the organization has now formally shut down after its final Feb 2026 cycle — remove from active planning lists.

name: Nancy Thorp Poetry Contest (Hollins University)
prerequisites: Open only to high school girls who are US citizens in sophomore or junior year; submit poems (unpublished, or previously school-honored work eligible).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; typically closes in winter/early spring.
howToStart: Prepare your best poems and submit per Hollins University's contest instructions before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A long-running, respected girls-only poetry prize with real scholarship value at Hollins (under 2% acceptance in some years); strong regional/national signal for young women poets, though gender- and citizenship-restricted.

name: NCTE Achievement Awards in Writing
prerequisites: High school sophomores and juniors; must be nominated by a teacher or English department (no self-submission), with nomination caps based on school enrollment.
costNote: Small per-student nomination fee paid by the school/teacher.
cadence: Annual; prompt released in summer, nominations open in fall, contest closes February 15, results emailed mid-May.
howToStart: Ask an English teacher to nominate you; if selected, you'll respond to a nationally issued prompt and submit two pieces of writing.
a2cInsight: A solid, teacher-gatekept national writing honor recognized by many colleges, but its teacher-nomination-only format means students can't self-initiate the way they can with open contests.

name: Foyle Young Poets of the Year Award
prerequisites: Ages 11-17 as of the entry deadline; UK entrants may submit up to 3 poems, international entrants up to 1 poem (max 40 lines each).
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; entries typically open spring/summer, closing around July 31.
howToStart: Write an original poem (max 40 lines) and submit online via the Foyle Young Poets website (run by The Poetry Society) before the annual deadline.
a2cInsight: The single most prestigious international teen poetry award available to students as young as 11; a Foyle win or commendation is a genuinely elite, globally recognized credential.

name: National Student Poets Program
prerequisites: Must be a gold/silver national medalist in poetry from the Scholastic Art & Writing Awards while in grade 10 or 11; selected by a national panel.
costNote: No direct application; selected from Scholastic Art & Writing Awards poetry medalists.
cadence: Annual appointment; five poets named nationally, one per US region.
howToStart: Submit outstanding poetry to the Scholastic Art & Writing Awards; national poetry medalists are automatically eligible for NSPP consideration.
a2cInsight: The nation's top honor for a teen poet, run with the Library of Congress and a presidential-committee-linked nonprofit; extraordinarily rare (5 students/year) and a marquee credential when earned.

name: Live Poets Society of NJ National High School Poetry Contest
prerequisites: US high school students; poem must be 20 lines or fewer, unpublished, original, in English.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Rolling/quarterly; one poem may be submitted per 90-day period, year-round.
howToStart: Write a poem of 20 lines or fewer and submit through highschoolpoetrycontest.com; eligible to resubmit a new poem every 90 days.
a2cInsight: Easy to enter repeatedly and legitimately free, but it's a high-volume, low-selectivity contest with many small prizes rather than a prestige marker for competitive admissions.

name: Poetry Out Loud
prerequisites: High school students (grades 9-12) at participating schools; memorize and recite poems from the Poetry Out Loud anthology.
costNote: Free; run through schools and state arts agencies.
cadence: Annual; school and classroom rounds in fall/winter, state finals in early spring, National Finals in late spring.
howToStart: Ask your English teacher if your school participates, or check state arts agency listings; compete first at the classroom/school level.
a2cInsight: A National Endowment for the Arts flagship program with huge reach (millions of participants since 2005); advancing to state or national finals is a strong, well-known credential, though it rewards recitation/performance more than original writing.

name: Signet Classics Student Scholarship Essay Contest
prerequisites: Full-time high school juniors or seniors (or homeschooled equivalents aged 16-18) in the US; write a 2-3 page essay responding to an assigned literary prompt.
costNote: No entry fee (was free when active).
cadence: Formerly annual; opened fall, closed spring, tied to a specific Signet Classics novel each year.
howToStart: The contest's official winners page and news archive show no activity beyond the 2019 (23rd Annual) cycle and a 2020 announcement post; no evidence of a running contest for 2021-2026 was found, so this credential should be treated as defunct despite the homepage still existing.
a2cInsight: Was a respectable, free literary-analysis essay contest tied to a major publisher, but public records (winners page, contest blog) stop after the 2019/2020 cycle with no newer winners announced in six-plus years; treat as discontinued rather than a currently pursuable credential.

name: Betty Gabehart Prizes (Kentucky Women Writers Conference)
prerequisites: Open to women writers (no strict age floor specified, but realistically most competitive for advanced high schoolers and up); submit in poetry, fiction, or nonfiction.
costNote: $10 entry fee per submission.
cadence: Annual; entries due June 1, winners announced by July 15.
howToStart: Prepare a polished manuscript in one genre category, pay the $10 fee, and submit by the June 1 deadline via the Kentucky Women Writers Conference.
a2cInsight: A legitimate adult-oriented literary prize that talented, ambitious teen women writers occasionally enter for the challenge and conference access, but it's not designed as a teen-specific credential and competes against adult writers.

name: Sejong Writing Competition (Sijo)
prerequisites: Open to all US and Canada residents regardless of ethnicity; Pre-college division covers ages 18 and under; write in the traditional Korean sijo poetic form.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; entries typically due in spring.
howToStart: Learn the sijo form (3-line, syllable-counted Korean poem) and submit an original sijo through the Sejong Cultural Society's website before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A distinctive niche credential that shows genuine engagement with a specific poetic tradition and Korean culture; valuable for students building an interdisciplinary/cultural portfolio, though its name recognition is limited outside Korean-American circles. Note: the competition's separate essay category was discontinued starting 2026, replaced by a Sejong Book Club; the sijo poetry category remains active and is now the sole competition track.

name: Ann Arbor District Library "It's All Write!" Teen Writing Contest
prerequisites: Students in grades 6-12 (three age brackets: 6-8, 9-10, 11-12); submit poetry, flash fiction, or short stories.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; submissions open January, close March 1, winners announced in May.
howToStart: Write an original poem, flash fiction, or short story and submit through the Ann Arbor District Library's contest page during the winter window.
a2cInsight: A well-run but geographically-branded local library contest; good low-stakes practice and a nice line item for younger students, not a nationally competitive credential.

name: Skyline Writing Prize / Teen Spirit (Ann Arbor Skyline Learning Center)
prerequisites: High school students (grades 9-12) submit work for consideration in the Teen Spirit literary magazine and associated Skyline Writing Prize.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; culminates in a spring release event at Literati Bookstore.
howToStart: Submit original writing for consideration in Teen Spirit via the Skyline Learning Center's submission process.
a2cInsight: A charming, community-rooted literary magazine and prize with real publication value locally, but limited national name recognition for competitive admissions purposes. Note: the program's site has been renamed from 'Skyline Writing Center' to 'Skyline Learning Center'; the older writingcenter-branded domains no longer resolve, so use the corrected URL below.

name: Teen Ink (magazine, website, and contests)
prerequisites: Ages 13-19 for site accounts/publication (roughly grades 7/8-12); teens of any grade within that band may submit writing, art, or photography.
costNote: No cost to submit or be published.
cadence: Rolling; digital magazine published every two months, plus periodic themed contests.
howToStart: Create a free account at teenink.com and submit poetry, fiction, essays, or reviews for consideration; work is automatically entered into relevant contests.
a2cInsight: One of the largest and easiest teen publication venues (750,000+ archived pieces), which makes it a great low-barrier first publication credit but a very common one that carries limited prestige on its own for competitive admissions. Note: the 13-19 age requirement effectively excludes most 6th/7th graders; only students who are already 13 (typically 8th grade and up) qualify.

name: Iowa Young Writers' Studio
prerequisites: Residential track: current or incoming 10th-12th graders. Online 6-week course track: current or incoming 9th-12th graders. Competitive application requires a writing sample.
costNote: Tuition applies (financial aid/scholarships available); free/low-cost need-based aid offered. 2-week residential fee is roughly $2,500; the 6-week online course track is lower cost and open to 9th graders, while the competitive 2-week residential track requires 10th-12th grade.
cadence: Annual; summer residential program application opens winter, closes in early spring; also offers 6-week online courses in winter/summer.
howToStart: Prepare a writing sample in fiction, poetry, or another genre and apply online via the University of Iowa's Iowa Young Writers' Studio site before the deadline.
a2cInsight: Acceptance into this selective pre-college program (taught by Iowa Writers' Workshop graduates) is a well-regarded signal of serious creative-writing commitment, though it is a summer program/credential rather than a competition with a single 'winner.' Note: the flagship 2-week residential program is only open to grades 10-12; grade 9 students can only access the lower-cost 6-week online course track.

name: Skipping Stones Youth Honor Awards
prerequisites: Ages 7-17; submit creative writing, photo essays, or artwork focused on multicultural/international awareness or nature.
costNote: $6 entry fee (waived for low-income entrants).
cadence: Annual; entries due in early May.
howToStart: Prepare a piece of writing, art, or photography on a multicultural or nature theme, write a cover letter, and mail/email your submission with the entry fee by the May deadline.
a2cInsight: A small, values-driven multicultural literary magazine award; nice for younger or MS students building an early portfolio, but modest prestige value at the high school competitive-admissions level.

name: Ledbury Young Poets Competition
prerequisites: Open to young people aged 5-18; poem must be original, max 40 lines.
costNote: No entry fee for the youth competition.
cadence: Annual; opens around April, closes in May, tied to an annual theme.
howToStart: Write an original poem (max 40 lines) responding to the year's theme and submit via the Ledbury Poetry website before the May deadline.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded UK regional youth poetry competition tied to a respected poetry festival; good international credential for younger teens, though smaller in scale and prestige than Foyle.

name: Smith College High School Poetry Prize
prerequisites: Open only to sophomore and junior girls attending school in New England or New York; submit original poetry.
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; typically closes in winter, with a spring reading/award event at Smith College.
howToStart: Prepare your strongest original poems and submit per the Boutelle-Day Poetry Center's contest guidelines before the winter deadline.
a2cInsight: A respected regional women's-college poetry prize judged by notable poets; strong for the New England/NY girls it's eligible to, but geographically and gender-restricted, limiting broader applicability.

name: Ringling College Storytellers of Tomorrow Creative Writing Contest
prerequisites: Current high school-age students worldwide; submit an English-language story, script, comic, or micro-RPG under 2,000 words (600 for micro-RPG).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; opens in fall, closes mid-January, winners announced in February.
howToStart: Write an original short story, script, comic, or micro-RPG within the word limit and submit via Ringling College's Storytellers of Tomorrow contest page.
a2cInsight: A creative and genre-inclusive contest (including comics and micro-RPGs) from a respected art-and-design college; solid for genre-fiction-leaning writers, though not as broadly prestigious as Scholastic or YoungArts.

name: Gwendolyn Brooks Youth Poetry Awards
prerequisites: K-12 students residing in Illinois; submit one original poem (25-line limit for grades K-5, 50-line limit for grades 6-12).
costNote: No entry fee.
cadence: Annual; entries due around May 1.
howToStart: Write an original poem within your grade band's line limit and submit through Illinois Humanities' online portal by the May deadline.
a2cInsight: A meaningful statewide honor with real literary heritage (founded by Gwendolyn Brooks herself), but its Illinois-residency requirement limits it to a single state's students.

name: The Louisville Review: The Children's Corner
prerequisites: Students in grades K-12; submit original, previously unpublished poetry (Cornerstone section) for consideration.
costNote: No fee to submit.
cadence: Rolling submission windows roughly twice a year (e.g. late Jan-mid March and Sept-Nov) rather than year-round; check current dates on the site.
howToStart: Submit original poetry through The Louisville Review's Submittable page (Cornerstone/Children's Corner section) during an open submission window.
a2cInsight: A genuine literary-journal publication credit accessible to even elementary-age writers, giving a low-barrier way to say 'published in a national literary journal,' though selectivity and prestige are modest.

name: Rattle Young Poets Anthology
prerequisites: Poet must have been 15 or younger when the poem was written, 18 or younger at submission; any nationality. Submit up to 4 poems in one file via Submittable.
costNote: No submission fee; poets receive 10 free contributor copies.
cadence: Annual open submission window (roughly winter into spring); anthology ships with Rattle's summer issue.
howToStart: Create a Submittable account and submit through rattle.submittable.com/submit/34387/young-poets-anthology during the open window; a parent/guardian must sign a release if accepted.
a2cInsight: Publication in a respected, widely-circulated literary magazine's dedicated youth issue is a real, non-vanity poetry-publishing credit — selective (about 20 poems chosen from thousands) and worth listing as a genuine literary acceptance.

name: River of Words International Poetry & Art Contest
prerequisites: Open to enrolled students grades K-12 (ages 5-19/20); up to 5 poem entries (max 32 lines or 3 min signed) and 5 art entries; English or Spanish.
costNote: Free to enter; run by Saint Mary's College of California Center for Environmental Literacy.
cadence: Annual; opens each fall, deadline typically January 31.
howToStart: Submit through the official contest portal linked from the Saint Mary's College Center for Environmental Literacy site; poems should engage with watershed/environmental themes.
a2cInsight: Billed as the world's largest youth poetry-and-art contest with Library of Congress ties; solid, legitimate mid-tier honor especially strong for environmental-themed portfolios, but breadth of entries makes it less selective than Foyle or Scholastic. Corrected: this is open starting in kindergarten, not just from grade 6 up.

name: Creative Communication Student Poetry Contest
prerequisites: Open to US students grades K-9; submit an original poem of 21 lines or fewer online, individually or via teacher.
costNote: Free to enter; anthology purchase (~$26+) is optional but heavily marketed to 'winners' (roughly 40-50% of entrants are selected for publication).
cadence: Runs three cycles per year (roughly fall, winter, spring) with rolling entry deadlines.
howToStart: Enter online at poeticpower.com/students; no fee to submit, but expect a sales pitch for the anthology if selected.
a2cInsight: A high-acceptance-rate 'vanity-adjacent' program — encouraging for young beginner writers but carries little admissions weight and shouldn't be confused with a selective literary honor.

name: Betjeman Poetry Prize (John Betjeman Poetry Competition)
ageNote: Founded 2006 for the John Betjeman centenary; the operating charity ('The Betjeman Poetry Prize', registered charity 1137579) was formally removed from the UK Charity Register on 15 February 2020 as 'ceased to exist,' confirming the press reports of closure after 13 years. The website remains live but has not been updated past 2015 content.
prerequisites: Was open to UK/British Isles students aged 10-13 (Key Stage 2-3) while active.
costNote: Was free to enter while active.
cadence: Formerly annual with a July deadline; no longer running.
howToStart: No longer operating — the charity has ceased to exist and there is no current entry pathway.
a2cInsight: A once well-regarded UK middle-school-age poetry prize judged by notable poets (Carol Ann Duffy, Imtiaz Dharker), but confirmed closed as of 2020 via UK Charity Commission records. Do not include in active planning; retained here only as a verified-defunct reference.

name: National Youth Poet Laureate Program
prerequisites: Ages 13-19; must generally first be selected as a city/regional Youth Poet Laureate (over 70 partner cities/organizations) through that city's own application and judging process before being eligible for the national title.
costNote: Free; entry typically requires first winning a local/regional Youth Poet Laureate title.
cadence: Annual; local youth poet laureate programs run on their own city/regional cycles, feeding into the national selection each fall (applications roughly Oct-Nov, results announced early the following year).
howToStart: Find and apply to a local Youth Poet Laureate program (e.g., through Urban Word NYC, a city library, or arts commission) — see youthlaureate.org for partner-city list and pathway.
a2cInsight: The single highest-prestige civic-poetry honor for a US teen — Amanda Gorman's platform — but access is gated by geography since it requires winning a local laureateship first, so it's not a simple open competition.

name: Rooted & Radical Youth Poetry Festival (formerly Louder Than a Bomb)
prerequisites: Chicago-area high school and community-org teams; register a team through a school or youth organization.
costNote: Free to participate; team-based, run through Young Chicago Authors.
cadence: Annual, roughly 6-week festival culminating in a spring (Feb-March) showcase in Chicago.
howToStart: Have a teacher, youth program, or spoken-word club register a team via youngchicagoauthors.org/rnr when registration opens.
a2cInsight: The largest youth slam-poetry festival in Chicago, rebranded from the HBO-documentary-famous Louder Than a Bomb after Young Chicago Authors paused and restructured the festival following abuse allegations against former leadership. Strong credential for spoken-word/performance poets, but participation is geographically limited to greater Chicago.

name: Brave New Voices International Youth Poetry Slam Festival
prerequisites: Ages 13-19; qualify by winning a spot with a local youth spoken-word organization/chapter that sends a team.
costNote: Free to compete but teams typically fundraise for travel/lodging to the host city; local Youth Speaks-affiliated chapters may have program fees.
cadence: Annual, held each July in a rotating host city (2026 festival: July 17-20, Washington DC, at the Kennedy Center).
howToStart: Find and join a local youth spoken-word program affiliated with Youth Speaks/Brave New Voices network (see youthspeaks.org/bravenewvoices) to try out for a team.
a2cInsight: The world's premier international youth spoken-word slam — genuinely prestigious in the performance-poetry world, but access depends entirely on having (or reaching) a local affiliated youth poetry organization.

name: Dear Poet (Academy of American Poets)
prerequisites: Students grades 5-12; write a letter responding to a poem read by a contemporary poet in a provided video.
costNote: Completely free, classroom-friendly.
cadence: Annual during National Poetry Month (April); response booklet published in summer.
howToStart: Participate via poets.org/dearpoet during April; teachers can assign as a class activity.
a2cInsight: Not a competitive prize but a legitimate, well-run Academy of American Poets educational project — good for building a poetry-engagement portfolio for younger students rather than as a headline honor.

name: National Poetry Month Poster Contest for Students (Academy of American Poets)
ageNote: Ran 2020-2022 (verified via poets.org's own poster archive, which lists a student grand-prize winner each of those years, e.g. Lara L., Grade 11, for 2022). Starting with the 2023 poster, the Academy of American Poets partnered with Scholastic and used professional/adult illustrators (Marc Brown in 2023, Jack Wong in 2024, Christy Mandin in 2025, Alfredo Richner in 2026) instead of running a student contest; no student-contest cycle has resumed through 2026.
prerequisites: Formerly open to US students grades 9-12; designed original poster artwork incorporating lines of poetry.
costNote: Was free to enter.
cadence: No longer running; was annual (tied to National Poetry Month each April) from 2020-2022 only.
howToStart: Discontinued — there is no current entry pathway. The official April poster is now produced via an Academy of American Poets/Scholastic partnership with a professional illustrator each year.
a2cInsight: Was a small, single-winner national contest from a highly credible organization (Academy of American Poets) with modest cash prize ($1,000) and Poets.org visibility — but it quietly ended after 2022 and should not be listed as a current opportunity.

name: Robert Frost Poetry Contest for Kids and Teens (The Studios of Key West)
prerequisites: Monroe County, Florida (or occasionally broader Keys-area) students ages 6-18; submit up to 3 poems in English or Spanish.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, entries open each April.
howToStart: Submit via The Studios of Key West website (tskw.org) during the April window.
a2cInsight: A charming, historically-grounded local contest (honoring Robert Frost's Key West winters) — good confidence-building recognition for younger or Florida Keys-area students, but its reach and prestige are strictly local.

name: National Federation of State Poetry Societies (NFSPS) Student Poetry Contests
prerequisites: US students grades 3-12; one poem per student per contest category via mail or Submittable, following each contest's specific rules.
costNote: Free entry for the student divisions.
cadence: Annual; contests open January 1 with a mid-March postmark/submission deadline; results at the NFSPS national convention.
howToStart: Visit nfsps.net/annual-contests for current student contest categories (e.g. the Manningham Student Poetry Contest, Poetry in the Classroom awards) and follow entry instructions.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, decades-old nonprofit federation with real judged contests and cash prizes, but it's a minor, low-visibility credential compared to national programs — useful mainly as an easy, free, confidence-building entry point.

name: Letters About Literature (Library of Congress Center for the Book)
ageNote: The national Library of Congress program concluded its 27th and final year with the 2018-2019 cycle; read.gov/letters/ now documents the program as ended, having engaged over one million students during its run. Some individual state Centers for the Book (e.g., Massachusetts, Texas) continue to run their own independently-branded 'Letters About Literature' contests, but there is no active national program to point students to.
prerequisites: Formerly open to students grades 4-12; wrote a personal letter to an author (living or dead) about how a book, poem, speech, or essay affected them.
costNote: Was free to enter.
cadence: No longer running nationally as of 2019; check individual state Center for the Book affiliates for any state-level continuations.
howToStart: The national program is discontinued. Interested students should check their own state's Center for the Book affiliate (via read.gov/cfb/) to see if a state-level version still runs.
a2cInsight: A once-respected, 27-year-running Library of Congress-affiliated reader-response letter contest, frequently used by young poets responding to poems — but the national program ended in 2019 and should not be presented as currently active.

name: Cadence Video Poetry Festival Youth Workshops & Screenings
prerequisites: Open to teen participants in Pacific Northwest-area youth workshops; general open-call submissions welcome more broadly for the video-poetry screening program.
costNote: Free youth workshops; festival submissions may have modest fees for the general open call.
cadence: Annual, held during April (National Poetry Month) in Seattle at Northwest Film Forum, with an associated open call for video-poem submissions and an online component.
howToStart: Check cadencevideopoetry.org each winter/spring for the youth workshop and open-call submission windows.
a2cInsight: A niche but genuine venue for poets experimenting with video/multimedia poetry — small-scale and regional, best framed as an interesting creative outlet rather than a resume-building prize.

name: Young Poets Network Writing Challenges & Competitions (The Poetry Society, UK)
prerequisites: Open to young people up to age 25 worldwide (some individual challenges set narrower age bands); follow each challenge's specific prompt and word/line limits.
costNote: Free to enter all challenges and competitions listed.
cadence: Rolling; new themed writing challenges and competitions posted throughout the year (in addition to hosting the annual Foyle Young Poets Award).
howToStart: Browse current opportunities at ypn.poetrysociety.org.uk/poetry-opportunities/ and submit per each challenge's instructions.
a2cInsight: A steady stream of low-stakes, free monthly/seasonal challenges from a highly credible UK poetry organization — great for building a writing habit and small publication credits, though individual challenge 'wins' carry modest weight versus Foyle itself.

name: Just Poetry!!! National High School Poetry Scholarship
prerequisites: US high school students; submit an original poem with no strict theme/style requirement.
costNote: No entry fee; affiliated anthology purchase optional and marketed to many entrants.
cadence: Runs on a quarterly cycle year-round (companion program to the Live Poets Society of NJ contest); operating since 1998.
howToStart: Submit via justpoetry.org; no fee required.
a2cInsight: Same operator and business model as the Live Poets Society NJ contest — free to enter with a real scholarship-payout track record ($100-$500 quarterly prizes), but the large number of 'winners' each quarter and anthology upsell mean it should be framed as encouragement, not a selective honor.

name: Polyphony Lit
prerequisites: High schoolers (grades 9-12, ages 14-18) worldwide; blind-juried; max 3 pieces per year; poetry <=80 lines, prose <=1800 words.
costNote: No submission fee; entirely free to submit and to join as a student editor.
cadence: Three open submission windows per year (roughly May, October, March), 200-submission cap per window; annual print/online volume plus the Claudia Ann Seaman Awards ($200 per genre).
howToStart: Create a Submittable account and submit during an open window at polyphonylit.org; consider also applying to be a peer editor to build a track record.
a2cInsight: Widely regarded as the most selective and editorially rigorous teen lit mag, with real peer-review training baked in, so acceptance carries genuine weight in an application's writing section.

name: The Apprentice Writer
prerequisites: Current secondary-school (high school) students; poetry, fiction, nonfiction, and photography accepted.
costNote: No submission or reading fee.
cadence: Annual issue, typically with a winter/spring submission window; published each year since 1984.
howToStart: Check current call for submissions at apprenticewriter.com (Susquehanna University Writers Institute) and submit per that year's guidelines.
a2cInsight: One of the longest-running, university-run teen journals (40+ years), so a placement signals durable editorial credibility rather than a flash-in-the-pan credential.

name: Cathartic Youth Literary Magazine
prerequisites: Open to ages 12-22; poetry, fiction, creative nonfiction, opinion pieces, mental-health-themed writing.
costNote: No submission fees; founded partly to avoid fees/long wait times common in adult lit mags.
cadence: Rolling submissions; updated daily/weekly online.
howToStart: Submit via the site at catharticlitmagazine.weebly.com; all submissions receive feedback.
a2cInsight: A low-barrier, feedback-focused stepping-stone journal — good for building a first publication credit and confidence, not a prestige signal on its own.

name: Canvas Literary Journal
ageNote: Published quarterly 2013-2020 as a teen-run print/online journal out of Writers & Books in Rochester, NY. Confirmed closed in 2020; no new issues or open calls since.
prerequisites: Was open to ages 13-18; poetry, short stories, novel excerpts, plays, nonfiction, visual art.
costNote: Was free to submit via Submittable when active.
cadence: Was quarterly through 2020; no longer publishing.
howToStart: No longer accepting submissions; kept here for historical/context reference only.
a2cInsight: Was a well-regarded, fully teen-run journal, but it shut down in 2020 — do not list this as a current target for students.

name: Élan: An International Student Literary Magazine
prerequisites: Grades 9-12 worldwide; fiction, poetry, creative nonfiction, screenwriting, plays, and visual art; max 3 pieces per period; Word doc format required; no AI-generated work.
costNote: No fee to submit.
cadence: Two online editions per year (fall and spring), combined into an annual print edition; each piece auto-entered in the Élan Art and Writing Contest.
howToStart: Submit via elanlitmag.com during an open fall or spring window.
a2cInsight: Run by Douglas Anderson School of the Arts' creative writing department, giving it solid institutional backing; the attached contest adds a modest extra credential.

name: Parallax Online Literary Journal
prerequisites: High school students worldwide; poetry, fiction, creative nonfiction, dramatic writing; simultaneous submissions allowed with notification.
costNote: No submission fee.
cadence: Annual submission window, roughly September 5 - April 1.
howToStart: Submit via Submittable at parallaxliteraryjournal.submittable.com with a short third-person bio.
a2cInsight: A solid, steadily-running teen journal (Idyllwild Arts-affiliated, ranked 'Superior' in NCTE's 2022 REALM recognition) — respectable but not a top-tier name recognized outside literary-magazine circles.

name: Aerie International
ageNote: Historically the only international high-school literary-arts journal wholly peer-run; as of the most recent check there was no open call and the Submittable portal referenced a long-past 2020 deadline, so confirm the annual spring publication is still active before relying on it.
prerequisites: Ages 13-19, enrolled in secondary school; all genres plus visual art; simultaneous submissions allowed.
costNote: Free submission via Submittable.
cadence: Historically annual (spring); check for current open calls.
howToStart: Monitor aerieinternational.submittable.com and aerieinternational.wordpress.com for the next open submission period.
a2cInsight: Distinctive because it's peer-edited by teens for teens internationally, but its irregular publication cadence means it's a nice-to-have, not a reliable annual target.

name: The WEIGHT Journal
prerequisites: High-school-age writers worldwide; poetry, spoken word/slam, fiction, hybrid work; no submissions on a student's behalf by teachers/parents.
costNote: No fee to submit.
cadence: Reopens roughly each September after a summer hiatus; single-genre submissions only.
howToStart: Wait for the fall reopening and submit via theweightjournal.com/submissions.
a2cInsight: Known for embracing raw, emotionally honest and spoken-word-adjacent work, giving it a distinct niche voice among teen journals rather than broad prestige.

name: One Teen Story
prerequisites: Ages 13-19; original unpublished short fiction, 2,000-4,500 words, with a teen protagonist; one submission per person per window, sorted by age group (13-15, 16-17, 18-19).
costNote: Free entry via Submittable; run as a contest with cash prize.
cadence: Rolling/periodic contest windows (published quarterly-ish); check one-story.com for current dates.
howToStart: Submit a polished short story via the One Story Submittable manager under the One Teen Story Contest category.
a2cInsight: Backed by the well-regarded adult literary magazine One Story, so a win/publication here is a genuinely competitive fiction credential, not just a participation journal.

name: Stone Soup
prerequisites: Writers and artists through age 18 (skews younger, core contributor base is 13 and under); poetry, flash fiction, short stories, novellas, visual art, music composition; no AI tools allowed.
costNote: Free for magazine subscribers; non-subscribers pay a modest reading fee for some categories (expedited response costs more).
cadence: Rolling submissions via Submittable; responses in about 10-12 weeks.
howToStart: Submit via stonesoup.com/how-to-submit-writing-and-art-to-stone-soup, noting the reading fee for non-subscribers.
a2cInsight: One of the oldest kid/tween literary magazines (founded 1972, first issue 1973) with real institutional reputation, but it skews toward middle-schoolers and younger, so high schoolers should treat it as an earlier-stage credential.

name: The Echo Teen Art & Lit Mag
prerequisites: Ages 13-19 worldwide; poetry, fiction, nonfiction, comics, scripts, art; max 5,000 words; no AI-generated or explicit content.
costNote: No submission fee; pays contributors ($15-$50 depending on category).
cadence: Themed submission windows plus year-round unthemed and cover-art calls; novel submissions June-July.
howToStart: Check open windows at echolitmag.com/submit and submit accordingly.
a2cInsight: One of the few teen lit mags that actually pays contributors, which adds legitimacy and makes it a nice combination of craft credit and small income.

name: Ember: A Journal of Luminous Things
prerequisites: Writers ages 10 to adult, with submissions from readers/writers ages 10-18 strongly encouraged; poetry, fiction, creative nonfiction.
costNote: No submission fee; pays contributors 2 cents/word or $20 minimum.
cadence: Semiannual journal; windows open ~April and ~October each year (confirm exact dates on site), closed on a rolling basis between windows.
howToStart: Review submission guidelines at emberjournal.org and submit through their online system during the next open window.
a2cInsight: A paying market open to a wide age range including middle schoolers, useful as an early, legitimate first-publication credit before targeting more selective teen-only journals; only about 3% of submissions are accepted, so it's more competitive than its low profile suggests.

name: The Interlochen Review
prerequisites: High school writers, singer-songwriters, and artists (grades 9-12 or postgraduate year) worldwide; fiction, poetry, nonfiction, hybrid genres, songs, scripts, visual art.
costNote: No submission fee.
cadence: Annual submission window opening around January each year (e.g. January 10 - February 21 for the 2026 cycle).
howToStart: Watch interlochenreview.org for the January opening and submit through their online form.
a2cInsight: Produced by Interlochen Arts Academy, a top arts boarding school, so it carries real cachet in creative-writing and arts-admissions circles despite being open to outside submitters.

name: Skipping Stones
prerequisites: Ages 7-18 (magazine submissions) and 7-17 (Youth Honor Awards); essays, stories, poems, letters, art on multicultural and nature/ecological themes, up to 1,000 words for prose and 30 lines for poems.
costNote: No fee to submit; separate Youth Honor Award has no entry fee either.
cadence: Rolling submissions for the magazine; the Youth Honor Awards run on an annual cycle (spring deadline, winners announced later in the year).
howToStart: Email a submission with a short cultural-background cover note to editor@skippingstones.org, or enter the annual Youth Honor Awards.
a2cInsight: A niche, values-driven nonprofit magazine — a nice fit for students whose work centers on multicultural or environmental themes, though its national profile is modest.

name: The Foredge Review
prerequisites: Ages 13-18 worldwide, with an editorial focus on Asian and pan-Asian perspectives; poetry, prose, creative nonfiction, flash fiction on any theme.
costNote: No submission fee.
cadence: Biannual online journal; rolling/periodic submission windows announced on their site and Tumblr.
howToStart: Check foredgereview.com for the current open call and submit per posted guidelines.
a2cInsight: Distinctive for its pan-Asian focus and diaspora perspective, useful for students wanting a journal with a clear thematic identity rather than a generalist one.

name: The Daphne Review
prerequisites: Ages 13-18; essays, interviews, poems, short plays, any visual art medium; up to 3 pieces or 9,000 words total, submitted as one file.
costNote: No submission fee; separate paid mentorship program is optional.
cadence: Rolling submissions, published in spring and fall issues; also runs periodic contests.
howToStart: Submit via thedaphnereview.org/submissions on a rolling basis.
a2cInsight: A solid mid-tier journal with an added mentorship track, giving motivated students both a publication credit and access to working writers as mentors.

name: The Cloudscent Journal
prerequisites: Ages roughly 12-25; fiction, creative nonfiction, poetry, digital art.
costNote: No submission fee.
cadence: Periodic submission windows (opens/closes intermittently); check the site for current status.
howToStart: Check cloudscentjournal.com's guidelines page for the next open window before submitting.
a2cInsight: A small, youth-run platform useful as a low-stakes first publication credit rather than a name-brand journal.

name: Marmalade Lit
ageNote: Founded in 2025, so it is very new with a thin track record so far.
prerequisites: International youth writers/artists (teen-focused); genres vary by call.
costNote: No submission fee.
cadence: Periodic themed contests (e.g., Spring Contest) and rolling submissions; check site for current call.
howToStart: Check marmaladelit.com for current submission or contest guidelines.
a2cInsight: Brand-new (2025) international youth journal — a fine place to start building a publication record, but too young to carry name recognition yet.

name: Raiya Magazine
ageNote: Founded 2025; still building its reputation and archive.
prerequisites: Youth writers/editors; fiction, poetry, critiques, essays, visual art.
costNote: No submission fee and no payment to contributors.
cadence: Periodic themed issues (e.g., 'Chasing Aurora') with posted deadlines; check the magazine's site or Chill Subs for current call.
howToStart: Check Raiya Magazine's current submission call at raiyamagazine.odoo.com and submit before the themed deadline.
a2cInsight: A fresh, youth-run magazine with a fairly high reported acceptance rate — accessible for a first credit, not yet a prestige name.

name: Blue Crystal Literary Magazine
prerequisites: Open to young writers; poetry, short stories, photography; some calls include Spanish-language poetry.
costNote: No submission fee found for its themed contests.
cadence: Runs frequent rotating themed contests/issues (e.g., seasonal, dark poetry, Spanish-language poetry) with regular deadlines roughly every few weeks.
howToStart: Check bluecrystallit.uk/submit for the current themed contest and deadline.
a2cInsight: A small UK-based youth zine running frequent micro-contests — good for prolific young writers wanting many quick publication credits, but low individual prestige.

name: Hanging Loose Press High School Section
prerequisites: High-school-age writers, submitting their own work directly (not through teachers); up to six poems, or flash fiction, or short fiction (up to 1,000 words).
costNote: No submission fee; submit by email.
cadence: Rolling submissions by email for inclusion in each print issue of Hanging Loose.
howToStart: Email a single Word or PDF file of your work to HighSchool@hangingloosepress.com per hangingloosepress.com guidelines, noting your school and age in the message.
a2cInsight: Unique among teen outlets for placing student writers in the same physical issue as adult professional poets for over 50 years, giving a distinctive real-world publishing credential beyond youth-only journals.

name: National History Bee & Bowl
prerequisites: Broad knowledge of world and US history; buzzer-quiz practice helpful. Open to elementary through high school (JV for 10th grade and under, Varsity for 11th-12th).
costNote: Regional tournament entry fees typically $20-50/student; Nationals in Arlington, VA adds travel costs and higher event fees.
cadence: Regional tournaments late October-early April; National Championships late April in Arlington, VA.
howToStart: Register a team or individual through iacompetitions.com/vjvhistorybee, find a nearby regional tournament, or start a school history bowl club.
a2cInsight: A strong showing (especially Nationals qualification) signals deep content mastery and quick recall, but admissions officers weight it below research-based history credentials like NHD.

name: International History Bee and Bowl
prerequisites: Same buzzer-quiz format as National History Bee/Bowl; open to students worldwide outside the US.
costNote: Regional entry fees vary by division/region; international divisions may have higher costs.
cadence: Regional tournaments held across Asia, Canada, Europe, Australia/NZ throughout the school year.
howToStart: Find your region's division site (e.g., ihbbeurope.com, ihbbcanada.com, iacompetitionsasia.com) and register for a local tournament.
a2cInsight: Useful for international students to show rigorous engagement with history outside a national curriculum; less recognized by US admissions than domestic NHD or NHB nationals.

name: International History Olympiad
prerequisites: Qualify via participation and results in IAC History Bee/Bowl tournaments (Asia, Canada, Europe, USA) or an Olympiad Qualifying Exam.
costNote: In-person biennial event requires international travel, lodging, and registration fees.
cadence: Biennial, held in odd-numbered summers (next in 2027).
howToStart: Compete well in a qualifying History Bee/Bowl tournament, or take the Olympiad Qualifying Exam listed at historyolympiad.com.
a2cInsight: One of the few truly international history quiz events; qualifying and attending signals sustained multi-year commitment, valuable but niche for admissions.

name: US Academic WorldQuest
prerequisites: Four-person high school team sponsored by a local World Affairs Council; broad current-events and global-affairs knowledge.
costNote: Local World Affairs Council registration fees are typically modest; national round trip to DC is fully funded for winners.
cadence: Local competitions held each winter; national finals in Washington, DC each April.
howToStart: Contact your local World Affairs Council in fall to register a team; study the official WorldQuest guide covering ten thematic categories.
a2cInsight: Signals genuine global-affairs literacy and teamwork; respected in international relations/policy-focused applications but not a nationally famous name outside that niche.

name: Gilder Lehrman History Scholars / Richard Gilder History Prize
prerequisites: Attend a Gilder Lehrman Affiliate School and be nominated by a teacher as a promising, passionate history student.
costNote: Nomination-based; no cost to student.
cadence: Annual nomination by Affiliate School teachers.
howToStart: Ask your history teacher whether your school is a Gilder Lehrman Affiliate and express interest in being nominated.
a2cInsight: A teacher-nominated honor rather than a competition win, so it reads as a solid but secondary recommendation-style credential, not a marquee achievement.

name: Ink of Ages Fiction Prize (World History Encyclopedia)
prerequisites: Write an 800-1,000 word historical or mythological short story (ages 13-17 youth category) set at least 50 years in the past; no AI-assisted writing allowed.
costNote: Free to enter internationally.
cadence: Annual; recent cycle results posted for 2026.
howToStart: Read the rules and enter directly at fictionprize.worldhistory.org.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, free, globally open creative-writing contest sponsored by a respected reference publisher and Oxford University Press, good for building a historical-fiction portfolio, though not a heavyweight admissions name.

name: World Historian Student Essay Competition (World History Association)
prerequisites: K-12 students write a roughly 1,000-word essay on how studying world history shaped their understanding of the world.
costNote: No entry fee; WHA membership not required.
cadence: Annual deadline of May 1.
howToStart: Prepare a double-spaced, 12-pt Times New Roman essay per WHA rules and submit by email/post before the May 1 deadline via members.thewha.org.
a2cInsight: A modest but genuine national essay award ($500 prize) run by a real scholarly association; solid for a student's first history-writing credential but not widely recognized in admissions.

name: United States Academic Decathlon (USAD)
prerequisites: Nine-student team spanning Honors, Scholastic, and Varsity GPA divisions; annual theme spans ten subjects including Social Science, Art, Music, and Essay.
costNote: Team registration and materials fees plus travel to state/national rounds.
cadence: Regular season through winter; state competitions in early spring, National Finals in spring.
howToStart: Join or start a school Academic Decathlon team through your state USAD affiliate; team composition and GPA divisions apply.
a2cInsight: A true academic decathlon-style humanities-plus-STEM event with real national prestige, but team-based structure means individual credit depends on team performance and role.

name: National Civics Bee
prerequisites: 6th-8th graders write a four-paragraph essay proposing a community-improvement idea grounded in civic principles.
costNote: Free entry; essay contest is the qualifying round.
cadence: Essay deadline early February; regional bees in spring; state and national finals follow.
howToStart: Check if your state participates via civics.uschamberfoundation.org and submit the essay before the local deadline.
a2cInsight: A fast-growing, well-funded middle-school civics contest (top prize $100,000 into a 529 plan) that is highly credible for younger students but too new to carry major weight for high-school admissions yet.

name: We the People: The Citizen and the Constitution
prerequisites: Requires a class or team using the We the People constitutional curriculum, culminating in simulated congressional hearing testimony.
costNote: Curriculum materials often free through state civic education partners; travel costs for finals.
cadence: Class-based curriculum culminating in state competitions and National Finals/Invitational each spring in Washington, DC.
howToStart: Ask a social studies teacher to adopt the We the People curriculum through civiced.org or your state civic education center.
a2cInsight: A rigorous, respected mock-congressional-hearing program that rewards public speaking and constitutional knowledge; strong for civics/pre-law-minded applicants, though tied to whether your school offers the class.

name: National High School Mock Trial Championship
prerequisites: Grades 9-12; team of 8 competitors plus timekeeper preparing a new case each year with attorney/teacher coaches.
costNote: State/regional entry fees plus travel for national qualifiers.
cadence: District, regional and state tournaments during the school year; national championship each May.
howToStart: Join or start a school mock trial team through your state bar association's mock trial program.
a2cInsight: A genuinely prestigious pre-law credential, especially state or national qualification; well recognized by admissions officers interested in legal/civic engagement.

name: Certamen (National Junior Classical League)
prerequisites: Membership in an NJCL Latin/Greek/Classics chapter; knowledge of Latin, Roman/Greek history, and mythology.
costNote: NJCL convention registration and travel fees; local chapters often participate for little cost.
cadence: State and local tournaments during the year; National Convention each summer.
howToStart: Join your school's Junior Classical League chapter (or start one) and register for local/state Certamen tournaments.
a2cInsight: A well-established quiz-bowl format within classics education; a legitimate niche credential for students pursuing Latin/classics, less recognized outside that specific track.

name: National Geography Bee (nationalgeobee.org)
prerequisites: High school students with strong geography knowledge; unaffiliated with the discontinued National Geographic GeoBee.
costNote: Registration fees for qualifying rounds; travel to Cambridge, MA finals for finalists.
cadence: Inaugural competition held May 2026; annual going forward.
howToStart: Register through nationalgeobee.org when qualifying rounds open.
a2cInsight: A brand-new revival competition (first held 2026) filling the gap left by the discontinued National Geographic GeoBee; not yet an established admissions name given its youth.

name: International Geography Bee
prerequisites: Primary and secondary students; open internationally, especially strong presence in Europe, Africa, and Asia.
costNote: Regional entry fees; costs vary by division.
cadence: Regional tournaments held throughout the school year across Europe, Africa, Asia, and other regions.
howToStart: Find your regional division site (internationalgeographybee.com or ihbbeurope.com) and register for a local tournament.
