howToStart: Make a short film (up to 12 minutes for the middle-school category) and submit via FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/KIDSFIRSTFilmFestival), noting your grade-level category.
a2cInsight: One of the longest-running dedicated K-12 student film showcases in the US (about a third of the ~300 films accepted yearly are student work, with a global partner network of 30+ venues); solid, legitimate but not high-prestige — good for a first or second festival selection on a resume rather than a flagship credential.

name: Real to Reel Global Youth Film Festival
prerequisites: High school (14-18) and college (19-24) aged youth filmmakers; short films addressing a local or global social issue (e.g., foster care, homelessness, immigration, identity).
costNote: Entry fees vary by deadline tier on FilmFreeway, generally under $50.
cadence: Annual; rolling FilmFreeway submission deadlines with a live Los Angeles screening event each fall.
howToStart: Produce a short film addressing a social issue that matters to your community and submit via FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/realtoreelglobal), run by the nonprofit Better Youth.
a2cInsight: An issue-driven youth festival (now in its 10th+ year) backed by Better Youth, a respected LA nonprofit serving foster and system-impacted youth; a legitimate, socially conscious addition to a film portfolio and a strong fit for students pairing filmmaking with advocacy, though it is mid-tier rather than a nationally famous name.

name: Reel 2 Real International Film Festival for Youth
prerequisites: Youth-made films (fiction, documentary, or animation of any length) suitable for viewers 19 and under; filmmakers must be 19 or younger.
costNote: FilmFreeway entry fees are modest; festival based in Vancouver, Canada.
cadence: Annual, competitive international event, running since 1999.
howToStart: Submit a youth-made short film via FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/Reel2RealInternationalFilmFestivalforYouth); films of any nationality are welcome.
a2cInsight: A well-established Canadian international youth media festival (media-education nonprofit-run since 1999, includes a week-long in-school program for grades 4-12); good for students seeking an international festival credit beyond US-centric options.

name: American Youth Film Festival (AYFF)
prerequisites: Open to elementary, middle, high school, or college students of any background; documentary category capped at 15 minutes; separate categories including movie posters.
costNote: Early-bird entry fees from ~$20 (elementary) to $50 (college); scales by category.
cadence: Annual; FilmFreeway submissions with an Atlanta, GA screening event.
howToStart: Create a short film or movie poster and submit through FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/AmericanYouthFilmFestival) for the appropriate school-level category.
a2cInsight: A small, regionally rooted (Atlanta) youth festival — a legitimate but low-prestige credential, best used as one of several festival entries rather than a standalone highlight. Note: submit only via the FilmFreeway link; the festival's own americanyouthfilmfest.org domain currently shows signs of being compromised with injected spam content, so avoid linking students there directly.

name: EDU Film Festival
prerequisites: Director(s) must be ages 14-18 at time of production; short films up to 20 minutes or micro-films up to 1 minute, across many genres.
costNote: FilmFreeway fees apply, though free-submission promo codes are sometimes offered.
cadence: Annual; based in Minneapolis, MN with FilmFreeway submissions, since 2007.
howToStart: Produce a short or micro-film and submit via the EDU Sparqfest platform on FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/EDUFilmFestivalMinnesota).
a2cInsight: Minnesota's only school-sponsored, statewide high school film festival (founded 2007, run by The Northern Film Alliance); a genre-broad, teacher-run event with a strong classroom-education focus and a real two-day industry-networking festival experience — solid regional credential, not nationally prestigious.

name: Boston International Kids Film Festival (BIKFF)
prerequisites: Currently enrolled in middle or high school for the dedicated student-film award category; films of any length (under 30 minutes most competitive).
costNote: FilmFreeway entry fees apply, generally modest.
cadence: Annual, held each November in the Boston area, since 2013.
howToStart: Submit a completed short film via FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/BostonInternationalKidsFilmFestival), noting the age-appropriateness rating (12+ or under 12).
a2cInsight: A respected New England youth/family film festival run by Filmmakers Collaborative (a nonprofit serving independent filmmakers since 1987); more than half its lineup is student work (18 and under), giving middle-schoolers a realistic shot at an official selection.

name: Rochester Teen Film Festival
prerequisites: High school teens ages 13-18; videos made during the current school year, any genre, under 10 minutes.
costNote: Free to submit via FilmFreeway.
cadence: Annual, run by WXXI public media with an August screening at The Little Theatre in Rochester, NY.
howToStart: Make a short film during the school year and submit via FilmFreeway or WXXI's entry form before the summer deadline.
a2cInsight: A well-run but strictly regional (Rochester, NY) teen festival backed by public broadcaster WXXI and The Little Theatre; good local recognition and small cash prizes (including a Best of Festival and a Social Justice award), minimal name recognition outside the region.

name: NFFTY 48-Hour Film Off
prerequisites: High school students in the US or Canada; teams write, shoot, and edit a 3-5 minute film in 48 hours using an assigned genre, line of dialogue, character, and prop.
costNote: Team registration around $50, though this includes multiple festival passes valued higher.
cadence: Annual, tied to the NFFTY festival weekend in Seattle each spring.
howToStart: Assemble a small production team, register for the NFFTY 48-Hour Film Off, and be ready to produce a full short film in one weekend once prompts are revealed.
a2cInsight: A fast-paced, high-intensity team filmmaking sprint (winning team gets $750 for their school's media program plus festival passes) that demonstrates real production hustle under pressure; a fun, legitimate credential for a resume but a niche one most admissions readers won't recognize by name.

name: High School 48 Hour Film Project
prerequisites: High school student teams; write, shoot, and edit a 3-7 minute film in exactly 48 hours incorporating a required genre, character, line, and prop.
costNote: Team-based registration fee (varies by city chapter, typically $50-150 per team).
cadence: Annual, city-by-city events (part of the global 48 Hour Film Project franchise) held on various weekends across the year.
howToStart: Find a participating city chapter at highschool48.com, register a team, and prepare gear/crew roles ahead of the assigned production weekend.
a2cInsight: Part of the globally recognized 48 Hour Film Project franchise (running for two decades), giving it more brand recognition than most local contests; a strong demonstration of teamwork and rapid production skill, though winners are city-specific rather than nationally ranked.

name: Youth Free Expression Film Contest (NCAC)
prerequisites: Entrants must be 19 or younger and US-based (residency, not citizenship); film under 3 minutes on a contemporary free-expression/First Amendment issue; AI-generated video not allowed.
costNote: Free to enter via FilmFreeway.
cadence: Annual; run by the National Coalition Against Censorship for over 20 years (22nd edition as of the current cycle).
howToStart: Choose a free-speech/censorship issue relevant today, script and produce a film under 3 minutes, and submit via FilmFreeway (filmfreeway.com/yfepfilmcontest).
a2cInsight: A niche but substantive contest tied to a respected free-expression advocacy nonprofit (NCAC, founded 1974) and co-presented with the New York Film Academy; strong fit for students interested in civics/law/journalism, with real cash prizes ($1,000/$500/$250) and an NYFA scholarship for first place.

name: Austin Film Festival Young Filmmakers Competition
prerequisites: Filmmakers ages 13-18, must be enrolled in middle or high school during production (proof of enrollment required); short films in narrative, documentary, or animation categories, no entry limit.
costNote: Free to enter; attending the festival in Austin to be recognized adds travel cost, though winners receive travel reimbursement and a hotel stay.
cadence: Annual, films screened during the main Austin Film Festival in late October/early November.
howToStart: Complete a short film after the eligibility window opens, then submit through the Austin Film Festival's Young Filmmakers portal ahead of the fall deadline.
a2cInsight: Riding on the coattails of the well-regarded Austin Film Festival (known for screenwriting), this youth sidebar carries more industry credibility than most teen-only festivals because of the parent festival's reputation; the winner gets festival badges, an awards luncheon, and travel reimbursement to attend.

name: SCAD Savannah Film Festival Student Showcase
prerequisites: High school and college-level student filmmakers; short films across fiction, documentary, and animation styles completed for degree/coursework requirements.
costNote: Submission fees apply (tiered by deadline); festival held in Savannah, GA with SCAD (a major art/design university) as host.
cadence: Annual; submissions open each January with a rolling deadline through late June, festival in late October.
howToStart: Complete a short film and submit through the SCAD Savannah Film Festival's official submission portal (filmfest.scad.edu) before the relevant deadline tier.
a2cInsight: Backed by SCAD, one of the most prominent art/film schools in the US, giving student selections real industry visibility at a major festival (26+ years running, 2,500+ submissions/year) that also hosts A-list talent — a strong line item for a serious film-arts applicant, though high schoolers compete in the same pool as college filmmakers.

name: Colorado High School Film Festival
prerequisites: High-school-age students (Colorado and out-of-state/international eligible); short films of any genre.
costNote: Free to submit via the Scholastic Arts portal for Colorado residents; out-of-state/international entrants submit via FilmFreeway (modest platform fee). Hosted by University of Colorado Denver's College of Arts & Media.
cadence: Annual, screened on the CU Denver campus (2026 deadline January 6; finalists announced by February 15).
howToStart: Submit a completed short film via the Scholastic Arts portal (Colorado residents) or FilmFreeway (non-Colorado) ahead of the annual January deadline.
a2cInsight: A university-hosted showcase that's a nice on-ramp to college film programs; twenty finalists get scholarships to a filmmaking camp and top films get gear prizes plus a slot at CU Denver's Cinefest. Modest national recognition, but genuinely accepts out-of-state submissions despite the state-sounding name.

name: Arizona Student Film Festival
prerequisites: Grade-school and high-school students statewide in Arizona; short films under 8 minutes.
costNote: Low-cost FilmFreeway submission fee.
cadence: Annual; submissions typically due by end of January (2026 deadline: January 31).
howToStart: Produce a short film under 8 minutes and submit via FilmFreeway before the January deadline.
a2cInsight: A friendly, accessible statewide festival good for Arizona students building an early film portfolio; best film wins a $1,000 scholarship and selected filmmakers get Phoenix Film Festival passes, but reach outside the state is limited.

name: One Earth Young Filmmakers Contest
prerequisites: Students grade 3 through age 25; live-action or animated shorts on environmental/sustainability themes (animation minimum 45 seconds, 3-8 minutes total).
costNote: No submission fee; entries via FilmFreeway.
cadence: Annual; deadline typically late June (2026 deadline: June 25), tied to the Chicago-based One Earth Film Festival.
howToStart: Create a short environmentally-themed film and submit via the official contest page at oneearthfilmfest.org before the June deadline.
a2cInsight: A free, environment-focused youth contest with real cash prizes ($100-$1,000) at the middle/high-school level; a great fit for students building an environmental-advocacy or sustainability narrative alongside filmmaking.

name: JEA National Student Media Contests (Broadcast Categories)
prerequisites: High school students whose journalism adviser is a JEA member; broadcast categories (Contests 36-50) include news stories, sports coverage, features, podcasts, and live reporting formats.
costNote: $30 per student entry; student must be a registered convention attendee with a JEA-member adviser.
cadence: Tied to the JEA/NSPA National High School Journalism Convention (fall and spring).
howToStart: Join your school's broadcast journalism program, have your adviser become a JEA member, and submit a broadcast package or on-site contest entry tied to a national convention.
a2cInsight: A respected, journalism-education-establishment contest (JEA is the leading national scholastic journalism organization); Superior ratings are a real, recognized honor among high school journalism/broadcast programs, though requiring adviser membership and convention attendance limits access.

name: National Scholastic Press Association (NSPA) National Pacemaker Award — Broadcast
prerequisites: High school (and separately, junior high/middle school) broadcast/news program with NSPA membership and a substantial body of work (three full news programs) submitted by the program/adviser.
costNote: No separate entry fee beyond current NSPA membership; Broadcast News entries submit three full student-produced news programs.
cadence: Annual; broadcast category deadline around early June, finalists announced in September, winners announced at the fall and spring JEA/NSPA National High School Journalism Conventions.
howToStart: Build a strong, consistent student broadcast news program over the school year, ensure your program has NSPA membership, then have your adviser submit a Pacemaker portfolio entry.
a2cInsight: Unofficially called the 'Pulitzer Prize of student journalism' — winning a Pacemaker is one of the most prestigious honors in scholastic broadcast journalism and is genuinely recognized by journalism-focused admissions readers, but it's a program-level (not individual) award requiring sustained institutional output and NSPA membership.

name: Student Murrow Awards (RTDNA)
prerequisites: Full-time high school (or undergraduate) student producing broadcast/digital journalism content; categories span audio, video, podcasts, and digital reporting.
costNote: $20 per entry for RTDNA members, $40 per entry for non-members.
cadence: Annual; entry period opens December for prior-year work, final deadline in February, national winners announced in summer, gala in October.
howToStart: Produce a strong piece of broadcast or digital journalism through a school program, then submit it through RTDNA's official Murrow Awards entry process.
a2cInsight: The Murrow Awards are among the most prestigious honors in professional broadcast journalism, and the student division extends real credibility to serious high school journalists — a standout credential for journalism-focused applicants, though it is competitive and requires access to a strong school broadcast program.

name: SIGGRAPH Computer Animation Festival — Best Student Project
prerequisites: Technically open to any creator, but realistically requires advanced 3D animation/VFX skills; entrants check a 'Student Project' box to qualify for the Best Student Project prize.
costNote: Submission is free across all categories; attending the SIGGRAPH conference itself is costly if pursued, but is not required to submit.
cadence: Annual, tied to the SIGGRAPH conference each summer (2026 edition: July 19-23 in Los Angeles; submissions due mid-March).
howToStart: Develop advanced computer-animation or VFX skills (via tools like Blender, Maya, or Houdini), produce a polished short piece, and submit via the SIGGRAPH Computer Animation Festival's official call for submissions (free).
a2cInsight: This is a genuinely elite, industry-gatekept honor (an Academy Award-qualifying festival) that virtually no high schooler wins, but an exceptional student with serious 3D/VFX chops could realistically submit — a true reach credential that signals top-tier technical animation skill if achieved.

name: Sony World Photography Awards – Youth Competition
prerequisites: Open to anyone aged 19 or under on the closing date; those under 16 need parental/guardian consent; no school affiliation required.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; opens June, closes January (2026 cycle: June 1, 2026 - January 5, 2027, winners announced April 2027).
howToStart: Register at worldphoto.org and submit up to 3 images responding to the year's theme through the online portal.
a2cInsight: One of the most internationally visible youth photo competitions run by a major, well-regarded organization (World Photography Organisation) — a shortlist or win is a genuinely strong, globally-judged credential.

name: National Wildlife Federation Photo Contest – Young Nature Photographers
prerequisites: Ages 13-17 for the Young Nature Photographers category.
costNote: Modest entry fees per photo support NWF's conservation work; exact amount varies by year.
cadence: Annual (55th annual in 2026), submissions roughly mid-January to late March; winners announced in the fall issue of National Wildlife magazine.
howToStart: Create an account at photocontest.nwf.org and submit wildlife/nature images to the Young Nature Photographers category.
a2cInsight: A respected legacy conservation-org contest; solid for an environmental-science or biology-adjacent portfolio but not a top-tier admissions signal on its own.

name: Environmental Photographer of the Year – Young EPOTY
prerequisites: Open to photographers aged 21 and under; no professional experience required.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, entries typically open spring/summer with a fall deadline; note the competition did not run in 2024 but resumed in 2025.
howToStart: Enter via the CIWEM/EPOTY website with a single image reflecting an environmental theme (climate, sustainability, human-nature relationship).
a2cInsight: Backed by a credible UK professional environmental body (CIWEM) and sponsored by Nikon — a legitimate, internationally-judged environmental-storytelling credential, though it has occasionally skipped a year (e.g., 2024), so check current-year status before committing.

name: UNESCO Youth Eyes on the Silk Roads International Photo Contest
prerequisites: Two age divisions: 14-17 and 18-25; minors need written guardian permission.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, typically opens in spring and closes mid-year (8th edition ran in 2026).
howToStart: Submit 1-20 photos on the year's cultural-heritage theme via the official contest website (unescosilkroadphotocontest.org).
a2cInsight: A genuine UN-agency-run global competition with real international reach — a strong credential for students interested in cultural heritage, geography, or international relations. The 14-17 division makes it accessible to younger high schoolers (and some middle schoolers who are 14).

name: PX3 – Prix de la Photographie Paris (Student/Non-Professional category)
prerequisites: No formal grade restriction; students enter the non-professional/student division.
costNote: Non-professional/student entry fees are roughly $20 per single image or $40 per series/book, with a 20% discount for additional categories.
cadence: Annual, entries typically open in fall/winter with a mid-year deadline (2026 final deadline: July 22).
howToStart: Register at px3.fr and submit images to one of the student/non-professional categories.
a2cInsight: A long-running, well-known international salon-style contest, but its pay-per-entry model and huge volume of categories make it more of a portfolio-building exercise than a highly selective honor.

name: Comedy Wildlife Photography Awards – Junior & Young Photographer Categories
prerequisites: Junior category: age 16 or under; Young Photographer category: age 25 or under.
costNote: Free entry.
cadence: Annual, entries open roughly spring through a June deadline (2026 deadline: June 30).
howToStart: Submit up to 10 images and 2 videos of humorous wildlife photography via comedywildlifephoto.com in the Junior or Young Photographer category.
a2cInsight: A media-famous, high-visibility contest (regularly covered by major outlets) that's genuinely fun and low-pressure — good portfolio color but not a heavyweight academic credential.

name: SPOA Student Photographer Contest (in partnership with Sony)
prerequisites: Open to all US students in grades 6-12 (public, private, or home school).
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual; opens February, entries close in April, winners announced mid-April.
howToStart: Submit up to 2 images per category (Action, Candid, Detail, Dominant, Reaction shots) through the SPOA website.
a2cInsight: Run by a niche scholastic-photography trade association; a nice free, MS-accessible on-ramp but carries little admissions weight beyond demonstrating consistent interest.

name: Jostens Photo Contest
prerequisites: Current middle/junior high or high school students, at least 13 years old, legal residents of the US, DC, or Canada (excluding Quebec); minors need parental permission.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, January 1 - March 15, winners announced in April.
howToStart: Submit photos through the official Jostens photography contest portal (jostens.com/photography/contest).
a2cInsight: A yearbook-company-sponsored contest that's easy to enter and MS-friendly, but it's a minor commercial promotion rather than an academically respected credential.

name: NIMH "Speaking Up About Mental Health" Essay Contest
prerequisites: US high school students ages 16-18; essay addresses mental health stigma, especially in racial/ethnic minority communities.
costNote: Free to enter; total prize pool around $15,000 in past cycles (24 gold/silver/bronze/honorable-mention winners from 370+ submissions in a recent cycle).
cadence: Annual; check challenge.gov for current cycle dates.
howToStart: Write a short reflective essay on youth mental health stigma and access to care; submit through the official NIH/challenge.gov portal (contact MHHighSchoolEssay@mail.nih.gov for details).
a2cInsight: A federally-run (NIH/NIMH) contest with real cash prizes and legitimate mental-health policy relevance — solid for students interested in psychology, public health, or health equity, though narrowly age-restricted (16-18).

name: Minds Underground Psychology Essay Competition
prerequisites: Students aged 8-18 worldwide; essays 1000-1500 words in English using Harvard citation format.
costNote: No entry fee found on the official competition page; open to students aged 8-18 worldwide (senior prizes aimed at Year 12 but open to younger entrants).
cadence: Annual, spring deadline (2026 cycle: Round 1 Feb 8, Round 2 Feb 22, essay submission deadline April 3, 2026).
howToStart: Choose a prompt from the year's Minds Underground psychology topics, write and submit an original essay via mindsunderground.com before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A newer UK-based essay competition riding the same wave as John Locke — legitimate, well-organized, and genuinely open to middle-schoolers as well as high schoolers, but far less established/selective, so treat it as a supplementary credential rather than a headline one.

name: Pi Gamma Mu International Honor Society in Social Sciences
prerequisites: Primarily for college students (junior/senior standing, upper 35% of class, 20 semester hours in social science, 3.0 GPA) — high schoolers generally not eligible except via rare affiliated programs.
costNote: One-time lifetime membership fee of about $50, per official site.
cadence: Rolling induction, chapter-dependent.
howToStart: Mostly relevant once in college; high schoolers should note it as a longer-term goal and focus on social-science coursework/extracurriculars now.
a2cInsight: The oldest social-science honor society (est. 1924) is genuinely prestigious at the college level, but it is not a realistic near-term target for a high schooler — include only as an aspirational marker.

name: IMPULSE: The Premier Undergraduate Neuroscience Journal
prerequisites: Aimed at undergraduates but does not explicitly exclude advanced high schoolers; original research or reviews require a faculty mentor acknowledgment.
costNote: No publication fee.
cadence: Rolling submissions; articles published online as accepted.
howToStart: Conduct original neuroscience/psychology research with a mentor, write it up per the journal's format, and submit via the IMPULSE PubPub portal.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, free, faculty-vetted undergraduate neuroscience journal (est. 2003, indexed in DOAJ) — exceptionally strong (and rare) for a high schooler to break into given its primary undergraduate audience, so best suited to advanced students with a real mentor-guided study.

name: National Social Science Association (NSSA) Student Paper Competition
prerequisites: Primarily undergraduate/graduate students; ambitious high schoolers with a faculty mentor could theoretically submit but should confirm eligibility with organizers.
costNote: Conference registration fee applies (undergraduate rate ~$200, plus travel); high schoolers not the primary audience.
cadence: Annual, spring conference; papers due mid-February.
howToStart: Contact the National Social Science Association directly to confirm high school eligibility before preparing a submission; if eligible, submit a peer-reviewable paper by the February deadline.
a2cInsight: Really a college-oriented conference/competition — not a realistic or cost-effective target for most high schoolers given the registration cost and primarily undergraduate audience.

name: Society for Applied Anthropology Student Endowed Award
prerequisites: Undergraduate/graduate students; not designed for high schoolers.
cadence: Annual, tied to SfAA's spring conference.
howToStart: Not realistic for high schoolers; noted only as a longer-term goal for those pursuing applied anthropology in college.
a2cInsight: College/graduate-level award, not a genuine target for a high school portfolio; skip unless already in an undergraduate program.

name: International Linguistics Olympiad (IOL)
prerequisites: Must place on your country's national team (usually top 4-8 students) via that country's national linguistics olympiad; no formal linguistics coursework required.
costNote: Free to compete; national team travel/lodging to host country typically funded by national organizing body or sponsors, though some students cover partial costs.
cadence: Annual, held late July/early Aug; 2026 edition (23rd) hosted in Bucharest, Romania (July 26-Aug 2), organized with University of Bucharest's Faculty of Letters. Qualification is via each country's national olympiad (e.g., NACLO, UKLO, PLO).
howToStart: Compete in and win your national olympiad (NACLO for US/Canada Anglophone, UKLO for UK, PLO for India, OzCLO for Australia, etc.) to qualify for team selection.
a2cInsight: The single most prestigious credential in this niche - an IOL medal is a genuine, internationally-recognized elite achievement comparable to IMO/IOI medals, though far less known to generalist admissions readers than math/science olympiads.

name: UK Linguistics Olympiad (UKLO)
prerequisites: Open to UK secondary (and some primary) school pupils; no prior linguistics knowledge needed.
costNote: Entirely free for every school to enter, per official statement.
cadence: Annual, four levels (Breakthrough/Foundation/Intermediate/Advanced) forming Round 1, typically Jan-Feb, with top 5% of Advanced entrants advancing to Round 2 and potential IOL team selection; see 2026 archives/noticeboard for exact dates.
howToStart: Register your school (or as an individual home-educated student) via uklo.org before the entry deadline; take the qualifying round paper.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded UK national STEM-adjacent olympiad; a Gold/high placement is a legible UK admissions credential, and top scorers advance to represent Team UK at IOL.

name: OzCLO - Australian Computational and Linguistics Olympiad
prerequisites: Australian students in Years 7-12, competing in teams of up to four (separate Junior [7-10] and Senior [11-12] divisions).
costNote: Regional round entry fees are typically modest and vary by host institution; some sites are free.
cadence: Annual; Regional Round in Feb/March (2026 registration open until Feb 27), National Round following, feeding into IOL team selection.
howToStart: Find and register a team through a participating regional host site listed on ozclo.org.au ahead of the regional round.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, university-backed Australian STEM competition (running since 2008, ~1500 students/year) with a real pipeline to IOL; solid for Australian college applications and demonstrates teamwork plus analytical rigor.

name: Panini Linguistics Olympiad (PLO)
prerequisites: Indian students grades 6-12; no prior linguistics background needed, just logical/analytical aptitude.
costNote: Registration fee of approximately Rs 1000 (not free as previously listed); coordinated by IIIT Hyderabad, Microsoft Research India, JNU Delhi, and regional partners.
cadence: Annual; multi-round competition with Junior (grades 6-8) and Senior (grades 9-12) divisions; see PLO 2026 Timeline on official site.
howToStart: Register through the official PLO site (ltrc.iiit.ac.in/plo) during the open registration window before the first round.
a2cInsight: India's official IOL feeder and a credible research-institute-backed olympiad (IIIT Hyderabad); a national-level placement is a strong, differentiated credential for Indian applicants beyond the usual math/science circuit.

name: Asia Pacific Linguistics Olympiad (APLO)
prerequisites: Secondary school students nominated/selected through a participating country's linguistics olympiad structure (e.g., Singapore, Malaysia, Japan, Korea, China feeder programs).
costNote: Free to enter through participating national/regional coordinating bodies.
cadence: Annual, late March/early April; 2026 edition (8th) held simultaneously across 10 countries/regions on March 29, 2026 at 31 official testing sites.
howToStart: Qualify through your national linguistics olympiad program (e.g., SLO in Singapore, MyCLO in Malaysia, KLO in Korea) which nominates students to APLO.
a2cInsight: A regional stepping-stone between national olympiads and IOL; less universally known than IOL but signals serious regional-level linguistic problem-solving ability, especially valuable for Asia-based applicants.

name: Online Linguistics Olympiad (Onling)
prerequisites: Open to any secondary/high school student worldwide (plus a post-secondary division); no national affiliation required.
costNote: Free online participation, open worldwide.
cadence: Was annual (typically late January, 24-hour open window; most recent edition Jan 26, 2025); organizers confirmed a hiatus for 2026 to redesign problems, planning to resume in 2027.
howToStart: Register on onling.org during the open registration window (when active) and solve problems within the 24-hour contest window from anywhere with internet access.
a2cInsight: A globally-open, volunteer-run linguistics contest with real IOL-veteran problem-writers behind it; useful for students without access to a national olympiad, though its prestige is modest and it's confirmed paused for 2026, returning in 2027.

name: IOL-AI 2026: The Linguistics Olympiad Challenge
prerequisites: Programming/ML skills required - contestants submit a Hugging Face repository with a script that is run against a hidden IOL-style test set; open to students and general public, not age-restricted.
costNote: Free to submit; runs on Hugging Face infrastructure.
cadence: Tied to the 2026 IOL cycle; open submission window ahead of/around the July-August 2026 IOL competition dates, deadline July 26, 2026.
howToStart: Create a Hugging Face account, build a solver script for linguistic puzzle problems, and submit via iolai.org's submission portal.
a2cInsight: A novel, ML/AI-flavored spinoff explicitly branded as an official open challenge from the International Linguistics Olympiad; too new to carry established prestige but a good showcase for a student aiming at CS/AI + linguistics intersection projects.

name: German Linguistics Olympiad (Deutsche Linguistik-Olympiade / DLO)
prerequisites: Must be enrolled at a German school; recommended from 8th grade onward. No prior linguistics coursework needed.
costNote: Free and voluntary at all rounds.
cadence: Annual, three rounds - online first round (Jan 17-Feb 16), second online round in March, in-person final in Berlin (May 1-4); recognized on Germany's KMK list of nationwide competitions.
howToStart: Register for the first (online) round via linguistikolympiade.de during the announced registration period.
a2cInsight: Officially recognized on Germany's KMK list of nationwide student competitions since 2024, giving it real domestic academic weight; strong credential for German-system applicants and a clear pipeline to IOL.

name: Dutch Linguistics Olympiad (Nederlandse Taalkunde Olympiade / TKO)
prerequisites: Dutch students in grades 4, 5, and 6 of VWO (pre-university track), roughly ages 15-18; no prior linguistics background required.
costNote: Free to enter; run by Leiden University's Centre for Linguistics (LUCL) and Honours Academy.
cadence: Annual; online qualifying round in early December, final round at Leiden University in January (26th edition final held January 16, 2026).
howToStart: Register for the online qualifying round through Leiden University's Taalkundeolympiade page during the announced fall registration window (opens ~November).
a2cInsight: A smaller, university-run national olympiad; solid domestic credential in the Netherlands and a legitimate feeder to Team Netherlands at IOL (one of the IOL's founding countries with a strong historic medal record).

name: Estonian Linguistics Olympiad (Lingvistikaolümpiaad)
prerequisites: Estonian students in grades 9-12; no prior linguistics knowledge required.
costNote: Free; organized by University of Tartu's science school (Teaduskool).
cadence: Annual since 2003, with preliminary/regional rounds and a final round organized by Tartu Ülikool Teaduskool.
howToStart: Register through Tartu Ülikool Teaduskool's olympiad portal (teaduskool.ut.ee) during the announced registration window.
a2cInsight: One of the founding national olympiads of the IOL system (since 2003), giving it unusual historical legitimacy for such a small program; a strong niche credential for Estonian applicants.

name: Korea Linguistics Olympiad (KLO)
prerequisites: Korean youth (middle/high school level); no prior linguistics coursework required, just logical/analytical reasoning.
costNote: Modest registration fee typical of Korean national olympiad programs; exact amount set annually by organizers.
cadence: Annual, held around November as the preliminary round for national team selection.
howToStart: Register via krlo.kr during the announced fall registration period.
a2cInsight: A well-established (since 2010) non-profit national olympiad with a genuine IOL pipeline; a strong résumé line for Korean students seeking to differentiate from the crowded math/science olympiad field.

name: Israeli Hebrew Language Olympiad (Tel Aviv University)
prerequisites: Students grades 7-12 from anywhere in the world (separate middle/high school tracks); includes special tracks for ultra-Orthodox schools, Arabic-speaking students, and non-native Hebrew speakers.
costNote: Free; hosted by Tel Aviv University's Department of Hebrew Language and Semitic Linguistics.
cadence: Annual, with an online first stage followed by a final round at Tel Aviv University; the fifth edition (2026) drew over 9,200 participants worldwide.
howToStart: Register through Tel Aviv University's Hebrew Language Olympiad page during the announced fall/winter registration window.
a2cInsight: A university-hosted, rapidly-growing olympiad (founded 2022) that doubles as Israel's IOL feeder; niche but legitimate, and the specialized tracks (Arabic-speaking students, Hebrew learners abroad) show real inclusivity effort worth noting in an application context.

name: Polish Mathematical Linguistics Olympiad (Olimpiada Lingwistyki Matematycznej)
prerequisites: Polish high school students; combines linguistic and mathematical/deductive reasoning, no formal coursework required.
costNote: Free; organized by the Foundation of Wrocław Mathematicians (Fundacja Matematyków Wrocławskich) with the University of Wrocław.
cadence: Annual since 2004/05, multi-round national competition; serves as Poland's IOL selection.
howToStart: Register through olm.math.uni.wroc.pl during the announced fall registration window for the first round.
a2cInsight: Explicitly frames linguistics as applied mathematics/logic, making it a distinctive credential for STEM-minded students who want a humanities-adjacent olympiad that still reads as rigorous and analytical.

name: Czech Linguistics Olympiad (Česká lingvistická olympiáda)
prerequisites: Czech secondary school students; problems span computational/formal linguistics, phonetics, and phonology - no prior coursework required.
costNote: Free; co-organized by Charles University's Faculty of Arts and Faculty of Mathematics and Physics with Ministry of Education support.
cadence: Annual, with school, regional, and central rounds.
howToStart: Register through your school for the school round, or directly via lingol.cz for the regional/open entry points.
a2cInsight: Ministry-recognized nationwide competition status gives it real domestic academic legitimacy in the Czech system; a solid credential that funnels the top 4 scorers to Team Czechia at IOL.

name: All Ireland Linguistics Olympiad (AILO)
prerequisites: Secondary school students from the island of Ireland (Republic and Northern Ireland); no prior linguistics or foreign-language knowledge required.
costNote: Free; run by the SFI-funded ADAPT Centre.
cadence: Annual since 2009; school-based qualifying round (late January) followed by a national final (early March), with top scorers qualifying for Team Ireland at IOL in late July.
howToStart: Register your school or as an individual via ailo.adaptcentre.ie ahead of the annual heat.
a2cInsight: Backed by Science Foundation Ireland's ADAPT Research Centre and winner of a European Language Label award, giving it credible institutional weight; Ireland's largest Science Olympiad (3,500-4,000 participants annually), making it a well-established and legible Irish STEM/humanities credential.

name: Singapore Linguistics Olympiad (SLO)
prerequisites: All students currently enrolled in any secondary or pre-tertiary school in Singapore; not open to university students. No prior linguistics background required.
costNote: Modest registration fee set annually; hosted by NTU's National Institute of Education.
cadence: Annual; hosted by NIE/NTU Singapore, serving as the official selection mechanism for APLO and IOL delegations.
howToStart: Register through the NTU NIE SLO event page (ntu.edu.sg/nie/SLO) during the announced registration window.
a2cInsight: University-hosted (NIE/NTU) national olympiad with a direct, transparent pipeline to APLO and IOL; a strong niche credential for Singaporean students looking to stand out from the saturated math/science olympiad pool.

name: Malaysian Computational Linguistics Olympiad (MyCLO)
prerequisites: Malaysian primary, secondary, and pre-university students; no prior linguistics knowledge required.
costNote: Free online national competition.
cadence: Annual since 2018; online national competition (now also branded 'Language Explorer Competition') with top performers advancing to APLO and IOL selection.
howToStart: Register via myclo.my during the open registration window for the annual online competition.
a2cInsight: A relatively young but active national program with a clear IOL/APLO pipeline; good for Malaysian students seeking an early, low-barrier entry point into competitive linguistics before pursuing the international stage.

name: Himalayan Computational Linguistic Olympiad Nepal (HLON)
prerequisites: Nepali students in grades 8-12; blends language analysis with computational thinking, no prior coursework required.
costNote: Low-cost entry; exact fee varies by year and is set by organizing committee.
cadence: Annual (running since at least 2018/2022 editions); national competition for grades 8-12 feeding Team Nepal at IOL and IOAI.
howToStart: Watch for registration announcements via HLON's Facebook page and hlon.org during the competition season.
a2cInsight: A grassroots but genuine national olympiad that has sent Nepal to IOL since 2019 and to IOAI in recent years; niche recognition value but a real differentiator for Nepali students given how few compete in this space.

name: Japan Linguistics Olympiad (JOL)
prerequisites: Selection category open to anyone under 20 who is not yet receiving university education at the time of the next IOL; an Open category has no participation restrictions. No prior linguistics background required.
costNote: Free national competition run by the Japan IOL organizing committee.
cadence: Annual, usually held in December; national qualification competition feeding Team Japan at IOL, application period announced each year.
howToStart: Apply during the announced application window via iolingjapan.org.
a2cInsight: Japan's official IOL feeder olympiad, running since 2015; a solid, distinctive credential for Japanese students, particularly those pursuing linguistics, CS, or humanities-STEM crossover profiles.

name: Canadian Linguistics Olympiad Francophone (Olympiade linguistique canadienne francophone, OLCF)
prerequisites: Pre-university Canadian students (secondary school or CEGEP) who identify with French-language education; no prior linguistics or foreign-language theory needed.
costNote: Free to enter; hosted via University of Ottawa infrastructure, run by volunteers.
cadence: Annual; asynchronous first stage typically held mid-March (2026 edition March 14-15), selecting a 4-person Team Canada Francophone for IOL.
howToStart: Register through kaitire.rdc.uottawa.ca/OLCF during the announced registration window before the March contest dates.
a2cInsight: The parallel, French-language counterpart to NACLO for Canada's IOL team selection; less well-known than NACLO but carries equal weight for Francophone Canadian students seeking an IOL berth.

name: Spanish Linguistics Olympiad (Olimpiada Lingüística Española, OLE)
prerequisites: Spanish secondary school (bachillerato) students; no prior linguistics coursework required.
costNote: Free; regional phases hosted by various Spanish universities, national phase at Universitat de València.
cadence: Annual, with regional phases at multiple Spanish universities (e.g. the Valencia edition held February 2026) feeding into a National Phase hosted by Universitat de València (April 2026), which selects 4 students for the International phase.
howToStart: Register through your local university's regional olympiad phase (e.g., Universitat de València, Universidad de Alicante) during the announced window.
a2cInsight: A newer, university-network-driven national olympiad (grew out of the EU Abu-MaTran project); legitimate feeder to Team Spain at IOL but still building broad name recognition domestically compared to Spain's math/science olympiads.

name: da Vinci Decathlon
prerequisites: School forms a team of 8 students (Years 5-11); no individual entry.
costNote: Team entry fees vary by state/region, typically in the AUD $50-300+ range for an 8-student team.
cadence: Annual regional heats (state-based) with a national final; academic-year cadence, team of 8.
howToStart: School registers a team through davincidecathlon.com for a regional round.
a2cInsight: A fun multidisciplinary team decathlon (math is one of 10 disciplines) - great for well-roundedness and teamwork narratives, not a standalone math-prestige credential.

name: ICAS Mathematics
prerequisites: None; open to all students Years 2-12, not restricted to top performers.
costNote: Entry fees vary by school/country, generally under AUD $20-30 per student.
cadence: Annual, multiple sitting windows depending on region; online multiple-choice/short-answer format.
howToStart: School registers via icasassessments.com (run by UNSW Global/Educational Assessment Australia).
a2cInsight: A broad, inclusive assessment (10M+ students, 41 countries) that signals consistent participation rather than elite selectivity - a nice-to-have line, not a differentiator.

name: SASMO (Singapore & Asian Schools Math Olympiad)
prerequisites: None; open to Primary 1 to Junior College 2 (roughly Grade 1-12) students internationally.
costNote: Typically SGD/local-currency equivalent of USD $20-40 per student depending on country center.
cadence: Annual, single 90-minute written round, typically mid-year.
howToStart: Register individually or through school via sasmo.simcc.org.
a2cInsight: One of Asia's largest privately-run olympiads (700k+ students, 50+ countries) with generous medal bands (top 40%) - broad participation credential, moderate selectivity, useful for showing regional engagement.

name: APMOPS / SMOPS (Asia-Pacific Mathematical Olympiad for Primary Schools)
prerequisites: Open to Primary 5-6 students (Grade 5-6) via school or overseas centre; top scorers invited to the Invitation Round.
costNote: Modest per-student entry fee set by Hwa Chong Institution/local centres.
cadence: Annual; first round in April at local centres, Invitation Round in late May/June in Singapore.
howToStart: Register through a participating school or official overseas centre listed at apmops.hci.edu.sg.
a2cInsight: A genuinely selective, Hwa Chong Institution-run olympiad respected across Asia-Pacific primary math circles - the Invitation Round medal is a real marker of top-tier primary-level ability, though it caps out before secondary school.

name: International Tournament of the Towns
prerequisites: No formal prerequisite, but problems are olympiad-difficulty; students sit at a local host city/university.
costNote: Generally free to sit; local host institutions organize sittings.
cadence: Twice yearly (Fall and Spring rounds), each with a Junior and Senior paper; local sittings, e.g. hosted at University of Sydney for Australian students.
howToStart: Find a local host city sitting (e.g., University of Sydney in Australia) via turgor.ru or a national coordinator.
a2cInsight: A historic, Russia-founded olympiad (since 1980) known for unusually creative, non-routine problems - deeply respected among serious olympiad coaches internationally, though it has low public name recognition with general admissions readers.

name: World Mathematics Invitational (WMI)
prerequisites: None to enter Preliminary; open Kindergarten through Grade 12; Final Round requires qualifying score.
costNote: Preliminary and Final Round fees plus travel costs for the in-person Final Round in a host city.
cadence: Annual; Preliminary Round Jan-Mar in-country, Final Round in July in a rotating host city.
howToStart: Register via a WMI member country/region organizer listed at wminv.org.
a2cInsight: A Taiwan-founded international invitational with a large (~30,000 competitor) reach and a travel-based Final Round - decent breadth credential, but pay-to-enter structure and lesser-known brand versus AMC/SASMO tier programs.

name: SEAMO (Southeast Asian Mathematical Olympiad)
prerequisites: None; open to primary and secondary students (ages 7-18) across 20+ countries.
costNote: Local-currency entry fee per round; Global Round (SEAMO X) adds travel cost.
cadence: Annual; national round in January, top 40% advance to Global Round (SEAMO X).
howToStart: Register through a local test center via seamo-official.org.
a2cInsight: A Singapore-founded, fast-growing regional olympiad (50,000+ students since 2016) aligned to Singapore MOE competency frameworks - solid regional credibility but younger and less internationally prestigious than SMO/APMO.

name: Thailand International Mathematical Olympiad (TIMO)
prerequisites: None; open Kindergarten through Grade 13 across many countries.
costNote: Modest per-round entry fee (Heat and Final rounds priced separately).
cadence: Annual; Heat Round at country level, Final Round in Thailand mid-year.
howToStart: Register through a national organizer (e.g., OCEC-affiliated centre) via thaiimo.com.
a2cInsight: A commercially-run, Hong Kong/Thailand co-organized olympiad in the same family as HKIMO/TIMO franchise contests - decent for building a competition track record but not a nationally elite credential.

name: Hong Kong International Mathematical Olympiad (HKIMO)
prerequisites: None; open Kindergarten through secondary levels internationally.
costNote: Per-round entry fee for Heat and Final rounds.
cadence: Annual; Heat Round conducted at country level, Final Round held in Hong Kong.
howToStart: Register through a local test centre listed at hongkongimo.com.
a2cInsight: Founded by a former HK Math Olympiad team leader as a commercial international franchise olympiad - useful for building a contest resume in Asia but carries less prestige than the national Hong Kong Mathematical Olympiad itself.

name: IMAS (International Mathematics Assessments for Schools)
prerequisites: None; targets middle-primary through lower-junior-secondary students.
costNote: Low per-round entry fee, two rounds typically offered per year.
cadence: Annual, two rounds (First and Second) across the school year.
howToStart: Register via a local school or organizer listed at chiuchang.org/imas.
a2cInsight: A broad-access Asia-founded assessment testing conceptual understanding and reasoning - accessible entry point for building a math-competition track record, not a selective distinguishing credential.

name: International Mathematical Modeling Challenge (IM2C / IMMC)
prerequisites: Teams of up to 4 high school students plus a faculty advisor; countries select up to two teams.
costNote: Modest per-team registration fee via COMAP/national organizer.
cadence: Annual, mid-Feb through late-April window; teams pick 5 consecutive days to solve and submit a modeling paper, judged by June.
howToStart: Register a team through a national coordinator (Australian portal: immchallenge.org.au) or COMAP directly.
a2cInsight: A genuine open-ended mathematical-modeling research competition (co-sponsored by COMAP) that produces a real writeup - more valuable for demonstrating applied research/writing skills than a timed problem-solving test.

name: Asian Science and Mathematics Olympiad (ASMO)
prerequisites: None; open to primary and secondary students (Grade 3-12) across multiple Asian countries.
costNote: Low per-subject entry fee at national and international rounds.
cadence: Annual; National Round followed by International Round for qualifiers.
howToStart: Register through a local Olympic Edu-affiliated centre via asmo2u.com.
a2cInsight: A Malaysia-based, high-volume regional olympiad (110,000+ entries in 2025) covering both math and science - accessible entry-level credential with limited prestige outside the region.

name: New Zealand Mathematical Olympiad (NZMO)
prerequisites: Open to New Zealand secondary students; no strict prerequisite but problems are olympiad-level.
costNote: No entry fee; run by the NZ Mathematical Olympiad Committee as a not-for-profit.
cadence: Annual, Round 1 (take-home) in June, Round 2 (invitation-only, in-person) in August/September, with a training/selection pathway toward the IMO team.
howToStart: Register through mathsolympiad.org.nz; many students first enter via school maths enrichment programs.
a2cInsight: New Zealand's official IMO-selection olympiad - genuinely elite and admissions-respected within NZ, directly comparable in stature to Australia's AMO.

name: Australasian Problem Solving Mathematical Olympiads (APSMO Maths Olympiad)
prerequisites: None; entered through school for Years 5-8 (Divisions J and S).
costNote: Per-student school subscription fee, typically tens of AUD/NZD per season.
cadence: Annual season, 5 monthly divisional contests (Years 5-6 Junior Division; Years 7-8 Senior Division) run August-December (Southern Hemisphere school terms).
howToStart: School coordinator registers via apsmo.edu.au; affiliated with MOEMS in the US.
a2cInsight: A well-established Australia/NZ problem-solving league (since 1987) valued as a sustained-engagement middle-years credential rather than a single elite test.

name: World Mathematics Championships (WMC)
prerequisites: None to start; Junior/Senior Finals require qualifying through Regional rounds.
costNote: Regional Qualifier and Finals fees, plus travel costs for in-person stages (e.g., University of Melbourne Junior Finals).
cadence: Annual; Primary/Secondary online rounds, Junior/Senior pathways add Regional Qualifiers then a week-long in-person Finals (Senior at Queen Mary University of London in June; Junior at University of Melbourne in December).
howToStart: Register through a school or individually via the WMC platform (wmc.eunoia.ventures / wmc.app).
a2cInsight: A newer app-based collaborative-assessment platform with an unusually global Finals footprint (London and Melbourne) - interesting format innovation, but still building brand recognition compared to legacy olympiads.

name: Wieża Babel (Tower of Babel) Linguistics Competition for Younger Students
prerequisites: Polish primary/lower-secondary students (grades 3-8) too young for the main Olimpiada Lingwistyki Matematycznej.
costNote: Free; run by the same Wrocław Mathematicians Foundation that runs Poland's main linguistics olympiad.
cadence: Annual; organized alongside the Olimpiada Lingwistyki Matematycznej for younger (grades 3-5 'Young' category and 6-8 'Junior' category) students.
howToStart: Register via the Fundacja Matematyków Wrocławskich site (fmw.math.uni.wroc.pl) alongside the main olympiad's announcements.
a2cInsight: A junior on-ramp rather than a standalone prestige credential; useful for building a multi-year linguistics-olympiad narrative starting in elementary/middle school before the 'real' Polish olympiad in high school.

name: International Geography Olympiad (iGeo)
prerequisites: Must be 16-19 years old (inclusive as of Aug 1) and win selection through your country's national geography olympiad/championship (in the US: US Geography Championships).
costNote: National-level entry (via US Geography Championships) is low-cost, but travel to represent your country at the international event is typically funded/organized by the national body; some self-funded costs may apply.
cadence: Annual, held each August; national qualification process runs during the school year.
howToStart: Register for your country's national qualifying competition (e.g., US Geography Championships) and place among the top scorers to be selected for the national team.
a2cInsight: A genuinely elite, hard-to-fake international credential -- comparable prestige to IMO/IPhO within geography circles, though far less universally recognized by admissions officers since geography olympiads are less well-known in the US.

name: United States Geography Championships -- Middle & Elementary School Divisions
prerequisites: Must have qualified for and be competing in the National Geography Bee National Championships; eligibility is for students in 8th grade and younger.
costNote: Requires prior qualification through the National Geography Bee's regional/national process; added national championship fee.
cadence: Annual, National Championships held online in spring (e.g., May).
howToStart: Qualify through the National Geography Bee's Online Regional Qualifying Exam and Regional Finals, then register for USGC Nationals.
a2cInsight: A fine early credential for a geography-minded middle schooler, but its prestige is capped by the narrow, non-selective on-ramp and young age range.

name: National Geographic GeoChallenge
prerequisites: Students in grades 4-8, teams of 4-6, coached by a registered Coach/Coordinator.
costNote: Free, project-based team competition.
cadence: Annual, multilevel competition running across the school year with a culminating showcase.
howToStart: Have a teacher or coach register a team at nationalgeographic.org/education/student-experiences/geochallenge.
a2cInsight: A solid, free, National Geographic-branded team project competition on real environmental issues -- good for building a geography/environmental portfolio in middle school, though it is being 'evolved' toward a global model so specifics change year to year.

name: Esri/NCGE ArcGIS Online (StoryMaps) School Competition
prerequisites: Grades 4-12, public/nonpublic/homeschool students; individual or 2-person teams; one entry per student.
costNote: Free entry; schools get free ArcGIS Online access.
cadence: Annual, state-level entries in spring, with state winners advancing to a national round.
howToStart: Have your school register and build a StoryMap analyzing a state-specific issue per the current year's theme, submitted via esri.com/en-us/c/industry/education/school-competition.
a2cInsight: A genuinely useful, free way for a student to demonstrate real GIS/data-storytelling skill with an industry-standard tool (ArcGIS) -- respected in STEM/geospatial circles though not a broadly known 'competition' name to generalist admissions readers.

name: North Carolina GIS Map Making Competition (Grades 4-12)
prerequisites: North Carolina public, private, or homeschooled students in grades 4-12; individual or 2-person teams.
costNote: Free to enter; cash prizes for top NC finishers.
cadence: Annual, aligned with the national Esri/NCGE competition timeline.
howToStart: Register through NC State Extension/4-H (nc4h.ces.ncsu.edu) and build an ArcGIS Online StoryMap on a NC-specific issue.
a2cInsight: A solid regional on-ramp into the national Esri GIS competition -- legitimate but small in scale, useful mainly as a stepping stone rather than a headline credential.

name: My California GIS Mapping Showcase and Competition
prerequisites: California students in grades 4-8 (middle) or 9-12 (high school); schools, after-school programs, or homeschools.
costNote: Free to enter; prize money and certificates for top entries.
cadence: Annual, spring registration/submission window (roughly February-May).
howToStart: Register via the California Geographic Alliance (calgeography.sdsu.edu) and build a StoryMap on a California-focused issue.
a2cInsight: A state-level feeder into the national Esri/NCGE competition -- fine early experience, but limited prestige beyond California.

name: GISCI Map Contest
prerequisites: Open to GIS professionals and students (undergraduate/graduate division noted); competitive high schoolers with strong GIS skills can sometimes enter the open/student category -- confirm current rules.
costNote: Free to submit a map entry.
cadence: Annual, aligned with GIS Day (November) submissions window.
howToStart: Submit an original map you created to the GIS Certification Institute via gisci.org/Learn-More/Map-Contest.
a2cInsight: A professionally judged cartography contest that signals real technical GIS skill, but it primarily targets college-level and professional cartographers, so a high schooler's entry is a reach rather than a natural fit.

name: USGIF Scholarship Program (High School Senior GEOINT Scholarship)
prerequisites: Graduating high school senior intending to study STEM with an emphasis on AI, ML, cybersecurity, or geospatial fields; requires transcripts, recommendation letters, short-answer essays.
costNote: Free to apply.
cadence: Annual application cycle, typically closing in early April.
howToStart: Apply directly through usgif.org/usgif-scholarship-program before the annual deadline.
a2cInsight: A real $10,000 scholarship from the leading US geospatial intelligence nonprofit -- niche but legitimate and highly relevant if a student's portfolio centers on GIS/GEOINT.

name: Canadian Geographic Challenge (Can Geo Challenge)
prerequisites: Canadian students in grades 4-10+ (Level 1: grades 4-6, school-level only; Level 2: grades 7-10, provincial/national; Level 3: ages 16-19, feeds into Team Canada for iGeo).
costNote: Free for schools to join; winners receive a magazine subscription and certificate.
cadence: Annual, running through the school year with school, provincial, and national rounds.
howToStart: Have your Canadian school/teacher register at challenge.canadiangeographic.ca.
a2cInsight: Canada's flagship school geography competition (30-year history) and the official Canadian pathway to iGeo -- strong, free, and well-regarded within Canada, though little known outside it.

name: Australian Geography Competition
prerequisites: Australian students in Years 7-12; entry through participating schools.
costNote: Modest per-student entry fee (around $5 AUD) set by the Australian Geography Teachers Association/school.
cadence: Annual, held in schools over a two-week window each spring (April-May).
howToStart: Have your school register via geographycompetition.org.au.
a2cInsight: Australia's largest and most established school geography competition (54,000+ entrants), a solid national-scale credential and a feeder for Australia's iGeo team, but Australia-specific.

name: RGS School Essay Competition
prerequisites: Students aged 16-18 studying A Level geography or equivalent (UK Year 12-13, i.e. roughly US grades 11-12, and international schools).
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, opens in fall with a deadline typically in November.
howToStart: Write an essay or ArcGIS StoryMap responding to the year's set geographical question and submit per rgs.org/schools/competitions rules.
a2cInsight: Run by the Royal Geographical Society in partnership with the Financial Times -- a genuinely prestigious UK essay competition that carries real weight for Oxbridge/UK university geography applications.

name: RGS Young Geographer of the Year
prerequisites: Age-banded categories: KS2 (ages 7-11), KS3 (11-14), KS4/GCSE (14-16), KS5/A Level (16-18); UK state-funded and independent/international school tracks.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, deadline typically in early October.
howToStart: Schools run an in-house heat and submit top entries per the annual theme via rgs.org/schools/competitions/young-geographer-of-the-year.
a2cInsight: The Royal Geographical Society's flagship student recognition across all secondary ages -- a well-established (20+ year) and genuinely respected UK credential, especially for younger students who can't yet enter the A-Level essay competition.

name: Minds Underground Geography Essay Competition
prerequisites: Students aged 15+; no formal coursework prerequisite; particularly recommended for Year 12 students.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, opens each year with a set deadline (check current cycle at mindsunderground.com/geography-competition).
howToStart: Write an original essay on the year's theme (global challenges, innovation, sustainability) and submit via the Minds Underground website.
a2cInsight: A newer UK-based tutoring-company-run essay contest -- decent low-stakes writing practice and portfolio piece, but far less institutionally prestigious than the RGS competitions.

name: Geo Genius Olympiad (National Geography Olympiad, India)
prerequisites: Students in classes 2-12 (Sub-Junior: 2-4, Junior: 5-8, Senior: 9-12) from any Indian curriculum board; school must register a minimum of 50 students.
costNote: Registration fee around Rs. 300 (roughly $3-4 USD) per student.
cadence: Annual, with preliminary online exam typically held in fall/winter.
howToStart: Register individually or through your school via the IBGB Learning Society's Geo Genius India portal.
a2cInsight: India's main feeder olympiad into iGeo (top scorers represent India internationally) -- legitimate and low-cost, though its brand recognition is confined mostly to Indian schools and Indian-curriculum international schools.

name: IAC European & Asian Division International Geography Bee
prerequisites: Students in Europe, the Middle East, Central Asia, the Caucasus, or Asia; divisions from Elementary through Varsity.
costNote: Regional exam and championship fees vary by division, generally comparable to the US IAC events (tens to low hundreds of dollars for finals).
cadence: Annual, following the same IAC Online Regional Qualifying Exam through World Championship structure as the US division.
howToStart: Register for the Online Regional Qualifying Exam via internationalgeographybee.com (European) or iacompetitionsasia.com (Asian).
a2cInsight: Same IAC-run commercial bee circuit as the US version, extended internationally -- useful and accessible for students outside the US, but carries the same for-profit-adjacent, moderate-prestige caveats.

name: International History Bee and Bowl -- International Geography Bee (Canadian Division)
prerequisites: Canadian students, divided by grade/age (Elementary, Middle School, Junior Varsity, Varsity); open to middle and high schoolers.
costNote: Regional and national event fees comparable to other IAC/IHBB-affiliated bees.
cadence: Annual, school-year competition cycle with regional and national rounds.
howToStart: Register through ihbbcanada.com/geography for a regional qualifying event.
a2cInsight: A smaller Canadian-market geography bee run by the IHBB organization -- legitimate but overshadowed by the much larger, free Canadian Geographic Challenge for most Canadian students.

name: Geo Genius National Geography Olympiad (IBGB Learning Society)
prerequisites: Indian students in grades 2-12 (State Board, ICSE, CBSE, or International Board); no prior geography olympiad experience required.
costNote: Low registration fee, roughly Rs. 300 (a few dollars equivalent), covering all levels.
cadence: Annual, exam window typically in the fall/winter olympiad season, with a school-level round followed by higher levels for top scorers.
howToStart: Register via the organizing body's portal; informational pages are also mirrored by coaching platforms such as allen.in/olympiad/national-geography-olympiad.
a2cInsight: Actually organized by the nonprofit IBGB Learning Society (not by the Allen coaching institute, which merely republishes an informational page about it) -- accessible practice for younger students but carries limited standalone prestige outside India and is often surfaced through test-prep coaching marketing funnels.

name: Geo-Institute GeoChallenge (GeoWall, GeoPrediction, GeoPoster, GeoVideo)
prerequisites: Primarily undergraduate/graduate geotechnical engineering students; not realistically accessible to middle/high schoolers.
costNote: Requires travel/registration to the annual Geo-Congress conference; costs vary by team/institution.
cadence: Annual, held each spring at the ASCE Geo-Institute's Geo-Congress conference.
howToStart: Form a university-affiliated team and register through geoinstitute.org/students/geochallenge ahead of Geo-Congress.
a2cInsight: A real, professionally respected geotechnical engineering competition, but it is a college/graduate-level event, not something a middle or high schooler can enter -- listed for completeness but not MS/HS accessible.

name: National Geographic Student Map Awards (AAG Cartography Specialty Group)
prerequisites: Undergraduate students and master's-degree candidates whose cartography work was completed for university credit, confirmed by an instructor; not realistically MS/HS accessible.
costNote: Free to submit; historically aimed at university-level cartography coursework, with cash prizes ($900/$300 for first/second place).
cadence: Annual; run through the AAG Cartography and Mapping Specialty Group.
howToStart: Check the AAG Cartography Specialty Group and nationalgeographic.org/media/student-map-awards pages for current submission rules.
a2cInsight: A respected juried cartography award administered with National Geographic and AAG backing, but it's structured for college-level cartography coursework, not K-12 students.

name: GIS Day Map Competitions (University-hosted, e.g., UNL, UF, ODU)
prerequisites: Varies by host institution; most (e.g., Old Dominion, University of Florida) restrict entry to enrolled university students, though a few open to the broader public -- check the specific host's rules.
costNote: Free to submit; cash prizes vary by hosting institution (e.g., $800/$600/$400 for top three at University of Florida).
cadence: Annual, tied to GIS Day each November; specific rules/eligibility set by each hosting university.
howToStart: Find a nearby university's GIS Day map competition (many post open calls each fall) and submit an original map per their theme and format rules.
a2cInsight: A loose network of local/regional GIS Day map contests hosted by individual universities -- most are actually restricted to enrolled college students, so verify eligibility carefully before treating this as MS/HS accessible.

name: Florida School GIS Competition
prerequisites: Florida students in grades 4-12 (public, non-public, online, or home school), not yet holding a high school diploma; individuals or teams of two.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, aligned with the Esri/NCGE national competition calendar.
howToStart: Register via flschoolgiscompetition.com and build a map-centric project analyzing a Florida-focused geographic issue.
a2cInsight: Another state-level feeder into the national Esri GIS competition ecosystem -- fine regional experience, minimal standalone prestige outside Florida. Note eligibility actually starts at grade 4, not middle school.

name: New Mexico Story Map Competition for Schools (NMGIC)
prerequisites: New Mexico students in grades 4-12 (elementary-middle and high divisions); individual or small team entries.
costNote: Free to enter; Esri provides $1,000 in prize money split among top five entries per grade-level category.
cadence: Annual, aligned with the Esri/NCGE national competition calendar.
howToStart: Register via nmgic.com/education/agoschoolcomp and submit an ArcGIS StoryMap on a New Mexico issue.
a2cInsight: A small state-level GIS StoryMap contest run by the New Mexico Geographic Information Council -- legitimate but very locally scoped.

name: Journal of Geography (National Council for Geographic Education)
prerequisites: Peer-reviewed academic journal aimed at geography education researchers and practitioners; a K-12 student would need a strong mentor-led research project and co-authorship to have any realistic shot.
costNote: No submission fee (standard academic journal via Taylor & Francis), but realistically requires faculty co-authorship or mentorship.
cadence: Rolling submissions, published in regular issues (bimonthly/quarterly).
howToStart: Review submission guidelines at ncge.org/teacher-resources/publications/the-journal-of-geography and via the Taylor & Francis portal; typically requires a faculty/teacher mentor to co-author.
a2cInsight: A real, respected peer-reviewed academic journal in geography education -- a genuine coup if a student is credited, but essentially inaccessible without a university mentor, so treat as an elite stretch goal, not a typical target.

name: Student Civics Challenge (Sandra Day O'Connor Institute)
prerequisites: Students in grades 6-12 (separate middle school and high school divisions); choose a civics topic (e.g., branches of government, Sandra Day O'Connor) and submit a video or song.
costNote: Free entry; finalists in middle and high school divisions share $5,000 in prizes, with awards up to $500 each.
cadence: Annual; announced each September, winners announced in spring.
howToStart: Register and submit an entry via oconnorinstitute.org/cc before the annual deadline.
a2cInsight: A creative, low-barrier national civics contest good for students who want to combine civics content with an artistic medium; less prestigious than flagship programs but legitimate and well-run.

name: Civic Action Project (CAP) Multimedia Contest (Teach Democracy)
prerequisites: High school students enrolled in a class using Teach Democracy's (formerly Constitutional Rights Foundation) Civic Action Project curriculum on a real public policy issue.
costNote: Free to enter.
cadence: Annual, tied to the school-year-long Civic Action Project curriculum.
howToStart: Teacher enrolls the class in Civic Action Project via crfcap.org; students then submit a short video about their policy issue to the multimedia contest.
a2cInsight: A legitimate, well-established (California-rooted but nationally used) action-civics program; the multimedia contest itself is a modest add-on rather than a headline credential.

name: National Student/Parent Mock Election
prerequisites: Open to any K-12 classroom or school nationwide; teachers register their class/school to participate in the simulated election and related activities.
costNote: All activities and materials are free.
cadence: Held in conjunction with actual U.S. general elections (even years), roughly 5+ days before Election Day.
howToStart: Teacher registers the school/class through nationalstudentparentmockelection.com ahead of the election-year event window.
a2cInsight: The oldest and largest civic-education simulation in the U.S. (76M+ cumulative participants), but it's a mass participatory activity rather than a competitive credential worth citing on an application by itself.

name: Ninth Circuit Civics Contest (and other Federal Circuit Civics Contests)
prerequisites: High school students in the relevant federal circuit's states/territories (public, private, parochial, charter, or homeschool); 500-1,000 word essay or 3-5 minute video on an annual constitutional-rights prompt.
costNote: Free to enter; cash prizes (e.g., $11,400 shared among winners in the Ninth Circuit in 2026: $3,000/$1,700/$1,000 per essay and video category).
cadence: Annual; entries typically open January, deadline in early March, winners announced by June.
howToStart: Submit through the circuit's federal courts civics contest website (e.g., ca9.uscourts.gov/civicscontest for the Ninth Circuit) within the annual window.
a2cInsight: A federal-judiciary-endorsed regional civics contest that's a nice, legitimate credential tied to real courts, though geographically limited to states within that circuit.

name: Kangaroo Math Competition (AKSF / Math Kangaroo Asia)
prerequisites: None; open internationally, all primary and secondary grades depending on national organizer.
costNote: Low national-organizer entry fee (e.g., Kangaroo Math Malaysia); Asia Championship adds travel cost for gold-medal invitees.
cadence: Annual, single sitting (typically March); gold medalists invited to the Math Kangaroo Asia Championship in Boao, Hainan over summer.
howToStart: Register through your country's AKSF-affiliated national organizer (e.g., kangaroomath.com.my in Malaysia, or amt.edu.au/ksf in Australia).
a2cInsight: The world's largest school-math competition (6M+ students, 115+ countries) via the France-founded Kangourou sans Frontières network - excellent breadth/accessibility credential, but very high participation numbers mean it signals engagement more than elite selectivity.

name: IMO Malaysia / IMONST (Malaysia National Selection Test)
prerequisites: Open to Malaysian primary and secondary students; no formal prerequisite for IMONST 1.
costNote: Low national entry fee for IMONST rounds.
cadence: Annual; IMONST 1 (broad entry) followed by IMONST 2 for top scorers, feeding Malaysia's IMO team selection.
howToStart: Register through school or directly via imo-malaysia.org.
a2cInsight: Malaysia's official national olympiad pipeline feeding its IMO team - genuinely selective and respected domestically, but access is effectively limited to Malaysia-based students.

name: Mathematics Trainers' Guild Philippines (MTG) Competitions
prerequisites: None; open to Philippine students Grades 1-12.
costNote: Low registration fee per MTG-run competition/training event.
cadence: Multiple events across the school year; rolling training programs plus periodic competitions.
howToStart: Register through a school or directly via mtgphil.org for training programs and competitions.
a2cInsight: A well-regarded Philippine math-training and competition organizer that feeds many students into PMO/international olympiad tracks - valuable as sustained training evidence more than a single prestige credential.

name: F=ma Exam (AAPT)
prerequisites: Strong AP Physics C: Mechanics-level knowledge; must be a US citizen, permanent resident, or enrolled at a US school; exam must be proctored by a teacher.
costNote: $75 school registration fee plus roughly $15 per student proctoring fee.
cadence: Annual; registration opens mid-October, exam given in mid-February.
howToStart: Have a school physics teacher register the school through AAPT's U.S. Physics Team portal (aapt.org/physicsteam) in the fall and administer the proctored 25-question, 75-minute mechanics exam.
a2cInsight: Top ~500 scorers advance to USAPhO, so even qualifying for the next stage is a genuine, hard-to-fake signal of physics aptitude that selective colleges recognize.

name: PhysicsBowl (AAPT)
prerequisites: Enrollment in a high school physics course (Division 1 for first-year students, Division 2 for second-year); proctored by school.
costNote: Roughly $10 per student; five students needed to qualify for team scoring.
cadence: Annual; exam window mid-March through early April.
howToStart: A school physics teacher registers online at aapt.org/programs/physicsbowl and administers the 40-question, 45-minute multiple-choice exam during the testing window.
a2cInsight: Widely taken and accessible, so it's a solid but not singularly prestigious credential; strong regional/national placement matters more than mere participation.

name: Online Physics Olympiad (OPhO)
prerequisites: None formal; teams of up to 3 students of any level, though problems are challenging (AP Physics C and beyond).
costNote: Roughly $10-30 per team entry.
cadence: Annual online competition, typically held in summer with open and invitational rounds.
howToStart: Register a team at opho.physoly.tech during the open registration window and complete problem sets online.
a2cInsight: A student-run, remote-friendly olympiad; useful for demonstrating initiative and physics depth but carries less institutional weight than AAPT-run exams.

name: Princeton University Physics Competition (PUPC)
prerequisites: Strong physics problem-solving background; teams or individuals depending on track (onsite vs. online).
costNote: Modest per-team registration fee; online exam option reduces travel cost.
cadence: Annual, typically held in fall (on-campus) with an online exam version students complete over about a week.
howToStart: Register through the official PUPC site ahead of the fall deadline; choose onsite (Princeton campus) or the online week-long exam.
a2cInsight: A respected university-run physics olympiad with real out-of-the-box problems; strong placement signals genuine problem-solving depth beyond textbook physics.

name: Physics Unlimited Premier Competition
