prerequisites: AP Physics 1/2/C-level preparation.
costNote: Low individual registration fee; partner-hosted international sites may vary.
cadence: Annual, individual 1.5-hour exam, typically held in November.
howToStart: Register through physicsu.org/premier; take the 1.5-hour, roughly four-problem individual exam either at a partner site or online.
a2cInsight: A newer nonprofit-run olympiad with growing international reach (Germany, China, Korea, Turkey); solid but not yet as recognized as AAPT-run exams.

name: Physics Unlimited Explorer Competition
prerequisites: None formal; team-based, open to high schoolers of varying levels.
costNote: Low team registration fee.
cadence: Annual; week-to-two-week-long open-ended research assignment, typically in November/December.
howToStart: Form a team and register at physicsu.org/explorer; complete and submit a written report responding to an open-ended, research-inspired physics prompt within the multi-week window.
a2cInsight: Good low-stakes entry point into physics research-style thinking, useful for building a portfolio piece rather than a headline credential.

name: International Physics Olympiad (IPhO)
prerequisites: Selection to the 5-member US Physics Team via F=ma and USAPhO (or equivalent national process in other countries).
costNote: Cost is borne by national teams/sponsors; individual US students do not pay to compete once selected.
cadence: Annual, held in July; national qualification (F=ma/USAPhO for the US) required to enter.
howToStart: For US students: excel through F=ma and USAPhO to be selected to the national team; entry is not open to direct individual registration.
a2cInsight: The single most prestigious physics credential available to a high schooler worldwide; a medal here is comparable in weight to IMO/IOI medals for admissions purposes.

name: United States Invitational Young Physicists Tournament (USIYPT/USAYPT)
prerequisites: School-based team; work is prepared over months investigating open-ended experimental physics problems before the live 'physics fight' debate format.
costNote: Team registration and travel to the annual tournament site adds up; costs vary by year/venue.
cadence: Annual, held the last weekend in January; teams work on open research problems released months in advance.
howToStart: Form a team with a faculty/teacher sponsor, register via usaypt.org, and begin researching the year's released problem set well before the tournament.
a2cInsight: A genuine research-and-debate format that rewards experimental depth and scientific communication, valued by admissions readers looking for authentic inquiry beyond exam-taking.

name: International Young Physicists' Tournament (IYPT)
prerequisites: Selection onto a national team (5 students plus 1-2 leaders); in the US this runs through the USAYPT selection process.
costNote: Team entry fees plus international travel; often runs well over $1,000 per participant when combined with team costs.
cadence: Annual, held June-August; problems released roughly a year in advance for team research.
howToStart: Compete well at USIYPT or an equivalent national qualifier to be selected for the US delegation, then prepare experimental solutions to the released 17 problems.
a2cInsight: Known as the 'Physics World Cup'; an internationally elite, research-oriented team credential that signals both deep physics understanding and scientific communication skill.

name: British Physics Olympiad (BPhO)
prerequisites: Full-time pre-university education; students register through a school or exam centre, not individually; UK national-team progression requires UK citizenship/education.
costNote: Modest per-student entry fee set by the registering school/centre.
cadence: Annual, multiple rounds (Preliminary/Intermediate/Advanced) from autumn through spring, registered through schools.
howToStart: Ask a school (including US schools that register as an international centre) to enroll through bpho.org.uk during the competition window.
a2cInsight: Internationally recognized UK olympiad that non-UK students can still take for a scored certificate; a strong add-on credential for US students seeking an additional physics olympiad beyond USAPhO.

name: Sir Isaac Newton Exam (University of Waterloo)
prerequisites: None; open to students of any grade, mainly covers Ontario-curriculum mechanics-focused physics.
costNote: Free to register and take.
cadence: Annual, held in early May, written under school supervision.
howToStart: Register through a school teacher at uwaterloo.ca/sir-isaac-newton-exam before the spring deadline; write the 2-hour proctored exam at school.
a2cInsight: A long-running (since 1969), free, low-stakes contest good for building a physics-contest track record early, but modest prestige compared to USAPhO/BPhO.

name: Beamline for Schools (CERN)
prerequisites: Team of secondary-school students aged 16+ (up to 30 members, at least one adult coach); propose a particle-accelerator beamline experiment.
costNote: Free to enter; winning teams get a fully funded trip to CERN, DESY, or ELSA to run their experiment.
cadence: Annual; proposal deadline typically in spring (around April).
howToStart: Form a team with a teacher/coach, review past winning proposals, and submit a written experiment proposal at beamlineforschools.cern before the deadline.
a2cInsight: A globally selective, real-physics-lab opportunity; winning is a rare and impressive credential showing genuine experimental design ability, not just exam performance.

name: Stevens Physics Olympiad
prerequisites: Grades 9-12; no formal prerequisite beyond general physics coursework.
costNote: Free to participate on the Stevens Institute of Technology campus.
cadence: Annual, held on campus in late spring (e.g., June 2026 for the 2nd edition).
howToStart: Register through Stevens Institute's physics department website ahead of the spring exam date and attend in person in Hoboken, NJ.
a2cInsight: A young (started 2024), regional, university-hosted contest; good local experience-builder but not yet a nationally recognized credential.

name: AAPT High School Physics Photo Contest
prerequisites: High school students (grades 9-12); submit an original photo plus a written physics-analysis essay.
costNote: $5-10 per photo entry (free for AAPT-member schools); max 15 entries per school.
cadence: Annual; registration/submission window roughly March-May 15, winners judged at the AAPT Summer Meeting.
howToStart: Have a teacher register the school at aapt.org/Programs/photocontest, then submit a photo and accompanying essay analyzing the physics shown before the May 15 deadline.
a2cInsight: A fun, creative-physics niche credential; nice supplement to a STEM+arts narrative but low weight on its own for admissions.

name: U.S. Physics Olympiad for Middle School Students (Physcira)
prerequisites: Grades 6-8; content aligned to pre-algebra math with conceptual mechanics, optics, and E&M questions.
costNote: No participation fee; sponsor-funded.
cadence: Annual, two-phase: school-level round in fall, national virtual round in spring.
howToStart: Register a school for the fall school-level round at physcira.com/olympiad; top finishers per school advance to the spring national round.
a2cInsight: One of the very few dedicated middle-school physics olympiads in the US; useful as an early on-ramp before F=ma eligibility in high school.

name: USA Astronomy and Astrophysics Olympiad (USAAAO)
prerequisites: Any student may take the first round; US citizenship/permanent residency required to represent the US at IOAA.
costNote: $30 per student (discounted to $20/student for schools with 5+ registrants).
cadence: Annual, two rounds: first-round multiple-choice exam (registration opens January), then a free-response National Astronomy Competition for top scorers.
howToStart: Register through usaaao.org for the proctored first-round exam administered by a school teacher, typically in the January window.
a2cInsight: The astrophysics counterpart to USAPhO; strong performance signals deep physics plus astronomy knowledge and is respected as an olympiad-tier credential (2025 US team won gold at IOAA).

name: International Astronomy and Astrophysics Competition (IAAC)
prerequisites: Open to any high school (and university) student worldwide; no formal prerequisite.
costNote: Roughly 8 euros registration fee for the qualification round.
cadence: Annual, three rounds from around February through June (Qualification, Pre-Final, Final).
howToStart: Register online at iaac.space before the qualification round deadline and complete the astronomy/astrophysics problem sets remotely.
a2cInsight: A large, accessible, fully online global competition (28,000+ students, 120+ countries since 2019); good breadth credential but less prestigious than USAAAO/IOAA.

name: National Science Bowl (DOE)
prerequisites: Team of 4 students plus 1 alternate and a teacher advisor; separate middle school and high school divisions.
costNote: Regional entry is typically low-cost or free; travel costs apply for teams advancing to DC finals.
cadence: Annual; regional tournaments January-March, national finals in Washington DC in April.
howToStart: Form a school team and register for a regional Science Bowl tournament through science.osti.gov/wdts/nsb in the fall.
a2cInsight: A broad, fast-paced multidisciplinary science quiz bowl (not physics-only) run by the US Department of Energy; one of the nation's largest science competitions (350,000+ alumni over 35 years). Strong regional/national placement is a solid STEM team credential.

name: Physics Field Day (Georgia Tech SPS)
prerequisites: High school students, individual or small-group; no formal prerequisite beyond general/AP physics coursework.
costNote: Approximately $25 per student/teacher registration fee; prize pool funded by the hosting SPS chapter.
cadence: Annual, held in spring (typically April, just before the AP Physics exam) on the Georgia Tech campus.
howToStart: Register through the Georgia Tech Society of Physics Students outreach page ahead of the spring event and attend in person in Atlanta.
a2cInsight: A regional, student-run competition good for AP Physics exam prep and campus exposure; minor credential weight for admissions outside the Southeast.

name: NJAAPT Physics Olympics (New Jersey)
prerequisites: School-based team; events combine building/design challenges (e.g., spaghetti towers, cantilevers) with physics knowledge.
costNote: Low team entry fee set by the state chapter.
cadence: Annual, held in January at North/Central and South Jersey sites.
howToStart: Have a school physics teacher register a team with the New Jersey AAPT chapter (njaapt.org) ahead of the January event.
a2cInsight: A fun regional build-and-compete event with strong local school pride, but a minor line-item nationally unless paired with stronger state/national results.

name: Canadian Physics Olympiad / CAP High School Prize Exam
prerequisites: Enrollment in a Canadian (or eligible international) high school; exam administered through a school's physics teacher.
costNote: Low or no fee depending on provincial coordinator; typically administered free through schools.
cadence: Annual, CAP exam in April; top 15 scorers proceed to a week-long Canadian Physics Olympiad Training Camp in May, with 5 selected for the IPhO national team.
howToStart: Register through a school with the Canadian Association of Physicists (cap.ca) or the Canadian Physics Olympiad site (olympiad.phas.ubc.ca) ahead of the spring exam.
a2cInsight: Canada's analog to F=ma/USAPhO; useful for US-based Canadian citizens/dual nationals or students at international schools eligible to enter, and a respected credential in Canadian admissions.

name: Physics Brawl Online (FYKOS)
ageNote: Organizers announced the competition ended after its 15th year (2025) partly due to AI tools undermining the format's integrity (175 teams were disqualified for AI-assisted cheating in the final year).
prerequisites: Teams of up to 5 students competed in high-school categories; an Open category existed for others.
costNote: Free to register and compete.
cadence: Was annual, one-day online team relay contest, typically in November.
howToStart: No longer running; historical archives and problem sets remain viewable at physicsbrawl.org for practice.
a2cInsight: Was once the world's largest online physics relay contest (1,500+ teams, 80+ countries); now defunct, so only useful as practice material, not as a fresh credential.

name: Society of Physics Students Awards (National SPS/Sigma Pi Sigma)
prerequisites: Primarily for undergraduate SPS chapter members; high schoolers typically engage indirectly via chapter outreach events (e.g., Physics Field Day, SOCKs kits) rather than applying directly.
costNote: Free to apply; primarily targets college-level SPS chapter members, though some outreach awards touch pre-college programs.
cadence: Annual application cycles for various scholarships/research/outreach awards.
howToStart: Explore students.aip.org/sps (SPS national site, formerly spsnational.org) for awards details; high schoolers should connect through a local university SPS chapter's outreach programming rather than applying for the scholarships directly.
a2cInsight: Mostly a college-level recognition system; relevant to a high schooler's story mainly through participation in SPS-run outreach competitions rather than as a direct award.

name: BPhO Junior Physics Challenge (Year 10 / GCSE level)
prerequisites: Aimed at Year 10 (roughly grade 9/10) students, but ambitious younger students (grade 8/Year 9) can be entered by their school.
costNote: School entry fee (around £20 for up to 999 students) via school registration.
cadence: Annual, delivered online in two 25-minute sessions in spring (school-selected window, e.g., late April-mid May).
howToStart: Have your school register for the BPhO Junior Physics Challenge; tens of thousands of students from UK schools participate annually.
a2cInsight: A gentle, credible entry point into the BPhO family; a gold/distinction here is a nice early marker but carries much less weight than the Senior Challenge or IPhO team selection.

name: Asian Physics Olympiad (APhO)
prerequisites: Selected onto an 8-student national team through a national physics olympiad pipeline (available to countries across Asia, Oceania, and some guest nations).
costNote: Entry is via national team selection; host country and national federations typically cover team costs.
cadence: Annual, typically held ~2 months before IPhO as additional training/selection for national IPhO teams (2026 edition held in Busan, South Korea, May 17-25).
howToStart: Enter your national physics olympiad program; APhO team selection typically overlaps with IPhO team selection pipelines.
a2cInsight: A regional-elite counterpart to IPhO; medals are a strong, internationally recognized STEM credential, especially valued by universities in Asia-Pacific.

name: European Physics Olympiad (EuPhO)
prerequisites: Secondary school student; individual (not strictly national-team-limited) entry, open to talented students across Europe and beyond via national coordinators.
costNote: Individual entry (unlike IPhO's national-team format); participating students' costs are usually covered by home institutions/national coordinators.
cadence: Annual, typically held in June, in-person at a rotating European host institution (2026 edition in Gothenburg, Sweden, June 12-16).
howToStart: Contact your country's national physics olympiad coordinator/federation about EuPhO nomination, or check eupho.ee for participation routes.
a2cInsight: A serious, individually-earned continental physics credential that fills a nice tier between national olympiads and IPhO — good evidence of research-level problem-solving.

name: International Physics Competition (IPhyC)
prerequisites: Secondary school or early university student; open worldwide via individual online registration, no national quota.
costNote: Registration fee applies per participant (e.g., ~12 euros for the Semi-Final Round); kept deliberately accessible/low-cost to be worldwide-inclusive, with financial aid available.
cadence: Annual, three rounds (Qualification, Semi-Final, Final) run online.
howToStart: Register directly at iphyc.org; complete the online qualification round of conceptual/problem-solving physics tasks.
a2cInsight: A newer, globally open online physics contest — legitimate and growing, but less established/selective than IPhO, EuPhO, or BPhO; good for students without access to a national olympiad pipeline.
